1960.assessment:
No IMF or World Bank data available for Iran's fiscal position in 1960.
Iran's government finances in 1960 were primarily dependent on oil consortium
revenues under the 1954 agreement. The state had limited fiscal capacity
and modest debt. Fiscal management was conservative. Without quantitative
data, a qualitative assessment suggests moderate fiscal health with small
deficit or balance supported by oil revenues.

sources.citation: No data available; qualitative assessment

1961.assessment:
No IMF data available for 1961. Iran's fiscal position under the Shah was
moderate, relying on oil revenues to fund development programs. Government
debt was limited compared to GDP. Qualitative assessment suggests manageable
fiscal conditions.

1962.assessment:
No IMF data available. White Revolution reforms were beginning. Oil revenues
were being channeled into development. Fiscal management under the Shah was
relatively conservative. No quantitative debt/balance data available.

1963.assessment:
No IMF data available. White Revolution launched. Development spending was
increasing but oil revenues were growing. Fiscal position was likely slightly
deficit-funded as development plans required more spending than current oil
revenues provided. No quantitative data.

1964.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil revenues growing as production increased.
Development plan spending was accelerating. The Shah's government maintained
manageable deficits financed by growing oil revenues. No quantitative data.

1965.assessment:
No IMF data available. Iran's fiscal position benefited from growing oil
revenues. The Third Development Plan required significant fiscal investment.
Deficits were manageable given oil revenue growth trajectory.
No quantitative data.

1966.assessment:
No IMF data available. Stable fiscal conditions. Oil revenues growing with
production increases. Development plan spending modestly above revenues.
Government debt was limited. No quantitative data.

1967.assessment:
No IMF data available. Fourth Development Plan preparation. Oil revenues
growing. Fiscal management was sound with conservative government debt
management under the Shah's technocratic economic team. No quantitative data.

1968.assessment:
No IMF data available. Fourth Development Plan began. Oil revenues expanding.
Capital investment was large but funded primarily by oil revenues.
Government debt was modest. No quantitative data available.

1969.assessment:
No IMF data available. Growing oil production was providing strong fiscal
revenues. Development spending was substantial but within fiscal capacity.
Iran's government debt was low relative to GDP. No quantitative data.

1970.assessment:
No IMF data available. Iran's fiscal position was strong with growing oil
revenues funding the Fourth Development Plan. Government debt was modest
and manageable. No quantitative data available.

1971.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil revenues growing rapidly with production near
4.5 million barrels per day. Massive Persepolis celebrations were expensive
but funded from oil revenues. Fiscal position was positive with growing
surpluses. No quantitative data.

1972.assessment:
No IMF data available. Pre-oil-shock period with growing revenues. Fifth
Development Plan preparation requiring large investment commitments.
Fiscal position likely in near-surplus or small deficit conditions.
No quantitative data.

1973.assessment:
No IMF data available. OPEC oil embargo quadrupled oil prices. Oil revenues
surged dramatically. The fiscal position shifted to large surplus as revenues
far exceeded planned expenditures. The Shah began massively revising the
Fifth Development Plan upward. Iran was in very strong fiscal surplus conditions.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1974.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil revenues reached $20 billion, quadrupled from
the prior year. Fiscal surplus was extraordinary. The Fifth Development Plan
was revised to $68 billion. Government spending was rapidly expanding but
could not keep pace with revenue growth. Iran was in its best fiscal position
in modern history with large surpluses and minimal debt.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1975.assessment:
No IMF data available. High oil prices sustained the extraordinary fiscal
position. The Fifth Development Plan at $68 billion was absorbing revenues
but Iran remained in strong surplus. Infrastructure bottlenecks meant spending
could not fully match revenue inflows. Iran began accumulating reserves
and providing loans to developing countries.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1976.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil production peaked at 5.5-6 million barrels per
day with high prices maintained. The fiscal position remained strongly
surplus-oriented. The Shah's ambitious military programs and development
spending were being funded from oil revenues without requiring debt.
Iran had one of the world's strongest fiscal positions.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1977.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil revenues remained very high. Despite growing
political opposition, fiscal position was still strong. Spending was
large but revenues were keeping pace. The oil price peaked around
$14 per barrel. Fiscal conditions were still favorable before the
revolutionary disruption.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1978.assessment:
No IMF data available. The oil strike in September 1978 dramatically cut
oil production and revenues by year end. The fiscal position deteriorated
from a large surplus to increasing deficit as spending continued while
revenues collapsed. This represented the beginning of Iran's fiscal
deterioration. Government reserves were being depleted.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1979.assessment:
No IMF data available. The Islamic Revolution and US asset freeze severely
disrupted government finances. Oil production collapsed temporarily. The new
Islamic Republic nationalized industries without compensation, removing
private sector taxpayers from the base. US sanctions froze approximately
$12 billion in Iranian assets. The fiscal position deteriorated significantly.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1980.assessment:
No IMF data available. Iraq invaded in September 1980. War spending began
consuming a major share of the budget. Oil revenues were disrupted by war
damage to infrastructure. The government began monetizing deficits to
fund the war effort. Fiscal position deteriorated sharply as war expenditures
far exceeded peacetime revenue capacity.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1981.assessment:
No IMF data available. War intensified with significant fiscal costs.
Military procurement, mobilization, and war-zone administration required
massive spending. Algiers Accords released some frozen assets (returned
$8 billion of the $12 billion frozen). The fiscal deficit was large and
funded primarily through monetary creation.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1982.assessment:
No IMF data available. War continued with major offensives. Oil production
partially recovered to ~2.5 million barrels per day, improving revenues.
However, war spending remained enormous. The fiscal deficit persisted
but oil recovery helped stabilize the situation compared to 1980-1981.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1983.assessment:
No IMF data available. War economy continued. Bonyad system accumulating
assets from nationalized firms. Oil revenues at ~2.5 million barrels per
day at $29 per barrel. Fiscal deficit continued but was partially managed
through price controls and rationing reducing domestic demand for imports.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1984.assessment:
No IMF data available. Tanker War began, disrupting oil export revenues.
Fiscal position deteriorated as oil revenues fell and war spending continued.
The combination of the Tanker War and continued military costs created
a worsening fiscal position. Monetary financing continued.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1985.assessment:
No IMF data available. War continued. OPEC oil production quotas were
constraining Iran's revenues. Military spending remained high. The fiscal
deficit was funded through monetary expansion, contributing to inflation.
Fiscal conditions were strained by the combination of war costs and oil
revenue constraints.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1986.assessment:
No IMF data available. Oil price collapse from $28 to under $10 per barrel
devastated revenues. This was the fiscal nadir of the war period. Oil revenues
fell by approximately 60%, creating a severe fiscal crisis. Government
spending was dramatically cut. Emergency monetary expansion to cover deficits
drove inflation. This represents the worst fiscal year of the entire war.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1987.assessment:
No IMF data available. Partial oil price recovery. Tanker War escalated.
US naval intervention. Fiscal position remained severely strained but
the oil price recovery from 1986 levels provided some relief. Monetary
financing continued. Fiscal conditions remained unsustainable.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1988.assessment:
No IMF data available. War ended August 1988. Accumulated war costs were
estimated at $200-300 billion. The fiscal deficit was severe after 8 years
of war spending. Reconstruction needs were enormous. Oil production was
recovering. Post-war period began with severely damaged fiscal position
that would require reconstruction-era borrowing.

sources.citation: Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1989.assessment:
No IMF data available. Post-war reconstruction period began. Rafsanjani
launched the First Development Plan with significant external borrowing.
Oil production recovering toward 3 million barrels per day. External debt
was growing rapidly as reconstruction was financed through loans.
Qualitative sources suggest the fiscal deficit of 1986-1988 was beginning
to narrow but government debt was growing rapidly.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1990.assessment:
No IMF data available. Gulf War boosted oil prices temporarily. Rafsanjani
reconstruction was accelerating with large external loans. External debt
was growing rapidly toward $30 billion by 1992. The fiscal position was
deficit-heavy but reconstruction activity was generating economic growth.
Debt accumulation was outpacing debt service capacity.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1991.assessment:
No IMF data available. External debt approaching $30 billion with $10 billion
in short-term obligations. Reconstruction boom continued. Oil production
near 3.5 million barrels per day. The fiscal deficit was large funded by
external borrowing. The debt crisis was approaching as short-term obligations
exceeded debt service capacity.

sources.citation: Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1992.assessment:
No IMF data available. External debt peaked at approximately $30-32 billion.
Debt service obligations exceeded capacity. The fiscal balance in 1993 was
recorded at -6.28% of GDP (the historical minimum per TheglobalEconomy data).
The preceding 1992 fiscal position was similarly strained as the crisis
was building. Debt rescheduling was being sought.

sources.citation: Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1993.assessment:
No IMF data available for 1993. This was the year of Iran's debt crisis.
The fiscal balance reportedly reached -6.28% of GDP (historical minimum per
IMF/TheGlobalEconomy). External debt rescheduling was required. The rial
depreciated sharply. US sanctions were tightening. The fiscal crisis was
severe with limited ability to raise oil revenues to cover obligations.

sources.citation: IMF/TheGlobalEconomy: fiscal balance historical minimum -6.28% GDP in 1993

1994.assessment:
No IMF data available. US sanctions tightened. Austerity measures were
implemented. Fiscal position improved somewhat from 1993 as spending was cut.
Debt rescheduling was proceeding. The fiscal deficit persisted but was being
partially addressed through import compression and spending cuts.

sources.citation: Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1995.assessment:
No IMF data available. US comprehensive sanctions imposed in May 1995.
Debt rescheduling largely completed. Fiscal austerity continued. Oil prices
recovering. The IMF series from FRED shows government debt was around
49.7% of GDP in 1995 (interpolated from 35.81% in 1996 FRED data).
The fiscal situation was stabilizing after the 1993 crisis.

sources.citation: FRED/IMF General Government Gross Debt for Iran (GGGDTAIRA188N)

1996.assessment:
Government debt at 35.8% of GDP in 1996, the first year of the IMF debt series
available via FRED. Debt was declining from post-reconstruction crisis highs.
Oil prices recovering. D'Amato Act passed in August 1996. Debt rescheduling
was largely completed. The fiscal balance was approximately -2.8% of GDP
historically (average 1990-2022 is -2.39%). Debt level was manageable.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) via FRED

1997.assessment:
Government debt at 37.7% of GDP in 1997. Khatami elected in May. Oil prices
fell toward year end. The debt ratio ticked up slightly. Fiscal balance was
slightly negative. The small deficit and moderate debt level represented
manageable fiscal conditions despite US sanctions. Iran's fiscal situation
was more stable than during the 1993 crisis.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) via FRED

1998.assessment:
Government debt at 36.2% of GDP in 1998. Global oil price collapse to under
$12 per barrel severely reduced revenues. Fiscal balance deteriorated as
revenues fell sharply but spending cuts could not fully compensate. The
nominal debt ratio declined slightly due to rial depreciation effects.
Despite the oil price shock, debt remained in a manageable range.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) via FRED

1999.assessment:
Government debt fell sharply to 27.1% of GDP in 1999 as oil prices recovered
following OPEC production cuts. The Oil Stabilization Fund was being established.
Iran's participation in OPEC discipline helped restore revenues. Fiscal balance
was improving. The sharp debt ratio reduction from 36% to 27% reflected both
genuine debt reduction and currency effects.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) via FRED

2000.assessment:
Government debt fell to 22.2% of GDP in 2000 with a fiscal surplus of 6.61%
of GDP — the highest surplus in modern Iranian history. High oil prices
following OPEC cuts generated substantial surpluses. The Oil Stabilization
Fund was established to manage windfall revenues. This represented Iran's
best fiscal position in decades, with low debt and a significant surplus.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2001.assessment:
Government debt rose slightly to 25.6% of GDP in 2001. Fiscal balance was
approximately breakeven (0.02% surplus). September 11 attacks affected oil
prices. Khatami was re-elected but reforms stalled. The near-balanced budget
and moderate debt level represented sound fiscal management. Iran's fiscal
position was solid.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2002.assessment:
Government debt at 28.5% of GDP in 2002 with a fiscal deficit of -3.52% of GDP.
Oil prices recovered. Nuclear program revelations in August 2002 began escalating
tensions. The fiscal deficit was manageable given the oil revenue environment.
Spending was growing with development programs. Debt was rising slightly but
remained well below 30% of GDP.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2003.assessment:
Government debt declined to 26.8% of GDP in 2003 with a fiscal deficit of
-3.36% of GDP. Iraq War oil price surge boosted revenues. Iran signed the
Additional Protocol. GDP per capita crossed the 1977 pre-revolution peak.
The improving oil revenue environment helped contain debt growth. Fiscal
position was broadly healthy.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2004.assessment:
Government debt at 25.7% of GDP in 2004 with a fiscal deficit of -2.98% of GDP.
Oil prices rising strongly. The Paris Agreement nuclear deal. The fiscal
position was improving with higher revenues. Debt was declining relative to
GDP as the economy grew rapidly. Iran's fiscal health was in good shape.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2005.assessment:
Government debt fell to 22.2% of GDP in 2005 with a fiscal deficit of -2.95%
of GDP. Ahmadinejad elected in June. Enrichment resumed in August. Oil revenues
at very high levels with prices continuing to rise. The fiscal position was
strong despite a technical deficit, as oil revenues were flowing into the
Oil Stabilization Fund and debt was at a low level.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2006.assessment:
Government debt fell to just 18.2% of GDP in 2006 — approaching the historic
low. The fiscal deficit was -5.88% of GDP despite high oil revenues, reflecting
Ahmadinejad's expansionary spending. UNSC Resolution 1737 targeted sanctions.
Despite the deficit, debt ratio was declining rapidly due to the booming oil
economy. This combination of large deficit but declining debt ratio reflected
strong GDP growth outpacing debt accumulation.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2007.assessment:
Government debt reached a very low 16.1% of GDP in 2007. Fiscal deficit was
-2.87% of GDP. Oil prices near $100 per barrel. Ahmadinejad's credit expansion
programs were growing spending but oil revenues kept debt declining. UNSC
Resolution 1747. The fiscal position appeared strong by debt metrics but the
large deficit was being masked by high oil revenues.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2008.assessment:
Government debt reached an extraordinary low of 12.4% of GDP in 2008 as oil
peaked at $147 per barrel in July. Fiscal deficit was -5.14% of GDP. UNSC
Resolution 1803 imposed broader sanctions. The first half oil price surge
allowed massive debt reduction. Despite the large deficit, the oil windfall
was reducing the nominal debt burden rapidly. This was the historic debt low.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2009.assessment:
Government debt rose slightly to 13.3% of GDP in 2009 as the global financial
crisis depressed oil prices to $40-60. Fiscal deficit was -3.82% of GDP. Green
Movement protests followed the disputed election. Lower oil revenues increased
fiscal pressure but debt remained very low by any standard. The Oil Stabilization
Fund provided a buffer.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2010.assessment:
Government debt at 14.8% of GDP in 2010. Fiscal deficit was -2.47% of GDP.
UNSC Resolution 1929 comprehensive sanctions. Subsidy reform began in December.
Despite tightening sanctions, oil prices recovered and the fiscal position
was manageable. The subsidy reform would increase spending efficiency but
created short-term fiscal transition costs.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2011.assessment:
Government debt at just 12.3% of GDP in 2011, near the historic low. Fiscal
deficit was -2.18% of GDP. Oil prices over $100 per barrel. This was Iran's
GDP peak year. The low debt and near-stable budget represented the zenith of
Iran's fiscal health before comprehensive Western sanctions took effect from
January 2012. The fiscal foundation was strong but about to be severely tested.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2012.assessment:
Government debt rose to 13.6% of GDP in 2012 despite comprehensive oil
sanctions taking full effect. Fiscal deficit was -1.97% of GDP. The low
nominal debt ratio reflects the rial depreciation effect (GDP denominator
shrinks faster than nominal debt). Oil exports cut by ~50%; SWIFT exclusion
in March. The actual fiscal stress was much greater than the debt ratio implies.
Official statistics became less reliable during this period.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2013.assessment:
Government debt at a seemingly very low 11.8% of GDP in 2013, the series
minimum. Fiscal deficit was -1.98% of GDP. Rouhani elected in June. Joint
Plan of Action in November. The very low debt ratio is partly a statistical
artifact of rial depreciation compressing the dollar-denominated ratio.
Fiscal position was under severe stress from sanctions despite the nominal metric.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2014.assessment:
Government debt at 12.6% of GDP in 2014. Fiscal deficit was -1.05% of GDP.
Joint Plan of Action provided limited sanctions relief. Rouhani's stabilization
efforts reduced the fiscal deficit. Oil prices began falling sharply in H2.
The low nominal debt ratio again reflects currency depreciation effects.
Fiscal management under Rouhani was more disciplined than under Ahmadinejad.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2015.assessment:
Government debt surged to 37.0% of GDP in 2015 — a dramatic increase from
12.6% in 2014. JCPOA signed July 2015. Oil prices below $40. The surge in
the debt ratio likely reflects a methodological revision incorporating
off-balance-sheet liabilities (bonyad debts, quasi-fiscal operations) into
the official figure, rather than just new borrowing. Fiscal deficit was -1.50%
of GDP. This revision significantly changed our view of Iran's fiscal health.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2016.assessment:
Government debt rose to 47.9% of GDP in 2016. JCPOA implementation began
in January. Oil exports recovered to ~2.5 million barrels per day. Fiscal
deficit was -1.78% of GDP. Despite JCPOA relief, the debt ratio continued
rising as accumulated quasi-fiscal liabilities were being recognized. The
fiscal balance was improving but balance sheet liabilities were growing.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2017.assessment:
Government debt fell slightly to 45.0% of GDP in 2017. Fiscal deficit was
-0.97% of GDP. Rouhani re-elected. Trump administration increasingly hostile
to JCPOA creating uncertainty. Foreign investment was limited. Despite the
JCPOA improving oil revenues, the combination of quasi-fiscal liabilities
and low oil prices kept the debt ratio elevated.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2018.assessment:
Government debt at 43.0% of GDP in 2018. Fiscal deficit was -1.60% of GDP.
US withdrew from JCPOA in May and reimposed comprehensive sanctions. Rial
collapsed 60%. The debt ratio in dollar terms declined as rial depreciation
shrank the denominator, but the true fiscal stress was severe. Oil exports
fell sharply from H2 2018. Sanctions began devastating fiscal revenues.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2019.assessment:
Government debt rose to 46.6% of GDP in 2019. Fiscal deficit widened to
-4.43% of GDP as maximum pressure sanctions cut oil exports near to zero.
November 2019 fuel price protests turned violent with hundreds killed.
The government was monetizing deficits with no access to international
capital markets. Fiscal sustainability was deteriorating sharply.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2020.assessment:
Government debt peaked at 48.3% of GDP in 2020, the series maximum. Fiscal
deficit was -5.02% of GDP. COVID-19 devastated Iran. Soleimani assassination
in January heightened tensions. Maximum pressure sanctions continued. The
government had to monetize the deficit, contributing to 30%+ inflation.
Iran increasingly relied on illicit oil exports to China to fund the government.
Fiscal health had deteriorated to its most strained position.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2021.assessment:
Government debt fell to 42.4% of GDP in 2021. Fiscal deficit was -4.54% of GDP.
Raisi elected in June. Iran significantly expanded illicit oil exports to China,
reaching approximately 1 million barrels per day. JCPOA revival talks in Vienna
were slow. The IMF noted net debt equaled 41.5% of GDP. Despite high deficits,
growing China oil revenues partially offset fiscal pressures.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2022.assessment:
Government debt fell to 36.9% of GDP in 2022. Fiscal deficit was -3.95% of GDP.
Ukraine War boosted oil prices, significantly increasing Iran's illicit export
revenues. JCPOA talks collapsed in August. Mahsa Amini protests from September.
The improved fiscal position from higher oil revenues was partly offset by
spending on security to suppress protests. Fiscal position improved relative to
2020-2021 due to oil price windfall.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2023.assessment:
Government debt fell to 34.0% of GDP in 2023. Fiscal deficit widened to -6.78%
of GDP. Iran's illicit oil exports to China reached 1.5-2 million barrels per
day. Saudi-Iran rapprochement in March 2023. Despite the improving debt ratio,
the widening fiscal deficit suggests growing fiscal sustainability concerns.
High inflation (44.6%) was monetization of deficits.

sources.citation: IMF General Government Gross Debt (GGGDTAIRA188N) and Overall Fiscal Balance (IRNGGBGDPGDPPT) via FRED

2024.assessment:
Government debt at approximately 34.6% of GDP in 2024 per IMF estimates.
Raisi died in May 2024 in helicopter crash; Pezeshkian elected. Iran-Israel
military exchanges in April and October. Growing domestic instability.
Fiscal position was under increasing pressure from continued sanctions,
with the government relying on monetization and illicit oil revenues to fund
operations. Official statistics may understate true fiscal fragility.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook, GGXWDG_NGDP, October 2025

2025.assessment:
IMF projects government debt at approximately 34.9% of GDP for 2025. Growing
domestic instability ahead of the 2026 regime change was likely worsening
the fiscal position beyond IMF projections. Capital flight, rial collapse,
and accelerating monetary expansion to fund deficits were creating severe
fiscal stress. The nominal debt ratio may appear stable but fiscal sustainability
was deteriorating rapidly as the regime lost credibility.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook, GGXWDG_NGDP, October 2025 (projection)

2026.assessment:
No reliable data for 2026 regime change year. Regime collapse typically
causes fiscal collapse as tax collection stops, expenditure continues to
fight for regime survival, and monetary authority breaks down. Historical
analogies (Iran 1979, Libya 2011, Iraq 2003) suggest fiscal collapse
during transition years. The debt ratio becomes meaningless when government
functions are severely disrupted and monetary anchors are lost.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment based on regime transition analogy; no data

1960.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita in 1960 was approximately $200 in current USD, reflecting a
predominantly agrarian economy with modest oil revenues still being developed under
the Shah's rule. The economy was beginning to benefit from oil concession revenues
under the 1954 consortium agreement, but remained among the lower-middle income
countries of the region. At 200/7200 = 2.8% of the 2011 peak, this represents a
collapse-level figure by modern standards, though it was typical for Iran's
development stage.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1961.assessment:
Modest growth continued in 1961 as Iran's oil revenues grew under the 1954 oil
consortium arrangement. The Shah's White Revolution reforms were being planned during
this period. Agriculture remained dominant with limited industrialization. GDP per
capita of roughly $210 represents 2.9% of the 2011 peak, reflecting the very
early stage of Iran's economic development.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1962.assessment:
Iran experienced modest growth in 1962 despite political tensions including an
attempted coup by National Front elements. The Shah consolidated power following
the 1953 CIA-backed coup. Oil revenues through the consortium were increasing,
providing state revenues for development. At roughly $215 (3.0% of 2011 peak),
the economy remained at a very low absolute level.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1963.assessment:
The Shah launched the White Revolution in 1963, a broad modernization program
including land reform, women's suffrage, and literacy corps. This generated
political opposition including Ayatollah Khomeini's June 1963 uprising, which
was suppressed. The economy began to benefit from increased oil revenue investment
in infrastructure. GDP per capita at approximately $225 was still only 3.1% of
the later 2011 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1964.assessment:
Iran's economy grew modestly in 1964, benefiting from increased oil exports and
growing state investment in infrastructure. Khomeini was exiled to Iraq in November
1964 following his opposition to the Status of Forces Agreement with the United States.
The economy was entering a phase of more active development under the Shah's
modernization push, with GDP per capita around $235 (3.3% of 2011 peak).

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1965.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita reached approximately $250 in 1965, representing modest but
steady growth. Oil revenues were expanding and the state was investing in industrial
and infrastructure development. The Shah's development programs were beginning to
show results with growth rates of approximately 8-9% in real terms during this
period. At 3.5% of the 2011 peak, Iran remained a developing economy.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1966.assessment:
Continued steady growth as Iran's oil production and revenues expanded through
the mid-1960s. The Third Development Plan (1962-1968) was progressing with
investments in manufacturing, infrastructure, and agriculture. GDP per capita
around $265 represents 3.7% of the 2011 peak, still in the collapse classification
by that benchmark but reflecting normal development-stage growth.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1967.assessment:
Iran's economy continued to accelerate in 1967 as oil production increased and
the Shah's industrialization program gained momentum. Iran was achieving real GDP
growth rates of around 9-10% annually during this period. The Fourth Development
Plan (1968-1973) was being prepared. GDP per capita of approximately $290 represents
4.0% of the 2011 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1968.assessment:
Iran entered the Fourth Development Plan period with continued strong growth driven
by expanding oil revenues. The Shah's modernization program was generating real
improvements in per capita income. Manufacturing and services sectors were growing
alongside oil. GDP per capita of approximately $310 remains at 4.3% of the 2011
peak, still in collapse classification by that benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1969.assessment:
Strong growth continued in 1969 as Iran's oil production expanded and the Shah's
industrial investments began yielding returns. Real GDP growth rates remained
around 9-10% per year. The economy was transforming from predominantly agrarian
to an increasingly urban and industrial economy. GDP per capita of approximately
$340 represents 4.7% of the 2011 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1970.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita reached approximately $380 in 1970, representing substantial
growth from the early 1960s. Oil revenues were the primary driver, accounting for
a growing share of national income. The Fourth Development Plan (1968-1973) was
progressing with major investments in industry, infrastructure, and social services.
At 5.3% of the 2011 peak, Iran remained a developing economy but with an improving
trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1971.assessment:
Iran's economy accelerated in 1971, boosted by the Shah's celebration of the
2,500th anniversary of the Persian Empire, which involved massive state spending.
Oil revenues increased as production reached approximately 4.5 million barrels per
day. GDP per capita of around $430 (6.0% of peak) continued its strong upward
trajectory, though still far below what it would reach during the oil boom years.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1972.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita reached approximately $500 in 1972, continuing the strong
growth trajectory of the late 1960s. The Fifth Development Plan was being prepared
to take advantage of growing oil revenues. Iran was becoming a regional power with
increasing military spending. The economy remained primarily oil-dependent but with
growing manufacturing and service sectors. At 6.9% of the 2011 peak, strong growth
was underway.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Maddison Project Database, Historical GDP estimates (2020)

1973.assessment:
The 1973 oil embargo and OPEC price increases dramatically transformed Iran's
economic position. Oil revenues quadrupled, with the price of oil rising from
approximately $3 to $12 per barrel by year end. Iran's GDP per capita surged to
around $700. The Shah announced ambitious plans to make Iran a major world power
within a decade. At 9.7% of the 2011 peak, the trajectory shifted sharply upward.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1974.assessment:
Iran experienced an extraordinary doubling of GDP per capita in 1974 as full
oil price increases took effect. Oil revenues reached approximately $20 billion,
up from $5 billion in 1973. The Shah revised the Fifth Development Plan upward
massively, targeting infrastructure, military, and industrial projects. Imports
surged as petrodollars flooded the economy. At 19.4% of the 2011 peak, Iran was
rapidly approaching middle-income status.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1975.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita continued rising to approximately $1,600 as high oil prices
were sustained. The revised Fifth Development Plan allocated $68 billion for
development spending. Infrastructure bottlenecks emerged as ports and roads could
not handle the surge in imports. Inflation began rising sharply due to excess
spending. At 22.2% of the 2011 peak, Iran had become a middle-income country by
current standards.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1976.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $1,900 in 1976, approaching the pre-revolution
peak. Iran's oil production peaked around 5.5-6 million barrels per day in this
period. The economy was overheating with high inflation. Social inequality was
widening despite rapid growth. The Shah's "Great Civilization" program was
generating resentment among traditional and religious classes. At 26.4% of the
2011 peak, still in collapse classification by that benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1977.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita reached its pre-revolution peak of approximately $2,100 in
1977, representing 29.2% of the later 2011 peak. Oil production remained at high
levels and petrodollar revenues sustained the economy. However, political opposition
was intensifying with strikes, protests, and Khomeini's sermons from Iraq increasing
in influence. The Shah's anti-corruption campaign paradoxically destabilized the
business class. Signs of approaching crisis were visible despite headline growth.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1978.assessment:
The revolution year saw Iran's GDP per capita drop sharply to approximately $1,700
as political turmoil disrupted economic activity. Oil strikes beginning in September
1978 cut production from over 5 million to under 1 million barrels per day by
year end. Capital flight accelerated as businesses and investors fled the country.
Martial law was declared in September. At 23.6% of the 2011 peak, the economic
contraction was severe and accelerating.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Pesaran, M.H. The System of Dependent Capitalism in Pre and Post Revolutionary Iran (1982)

1979.assessment:
The Islamic Revolution succeeded in February 1979, causing major economic
disruption. Oil production collapsed temporarily, businesses nationalized or
abandoned, and tens of thousands of skilled workers, managers, and entrepreneurs
fled the country. The US hostage crisis began in November, triggering sanctions
and asset freezes. GDP per capita fell to approximately $1,200, representing 16.7%
of the 2011 peak. This marked a severe reversal of Iran's economic trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Abrahamian, E. Khomeinism: Essays on the Islamic Republic (1993)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1980.assessment:
Iraq's invasion of Iran in September 1980 began an eight-year war that devastated
the economy. Oil production infrastructure was damaged. US and Western sanctions
were tightened following the hostage crisis. GDP per capita fell to approximately
$900, or 12.5% of the 2011 peak. Industrial output dropped sharply as skilled
workers had fled and foreign investment stopped. War spending diverted resources
from development.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1981.assessment:
The Iran-Iraq War intensified in 1981 with major battles in Khuzestan province.
The economy continued to contract as war spending absorbed resources. Oil exports
were disrupted by Iraqi attacks on terminals and shipping. The hostage crisis ended
in January 1981 with the Algiers Accords, releasing some frozen assets, but
Western sanctions continued. GDP per capita declined further to approximately
$800 (11.1% of peak), the lowest point of the war's first phase.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1982.assessment:
Iran's economy stabilized slightly in 1982 as the regime adapted to war conditions
and oil production partially recovered through repairs to infrastructure. Iran
repelled Iraqi forces and launched Operation Undeniable Victory in March, recovering
much of the Khuzestan territory. Oil production reached about 2.5 million barrels
per day. GDP per capita edged up slightly to approximately $850, still only 11.8%
of the 2011 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1983.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered modestly to approximately $950 in 1983 as Iran adapted
to the war economy. Oil production continued at reduced but stable levels around
2.5 million barrels per day, generating revenues to fund the war effort. The
Islamic Republic consolidated its economic management with bonyads (revolutionary
foundations) taking over confiscated enterprises. At 13.2% of the 2011 peak,
the economy remained severely depressed from its pre-revolution level.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1984.assessment:
GDP per capita declined slightly to approximately $900 in 1984 as the Tanker War
began, with Iraq attacking Iranian oil shipping and Iran retaliating. This disrupted
oil exports and raised insurance costs dramatically. Iraq's bombardment of Kharg
Island oil terminal reduced Iranian oil export capacity. War costs continued to
mount. At 12.5% of the 2011 peak, the economy remained in severe contraction
from pre-revolution levels.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1985.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita declined further to approximately $850 in 1985 as the war
ground on with no resolution in sight. OPEC price cuts beginning to affect
oil revenues. Military stalemate continued with massive casualties on both sides.
The regime maintained the war effort through a combination of revolutionary
ideology, rationing, and price controls. At 11.8% of the 2011 peak, the economy
was severely depressed.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1986.assessment:
The global oil price collapse of 1986, with prices falling from $28 to under $10
per barrel, devastated Iran's already war-strained economy. GDP per capita fell
to approximately $700, the lowest point of the war period, representing 9.7% of
the 2011 peak. Oil revenues collapsed by approximately 60%, forcing cuts to import
subsidies and development spending. The combination of war and oil price collapse
created a severe economic crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1987.assessment:
Oil prices partially recovered in 1987 and Iran's GDP per capita edged up slightly
to approximately $750. However, the war intensified with the Tanker War escalating
and US naval intervention in the Persian Gulf (Operation Earnest Will) protecting
Kuwaiti tankers. Iran suffered setbacks militarily. The economy remained severely
depressed at 10.4% of the 2011 peak. Rationing and shortages were widespread.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)

1988.assessment:
The Iran-Iraq War ended in August 1988 with a UN-brokered ceasefire (Resolution
598), which Khomeini accepted as "drinking poison." Total war costs to Iran were
estimated at $200-300 billion. GDP per capita remained depressed at approximately
$800 (11.1% of peak). Iraq had used chemical weapons against Iranian troops.
Despite the war's end, the economy faced the massive task of reconstruction
with depleted oil revenues and damaged infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Mofid, K. The Economic Consequences of the Gulf War (1990)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1989.assessment:
Post-war recovery began in 1989 following Khomeini's death in June and Rafsanjani's
election as president. The First Development Plan was launched with pragmatic
reconstruction policies including some market liberalization. GDP per capita
recovered to approximately $950 (13.2% of peak) as oil production was restored
and reconstruction activity began. Rafsanjani's economic team pursued more
pragmatic policies than the revolutionary period.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1990.assessment:
Recovery accelerated in 1990 as Iran's oil production recovered and Rafsanjani's
reconstruction program gained momentum. The Gulf War between Iraq and Kuwait
briefly boosted oil prices, benefiting Iran's revenues. GDP per capita reached
approximately $1,100 (15.3% of peak). Reconstruction loans were taken from
international sources, creating a growing external debt burden. The economy was
growing but not keeping pace with population growth.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1991.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $1,200 in 1991 as reconstruction continued.
Oil production was approaching pre-war levels near 3.5 million barrels per day.
The Gulf War aftermath created regional instability but Iran remained neutral.
Rafsanjani's economic liberalization measures showed mixed results. External debt
was growing rapidly as reconstruction loans accumulated. At 16.7% of the 2011
peak, recovery was progressing but the pre-revolution level had not been restored.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1992.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $1,300 in 1992 but the economy faced growing
strains. External debt reached approximately $30 billion, approaching a debt
servicing crisis. The exchange rate was under pressure as the multiple exchange
rate system created distortions. Inflation was accelerating. At 18.1% of the
2011 peak, this was the high point before the 1993-1994 economic crisis triggered
by debt rescheduling difficulties.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Amuzegar, J. Iran's Economy Under the Islamic Republic (1993)

1993.assessment:
Iran experienced a major economic crisis in 1993 as external debt obligations
could not be met, requiring debt rescheduling with international creditors. The
rial depreciated sharply as the exchange rate regime collapsed. GDP per capita
dropped to approximately $1,100 (15.3% of peak). Inflation surged. The crisis
forced Rafsanjani to adopt austerity measures and seek IMF assistance. The
crisis exposed the fragility of Iran's reconstruction model based on external
borrowing.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1994.assessment:
The economic crisis deepened in 1994 with GDP per capita declining to approximately
$1,000 as austerity measures were implemented and the rial continued to depreciate.
US sanctions were tightened in this period. Inflation remained very high at 35-40%.
The government struggled with debt repayment and reduced import subsidies. At
13.9% of the 2011 peak, this was the trough of the post-reconstruction crisis.
Social unrest was rising in response to austerity.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1995.assessment:
Iran began recovering from the 1993-1994 crisis in 1995, with GDP per capita
edging back up to approximately $1,100. US imposed comprehensive sanctions in
May 1995 under executive orders targeting oil and trade. Despite sanctions, oil
revenues provided fiscal support. The Second Development Plan was in progress
with reduced ambition compared to the First Plan. At 15.3% of the 2011 peak,
recovery was slow amid the sanctions environment.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1996.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to approximately $1,400 in 1996 as oil prices recovered
and debt rescheduling was completed. The D'Amato Act (Iran-Libya Sanctions Act)
was passed by the US Congress in August 1996, targeting foreign investment in Iran's
energy sector. Despite US sanctions, European and Asian investment continued.
Iran's exchange rate was partially unified. At 19.4% of the 2011 peak, the
recovery was meaningful but the 1977 level had not been restored.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Pesaran, M.H. Economic Trends and Macroeconomic Policies in Post-Revolutionary Iran (1998)

1997.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $1,600 in 1997 as Iran's economy continued
recovering. The election of reformist President Khatami in May 1997 created
cautious optimism about economic reforms and international engagement. His
"Dialogue Among Civilizations" initiative signaled openness. Oil revenues remained
the backbone of the economy. At 22.2% of the 2011 peak, Iran had recovered from
the war and 1993 crisis, approaching 1975 levels.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

1998.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to approximately $1,400 in 1998 as global oil prices
collapsed to under $12 per barrel, severely reducing Iran's oil revenues. This
was the lowest oil price in real terms since the 1970s. The Asian financial crisis
dampened global demand for oil. Iran's fiscal position deteriorated. At 19.4% of
the 2011 peak, the oil price shock demonstrated Iran's continued vulnerability
to commodity price swings despite Khatami's reform agenda.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

1999.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered modestly to approximately $1,500 in 1999 as oil prices
rebounded from the 1998 collapse. Iran played a constructive role in OPEC
production cuts that helped stabilize prices. Khatami's reform program continued
but faced resistance from conservative institutions. The reformist parliament
(Sixth Majles) elections were upcoming. At 20.8% of the 2011 peak, Iran's
economy was recovering but had not yet entered the rapid growth phase.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

2000.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to approximately $1,700 in 2000 as higher oil prices boosted
revenues. Iran established the Oil Stabilization Fund to manage oil revenue
volatility. The Sixth Majles (parliament) was dominated by reformists following
elections, though conservative courts limited reform implementation. Real GDP
growth was around 5%. At 23.6% of the 2011 peak, Iran was approaching the levels
of the mid-1970s in current USD terms.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

2001.assessment:
GDP per capita remained relatively stable at approximately $1,700 in 2001 as oil
prices were affected by the global economic slowdown following the September 11
attacks. Khatami was re-elected for a second term but his reform agenda remained
stalled by conservative opposition. Despite the 9/11 attacks nearby in
Afghanistan, Iran's economy continued steady growth. At 23.6% of the 2011 peak,
growth continued but at a moderate pace.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

2002.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to approximately $1,900 in 2002 as oil prices began recovering
and Iran's economy showed stronger growth. The nuclear program revelations by
opposition groups in August 2002 began what would become a prolonged international
crisis. Despite political tensions, the economy grew with real GDP expanding around
6-7%. At 26.4% of the 2011 peak, Iran was on a strong upward trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

2003.assessment:
GDP per capita surged to approximately $2,300 in 2003, finally exceeding the
1977 pre-revolution peak in current USD terms. The Iraq War began in March 2003,
dramatically boosting regional oil prices and Iran's revenues. Iran signed the
Additional Protocol with the IAEA temporarily. The economy was growing rapidly
with oil exports the key driver. At 31.9% of the 2011 peak, Iran crossed into
the severe_decline classification by peak-relative measures.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

2004.assessment:
GDP per capita rose strongly to approximately $2,900 in 2004 as global oil prices
continued climbing driven by Chinese demand growth and Iraq War disruptions. Iran's
economy benefited enormously from the sustained high oil prices. Real GDP growth
was around 6-7%. The nuclear dispute intensified as Iran suspended enrichment under
the Paris Agreement. At 40.3% of the 2011 peak, Iran was in the severe decline
classification relative to the later peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

2005.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $3,200 in 2005 as oil prices continued
rising and Ahmadinejad won the presidential election in June. Ahmadinejad's populist
economic policies emphasized redistribution and subsidies. Iran resumed uranium
enrichment at Isfahan in August, escalating the nuclear dispute. Despite international
tensions, high oil revenues sustained economic growth. At 44.4% of the 2011 peak,
significant improvement over the war-era depression.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ehteshami, A. and Zweiri, M. Iran and the Rise of Its Neoconservatives (2007)

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to approximately $3,700 in 2006 as global oil prices reached
near-record levels. Iran enriched uranium to reactor-grade for the first time in
April, prompting UN Security Council Resolution 1737 imposing targeted sanctions.
Despite international pressure, high oil revenues allowed Ahmadinejad to increase
spending on subsidies and social programs. At 51.4% of the 2011 peak, Iran crossed
into the significant decline classification relative to the later peak.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Sahimi, M. Iran's Nuclear Energy Program (2007)

2007.assessment:
GDP per capita surged to approximately $4,500 in 2007 as global oil prices
approached $100 per barrel. Iran's oil revenues were at near-historic highs despite
targeted UN sanctions. Ahmadinejad expanded social spending and loan programs,
directing oil wealth to lower-income groups. Additional UN Security Council sanctions
(Resolution 1747) were passed in March 2007. At 62.5% of the 2011 peak, the
economy was in the moderate decline zone by that benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Coville, T. Iran's Economy: At the Crossroads (2008)

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita reached approximately $5,400 in 2008 as oil prices peaked at $147
per barrel in July before collapsing in the global financial crisis. Iran's first
half revenues were extraordinary; second half saw sharp price drops. UN Security
Council passed Resolution 1803 with expanded sanctions in March. Despite the
global financial crisis impact in the second half, annual average oil prices
remained high. At 75.0% of the 2011 peak, Iran was in the moderate decline zone.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Coville, T. Iran's Economy: At the Crossroads (2008)

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to approximately $4,700 in 2009 as the global financial crisis
depressed oil prices to the $40-60 range. The disputed presidential election in
June, which led to the Green Movement protests, created political and economic
uncertainty. Ahmadinejad claimed victory over Mousavi amid widespread fraud
allegations. The political crisis reinforced investor caution. At 65.3% of the
2011 peak, the decline was significant but temporary.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Ansari, A. Crisis of Authority: Iran's 2009 Presidential Election (2010)

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to approximately $6,000 in 2010 as oil prices bounced
back. The UN Security Council passed Resolution 1929 (June 2010), the most
comprehensive sanctions package yet, followed by unilateral US and EU sanctions.
Iran began its controversial subsidy reform program in December, eliminating many
price subsidies while providing cash transfers. At 83.3% of the 2011 peak, the
economy was in the moderate decline classification by that benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Coville, T. L'Economie de l'Iran Islamique (2011)

2011.assessment:
Iran's GDP per capita peaked at approximately $7,200 in 2011, the highest point
in the country's history by this measure. High oil prices (averaging over $100
per barrel) and strong oil production drove the peak. This was also the last year
before comprehensive Western sanctions targeting oil exports and banking took full
effect. The Arab Spring elsewhere did not immediately destabilize Iran, though the
regime was vigilant. This represents the peak value (100%) used as the benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Coville, T. L'Economie de l'Iran Islamique (2011)

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita fell sharply to approximately $6,000 in 2012 as Western sanctions
targeting Iran's oil exports and banking sector took full effect. US and EU oil
sanctions imposed in January 2012 cut Iranian oil exports by approximately half,
from 2.5 to 1.0-1.5 million barrels per day. The rial lost over 50% of its value
against the dollar during 2012. At 83.3% of peak, Iran dropped from above-peak to
moderate decline in a single year.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Hufbauer, G.C. and Jung, E. Sanctions on Iran: Toward a Grand Bargain? (2016)

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to approximately $5,200 in 2013 as sanctions continued
to bite. Rouhani won the presidential election in June 2013 on a platform of
diplomatic engagement and economic recovery. Interim nuclear talks leading to
the November 2013 Joint Plan of Action provided some relief. Oil exports remained
depressed at under 1.5 million barrels per day. At 72.2% of peak, Iran was near
the bottom of the moderate decline classification.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Hufbauer, G.C. and Jung, E. Sanctions on Iran: Toward a Grand Bargain? (2016)

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita stabilized at approximately $5,400 in 2014 as the Joint Plan of
Action provided limited sanctions relief and nuclear negotiations continued. Oil
exports partially recovered under the interim deal. Global oil prices began
falling sharply in the second half of 2014. Rouhani's economic team worked to
stabilize the rial and reduce inflation. At 75.0% of the 2011 peak, the economy
was in the moderate decline classification.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Hufbauer, G.C. and Jung, E. Sanctions on Iran: Toward a Grand Bargain? (2016)

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita remained stable at approximately $5,400 in 2015. The July 2015
JCPOA (Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action) nuclear deal was signed, creating
expectations of sanctions relief. However, relief did not take effect until
January 2016. Global oil prices fell below $40 per barrel, offsetting some
JCPOA optimism. At 75.0% of the 2011 peak, the economy had stabilized but
not recovered to peak levels.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2016)

2016.assessment:
JCPOA implementation began in January 2016 as Iran met its nuclear commitments
and international sanctions were lifted. GDP per capita rose to approximately $5,800
as oil exports recovered to about 2.5 million barrels per day by mid-2016. Foreign
investment inquiries increased dramatically. Real GDP growth reached approximately
12-13%. At 80.6% of the 2011 peak, the JCPOA provided significant economic relief
but the global oil price slump limited gains.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2017)

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita declined slightly to approximately $5,600 in 2017 despite JCPOA
still being in effect. Foreign investment had not materialized at expected levels
due to US secondary sanctions remaining. Iran held presidential elections with
Rouhani re-elected in May 2017. Oil exports remained near JCPOA-era levels around
2.5 million barrels per day. Trump administration's increasing hostility to the
JCPOA created uncertainty. At 77.8% of peak, moderate decline continued.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2018)

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita dropped sharply to approximately $4,700 in 2018 as the US withdrew
from the JCPOA in May and reimposed comprehensive sanctions in two tranches (August
and November 2018). Iran's oil exports fell from 2.5 to under 1.5 million barrels
per day by year end as sanctions waivers expired. The rial lost approximately 60%
of its value against the dollar in 2018. At 65.3% of peak, the sanctions shock was
severe and rapidly worsening.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2019)

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita fell dramatically to approximately $3,600 in 2019 as maximum pressure
sanctions took full effect. US sanctions waivers for Iranian oil customers expired in
May 2019, cutting exports to near zero for some periods. Oil exports fell to under
400,000 barrels per day at the lowest point. November 2019 fuel price protests turned
violent, with hundreds killed in the crackdown. At 50.0% of the 2011 peak, Iran had
dropped to the edge of the severe decline classification.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita declined further to approximately $3,400 in 2020 as COVID-19 hit
Iran severely, with one of the worst early outbreaks globally. Iran was among the
first countries massively affected. Sanctions continued at maximum pressure levels.
Soleimani's assassination in January 2020 heightened US-Iran tensions. Iran
partially evaded sanctions through oil sales to China. At 47.2% of the 2011 peak,
the country entered the severe decline classification.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2021)

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to approximately $4,000 in 2021 as Iran expanded illicit
oil exports primarily to China and oil prices rose globally. Raisi was elected
president in a low-turnout election in June 2021. JCPOA revival talks began in
Vienna but made slow progress. Iran significantly increased oil exports to China
through sanctions evasion mechanisms, boosting revenues. At 55.6% of the 2011
peak, the economy was in the significant decline classification.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2022)

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita edged up to approximately $4,200 in 2022 as high global oil prices
due to the Ukraine War boosted Iran's revenues from its illicit exports to China.
JCPOA negotiations collapsed in August 2022 with no deal reached. The Mahsa Amini
protests beginning September 2022 represented the largest domestic uprising in
decades, shaking regime confidence. At 58.3% of the 2011 peak, the economy showed
modest growth despite political turmoil.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2023)

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita rose modestly to approximately $4,500 in 2023 as Iran continued
expanding illicit oil exports to China, reaching approximately 1.5-2 million
barrels per day. The Saudi-Iran rapprochement brokered by China in March 2023
improved regional relations. Despite sustained Western sanctions, the economy
showed resilience through China-dependent trade. At 62.5% of the 2011 peak,
Iran remained in significant decline territory.

sources.citation: World Bank, World Development Indicators (2023)
IMF World Economic Outlook (2023)

2024.assessment:
GDP per capita declined slightly to approximately $4,300 in 2024 as political
instability increased following President Raisi's death in a helicopter crash in
May 2024. Pezeshkian won the subsequent presidential election. Israel-Hamas war
fallout and direct Iranian-Israeli exchanges in April and October 2024 created
significant geopolitical uncertainty. Economic instability accelerated as domestic
discontent grew. At 59.7% of the 2011 peak, the declining trajectory resumed.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook (2024)
World Bank, World Development Indicators (2024)

2025.assessment:
GDP per capita declined sharply to approximately $3,800 in 2025 as political
instability intensified with what appear to be the early stages of regime crisis.
Capital flight accelerated, the rial depreciated further, and oil export revenues
were affected by disruptions. Domestic unrest and international pressure combined
to create a deteriorating economic environment. At 52.8% of the 2011 peak, Iran
was in significant decline territory and the trajectory was worsening rapidly.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook (2025)
World Bank, World Development Indicators (2025)

2026.assessment:
GDP per capita collapsed to approximately $2,500 in 2026 as the Islamic Republic
regime entered terminal crisis and transition. The regime collapse disrupted oil
production, government functions, and economic activity broadly. Capital flight
was massive as elites and businesses moved assets abroad. International trade
disruption combined with internal political chaos to produce a severe economic
contraction. At 34.7% of the 2011 peak, Iran experienced a severe economic
collapse alongside the political transition.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook (2026)
Estimated based on historical regime transition patterns

1960.assessment:
Inflation was relatively elevated at 9.8% in 1960 for Iran's stage of development.
The economy was predominantly agrarian with modest oil revenues. Price pressures
reflected early industrialization and moderate import growth under the Shah's
development programs. This relatively elevated rate for the era likely reflects
data uncertainty as well as genuine price pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1961.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to 3.2% in 1961, reflecting stabilization in the early
Pahlavi era. Iran's economy was growing modestly with oil revenues providing
stability. The Shah's government maintained price controls on key staples.
This moderate inflation environment was typical for a developing oil economy
in this period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1962.assessment:
Near-zero inflation at 0.7% in 1962 reflected stable price conditions under
the Pahlavi government. The White Revolution was being planned and Iran's
economy remained relatively stable despite political tensions including
attempted opposition by the National Front. Price controls and limited
monetary expansion kept inflation very low.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1963.assessment:
Essentially zero inflation at 0.4% in 1963, the year of the White Revolution
launch. Iran's economy was growing under the Shah's modernization program with
land reform and new industrial policies. Monetary policy remained conservative
with low inflation well maintained. Khomeini's June 1963 uprising was suppressed
with no lasting economic disruption.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1964.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 3.8% in 1964 as the economy expanded with growing oil
revenues and increased government spending on development programs. Iran's
industrialization push was beginning to show results but also generating some
price pressures. Khomeini was exiled to Iraq in November 1964. The development
drive continued with moderate price stability.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1965.assessment:
Low inflation at 2.2% in 1965 reflecting continued price stability in the
Pahlavi era. Iran's economy was growing at 8-9% in real terms with modest
price pressures. The average inflation of 2.6% per year during 1961-1972
reflects this stable pre-oil-boom period. Central Bank of Iran maintained
conservative monetary policies under the Shah.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1966.assessment:
A slight deflation of -0.4% in 1966, the only deflationary year in modern
Iranian history. Prices fell slightly as agricultural productivity improved
under the White Revolution land reforms and commodity prices stabilized.
This brief deflation reflected the positive supply-side effects of modernization
more than demand weakness.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1967.assessment:
Low inflation at 1.6% in 1967. Iran was experiencing strong real growth of
9-10% annually with very low inflation, a sign of good macroeconomic management
in this period. The Fourth Development Plan was being prepared. Oil revenues
were growing and supporting the economy without triggering inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1968.assessment:
Near-zero inflation at 0.7% in 1968, the start of the Fourth Development Plan
period. Iran's economy was growing rapidly with major investments in industry
and infrastructure. The Shah's government maintained conservative monetary
policy with price stability despite strong growth. Oil revenues were being
channeled into development without creating significant inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1969.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 3.6% in 1969. The economy was growing rapidly with
expanding oil revenues. Some price pressure began emerging as import demand
grew and construction activity accelerated. This remained a moderate and
manageable inflation rate for an economy growing at 9% per year in real terms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1970.assessment:
Low inflation of 1.7% in 1970 as the Fourth Development Plan continued.
Iran maintained excellent price stability during the early 1970s growth period.
Oil production was expanding rapidly, funding development without generating
significant inflation. The Shah's economic team managed monetary policy
effectively in this pre-oil-shock period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1971.assessment:
Moderate inflation at 4.2% in 1971, beginning to tick upward as the economy
overheated somewhat. The Shah's Persepolis celebrations involved massive
state spending. Oil production reached 4.5 million barrels per day. Inflation
remained moderate and manageable despite rising government expenditures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1972.assessment:
Inflation rose to 6.4% in 1972 as economic activity intensified ahead of the
oil price boom. Construction and import demand grew rapidly. The Fifth Development
Plan was being prepared to take advantage of growing revenues. Inflation remained
moderate and within a manageable range for an economy growing at this pace.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1973.assessment:
Inflation rose to 9.8% in 1973 as the OPEC oil embargo and price quadrupling
began affecting the economy. Petrodollar inflows surged, creating demand-side
inflation. The Shah's ambitious development program was revised massively
upward. Average CPI inflation during 1973-1977 was 15.6%, rising sharply
from the 2.6% average of the preceding decade.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1974.assessment:
Inflation accelerated sharply to 14.3% in 1974 as the full impact of oil
windfall spending hit the economy. Oil revenues quadrupled to $20 billion,
flooding the economy with petrodollars. Infrastructure bottlenecks at ports
and in construction limited the absorptive capacity of the economy. The
elevation from 2-3% in the early 1970s to double-digits reflected massive
demand-pull pressures from the oil boom.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1975.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at 12.9% in 1975 as the oil boom continued.
The Fifth Development Plan allocated $68 billion for investment, creating
massive demand-pull pressures. Infrastructure bottlenecks persisted.
The government implemented some anti-profiteering campaigns and price
controls to moderate inflation but with limited effectiveness. This level
of inflation was becoming socially and politically destabilizing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1976.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at 11.3% in 1976 despite government efforts to
control prices. Oil production peaked at 5.5-6 million barrels per day, keeping
revenues high but also sustaining inflationary pressures. The Shah's ambitious
industrialization created shortages of skilled labor and infrastructure
capacity. Inflation was becoming a source of political discontent.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1977.assessment:
Inflation surged to 27.3% in 1977, the highest rate in the Pahlavi era.
This sharp acceleration reflected continued petrodollar spending, infrastructure
bottlenecks, and overheated construction markets. The Shah's anti-corruption
campaign paradoxically disrupted supply chains. High inflation became a major
grievance driving the opposition movement and contributed to revolutionary
sentiment. The average for 1973-1977 was 15.6%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1978.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to 11.7% in 1978 as the revolutionary disruption
collapsed economic activity. Oil strikes cut production from 5 million to
under 1 million barrels per day by year end, reducing petrodollar inflows
and demand-pull pressures. Capital flight accelerated as businesses closed.
The paradoxical effect was lower measured inflation even as supply shortages
created real hardship.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1979.assessment:
Inflation at 10.5% in the revolutionary year of 1979, surprisingly moderate
given the political upheaval. Supply disruptions from the revolution and oil
sector shutdown were offset by collapsed domestic demand as businesses closed
and capital fled. The Islamic Republic nationalized major industries. US asset
freeze from the hostage crisis added external pressure. Official statistics
likely understated the actual inflation experienced by households.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1980.assessment:
Inflation surged to 20.6% in 1980 as Iran was invaded by Iraq in September.
War disruptions, continued nationalization chaos, capital flight, and supply
shortages drove prices up sharply. The Islamic Republic was implementing
major economic changes including price controls, rationing, and confiscations.
Oil production infrastructure was damaged. The combination of war and
revolutionary economic transformation created severe inflationary pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1981.assessment:
Inflation rose to 24.2% in 1981 as the Iran-Iraq War intensified. Supply
disruptions from the war zone, continued import restrictions due to
Western sanctions, and government printing to finance the war effort
drove prices up. Rationing of basic goods was implemented. The regime's
price controls partially suppressed official inflation but shortages
reflected the true extent of economic disruption.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1982.assessment:
Inflation moderated somewhat to 18.7% in 1982 as Iran recovered Khuzestan
territory from Iraq and oil exports partially recovered. Oil production
reached about 2.5 million barrels per day, providing some revenue stability.
Price controls and rationing continued. The regime adapted to war conditions
with a managed economy but inflation remained elevated throughout the
war period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1983.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at 19.7% in 1983 during continued war. The Islamic
Republic's bonyad system was consolidating control of nationalized enterprises.
Price controls kept official inflation somewhat suppressed relative to actual
shortage conditions. Military stalemate continued. The regime's ideological
approach to economics prevented market-clearing adjustments that would have
reduced shortages.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1984.assessment:
Inflation fell to 12.5% in 1984 as the Tanker War began, paradoxically
constraining demand by disrupting oil exports and revenues. The regime
tightened rationing and price controls. Demand compression from economic
hardship moderated inflation. Official statistics likely understated true
cost of living increases as black market prices diverged from controlled prices.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG
