1985.assessment:
Officially recorded inflation fell to just 4.4% in 1985, a surprisingly low
figure given ongoing war conditions. This likely reflects strong price controls
and rationing suppressing measured inflation rather than genuine price stability.
OPEC production quotas were cutting into Iranian revenues. The discrepancy
between official CPI and real-world price experience was significant during
this period of rationing and controlled economy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1986.assessment:
Inflation jumped back to 18.4% in 1986 as the global oil price collapse
devastated Iran's revenues. Oil prices fell from $28 to under $10 per barrel,
causing a fiscal crisis. The government monetized deficits to fund the war,
driving inflation up sharply. Import subsidies were cut, raising prices on
imported goods. The worst year of war-period economic deterioration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1987.assessment:
Inflation surged to 28.6% in 1987 as the fiscal crisis from the 1986 oil
price collapse continued. The Tanker War escalated with US naval intervention
(Operation Earnest Will). Iran suffered military setbacks. The government
continued to monetize deficits to fund the war. Shortages of basic goods
were widespread. This was near the peak of war-era inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1988.assessment:
Inflation remained very high at 28.7% in 1988, the year the Iran-Iraq War
ended (August ceasefire). Eight years of war had severely damaged the economy.
Monetary financing of war costs had accumulated inflationary pressures.
Despite the ceasefire, economic disruption continued and inflation remained
stubbornly high. The massive reconstruction need meant continued fiscal
pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1989.assessment:
Inflation moderated somewhat to 22.4% in 1989 following the war's end.
Khomeini died in June 1989 and Rafsanjani became president with a more
pragmatic economic approach. The First Development Plan began with some
market liberalization measures. Oil production recovered to about 3 million
barrels per day. However, reconstruction spending and import liberalization
maintained inflationary pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1990.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to 7.6% in 1990 — a surprisingly low figure for
post-war Iran. The Gulf War between Iraq and Kuwait temporarily boosted
oil prices, improving Iran's fiscal position. Rafsanjani's economic
liberalization was attracting reconstruction investment. Some analysts
suggest official CPI may have understated true inflation due to continued
price controls and subsidies on basic goods.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1991.assessment:
Inflation rebounded to 17.1% in 1991 as import liberalization under Rafsanjani
led to a rapid expansion of imports and external borrowing. The foreign exchange
market was partially liberalized. Reconstruction spending was accelerating.
External debt was growing rapidly, laying the groundwork for the 1993 crisis.
Price controls were partially removed, allowing suppressed inflation to emerge.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1992.assessment:
Inflation surged to 25.8% in 1992 as the overheated Rafsanjani reconstruction
boom created significant demand-pull inflation. External debt reached $30 billion,
approaching the crisis threshold. The multiple exchange rate system was creating
economic distortions. Import expansion driven by cheap credit was pushing prices
up as domestic capacity was strained. The approaching debt crisis added uncertainty.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1993.assessment:
Despite the 1993 debt crisis and rial depreciation, official inflation at 21.2%
was somewhat lower than 1992. The exchange rate collapse caused a sharp rise in
import prices. Austerity measures constrained demand. The real effective
inflation including black market exchange rate effects was much higher.
IMF assistance was sought and debt rescheduling was needed, compressing imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1994.assessment:
Inflation surged to 31.5% in 1994 as the full effects of the 1993 rial
devaluation worked through the economy via import prices. US sanctions were
tightened with executive orders in May 1995 (the action was announced in advance,
affecting 1994). Austerity measures were implemented but inflation remained very
high. This was the trough of the post-reconstruction economic crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1995.assessment:
Inflation peaked at 49.7% in 1995, the highest rate in modern Iranian history
up to that point (since surpassed). US comprehensive sanctions were imposed in
May 1995 via executive order targeting oil trade and investment. The rial
continued depreciating. The combination of continued debt restructuring costs,
import price inflation, and monetary financing of deficits drove inflation to
near-hyperinflation levels. This was the worst inflation crisis of the
Rafsanjani era.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1996.assessment:
Inflation fell significantly to 28.9% in 1996 as oil prices recovered and
the debt rescheduling was largely completed. The D'Amato Act (Iran-Libya
Sanctions Act) was passed by the US Congress in August 1996, but US unilateral
sanctions had limited effect as European and Asian trade continued. Fiscal
situation improved somewhat with higher oil revenues. Exchange rate partially
unified.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1997.assessment:
Inflation fell to 17.4% in 1997 as the economic crisis receded and Khatami
was elected on a reform platform in May. Oil revenues provided fiscal stability.
Monetary policy was somewhat tightened under the more pragmatic approach.
The downward trend in inflation reflected the recovery from the mid-1990s
crisis. Inflation remained elevated but was on a declining path under Khatami's
early administration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1998.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at 17.9% in 1998 as the global oil price collapse
to under $12 per barrel severely cut Iranian revenues. The Asian financial
crisis contributed to oil demand weakness. Fiscal pressures from lower revenues
forced some monetary accommodation. Khatami's reform agenda remained stalled
by conservative resistance. Inflation stayed above the long-run average.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

1999.assessment:
Inflation rose slightly to 20.1% in 1999 despite oil price recovery as the
lagged effects of the 1998 fiscal pressures worked through. Monetary expansion
continued. The Oil Stabilization Fund was being established. Khatami's reformist
Sixth Majles was being elected. Exchange rate issues and subsidy costs continued
to maintain structural inflation above 15-20%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG

2000.assessment:
Inflation fell to 14.5% in 2000 as higher oil prices (following OPEC cuts)
improved the fiscal position. The Oil Stabilization Fund was established,
creating a buffer against price volatility. Khatami's reforms included some
monetary tightening. Inflation remained chronically above 10% but was on
a downward trend from the 1995 peak of nearly 50%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2001.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 11.3% in 2001 as oil prices softened following the
global economic slowdown and September 11 attacks. Khatami was re-elected
but his reform agenda remained contested. Monetary policy remained moderately
loose but the inflation trend continued downward. Iran's chronic inflation
persisted due to structural factors including large subsidies and monetary
financing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2002.assessment:
Inflation rose to 14.3% in 2002 as oil prices recovered and government
spending increased. Nuclear program revelations in August 2002 began a
new phase of international pressure. Exchange rate reforms and subsidy
reductions contributed to inflation. Iran's structural inflation remained
persistently in the 10-20% range due to monetary policy and fiscal
pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2003.assessment:
Inflation was 16.5% in 2003 as oil prices surged following the Iraq War in
March. Higher government spending from oil revenues drove demand. Iran signed
the Additional Protocol with the IAEA. Structural factors kept inflation
stubbornly above 15%. The economy was growing strongly but generating
ongoing inflationary pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2004.assessment:
Inflation at 14.8% in 2004 as the oil boom continued. Chinese demand drove
global oil prices up. Nuclear dispute intensified with suspension under the
Paris Agreement. Government spending maintained elevated prices. The central
bank had limited independence to fight inflation against fiscal pressures
from oil windfall spending.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2005.assessment:
Inflation moderated slightly to 13.4% in 2005. Ahmadinejad was elected in
June 2005 on a populist platform promising economic redistribution. His
policies would subsequently increase inflationary pressure through expanded
subsidies and credit expansion. Iran resumed uranium enrichment at Isfahan
in August. The relatively contained inflation in 2005 preceded the
Ahmadinejad-era expansion.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2006.assessment:
Inflation fell to just above 10% in 2006, the lowest level in many years.
High oil revenues reduced fiscal pressure and helped subsidize basic goods.
UNSC Resolution 1737 imposed targeted sanctions but had limited immediate
economic effect. Ahmadinejad's expanded social spending was not yet fully
inflationary as oil revenues were covering the costs. This represented
a brief period of relative price stability.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2007.assessment:
Inflation accelerated to 17.3% in 2007 as Ahmadinejad's populist policies
expanded credit through state banks, distributing loans across the country.
UNSC Resolution 1747 imposed additional sanctions in March. Housing prices
surged. The expansion of money supply through directed lending created
significant inflationary pressure. Oil prices approaching $100 per barrel
added import price pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2008.assessment:
Inflation surged to 25.4% in 2008 as Ahmadinejad's credit expansion policies
drove massive monetary growth. UNSC Resolution 1803 (March 2008) tightened
sanctions. Oil prices peaked at $147/barrel but then crashed globally. The
combination of loose monetary policy, subsidy costs, and external sanctions
created significant inflation. This marked a renewed acceleration from
the 2006 low.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2009.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to 13.6% in 2009 as the global financial crisis
depressed oil prices and economic activity. The disputed June election led
to the Green Movement protests. Lower oil revenues constrained government
spending. The sharp demand slowdown in the second half partially offset
monetary expansion. This represented a brief disinflationary respite.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2010.assessment:
Inflation at 10.1% in 2010, again relatively contained. UNSC Resolution 1929
imposed the most comprehensive sanctions package yet in June 2010. The subsidy
reform program began in December, replacing energy and food subsidies with
cash transfers. This reform would significantly impact inflation in subsequent
years. Oil revenues recovered with higher prices.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2011.assessment:
Inflation surged to 26.3% in 2011 as the December 2010 subsidy reform
worked through the economy, raising energy and food prices dramatically.
The removal of subsidies on gasoline alone raised prices by 400%. High
oil prices maintained government revenues but did not prevent the inflation
surge from subsidy elimination. US and EU comprehensive sanctions on oil
and banking began being imposed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2012.assessment:
Inflation rose to 27.3% in 2012 as comprehensive Western sanctions targeting
oil exports and banking took full effect. US and EU sanctions cut oil exports
by approximately 50%. The rial lost over 50% of its value against the dollar.
Iran was excluded from SWIFT banking system in March 2012. The import price
inflation from the rial collapse added significantly to CPI. Note: some
estimates for 2012 inflation are higher (~30%+) given the rial collapse.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2013.assessment:
Inflation spiked to 36.6% in 2013 as the full impact of comprehensive sanctions
drove import prices sharply higher via rial depreciation. Rouhani won the
presidential election in June on a platform of negotiations and economic relief.
The Joint Plan of Action interim nuclear deal in November 2013 provided some
relief. Official statistics may understate true inflation given black market
exchange rate divergence from official rates.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2014.assessment:
Inflation fell sharply to 16.6% in 2014 under Rouhani's economic stabilization
policies. The Joint Plan of Action provided limited sanctions relief and nuclear
negotiations continued. Rouhani's team tightened monetary policy and reduced
the fiscal deficit. The sharp disinflation from 37% to 17% reflected genuine
policy improvement. Oil prices began falling sharply in H2 2014, which added
some deflation pressure on imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2015.assessment:
Inflation fell further to 12.5% in 2015 as Rouhani's monetary stabilization
continued. The JCPOA nuclear deal was signed in July 2015, creating expectations
of sanctions relief. Oil prices collapsed below $40, reducing import price
pressures. The rial stabilized. Inflation continued declining from the 2013 peak.
The JCPOA implementation date of January 2016 was pending at year end.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2016.assessment:
Inflation fell to a historic post-revolutionary low of 7.3% in 2016 as JCPOA
sanctions relief took effect from January. Oil exports recovered to about 2.5
million barrels per day. The rial appreciated. Rouhani's monetary tightening
was effective. This represented the best price stability since the early
post-revolutionary period and reflected the JCPOA's positive economic impact.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2017.assessment:
Inflation remained low at 8.0% in 2017 under JCPOA. Rouhani was re-elected
in May 2017. Foreign investment had not materialized at expected levels
due to remaining US secondary sanctions. Trump administration increasing
hostility to the JCPOA created uncertainty. Despite the positive inflation
environment, structural economic weakness continued. This was the second
consecutive year of single-digit inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2018.assessment:
Inflation surged back to 18% in 2018 as the US withdrew from the JCPOA in
May and reimposed comprehensive sanctions in August and November. The rial
collapsed, losing approximately 60% of its value against the dollar during 2018.
Import prices rose sharply. Official statistics likely understate true inflation
given parallel market exchange rates. The acceleration from 8% in 2017 to 18%
marked the resumption of the sanctions-driven inflation crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2019.assessment:
Inflation surged to 39.9% in 2019 as maximum pressure sanctions took full
effect. Oil exports fell to near zero at points, cutting government revenues
dramatically. The rial continued depreciating. November 2019 fuel price
increases triggered protests with hundreds killed in the crackdown. Official
statistics are less reliable during this period due to divergence between
official and black market exchange rates.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2020.assessment:
Inflation at 30.6% in 2020 as COVID-19 hit Iran severely with one of the
worst early global outbreaks. Sanctions continued at maximum pressure levels.
Soleimani assassination in January 2020 heightened tensions. Iran partially
evaded sanctions through oil sales to China. Monetary expansion to fund
COVID-19 response added to inflation. The combination of supply disruptions
and demand stimulus maintained very high inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG, accessed 2026-03-02

2021.assessment:
Inflation reached 43.4% in 2021, the highest level since 1995. Raisi was
elected president in June in a low-turnout election after major candidate
disqualifications. JCPOA revival talks began in Vienna but made slow progress.
Iran significantly increased illicit oil exports to China, but revenues were
insufficient to stabilize the rial. Continued monetary expansion to fund
deficits drove inflation to near-hyperinflation levels.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, April 2025

2022.assessment:
Inflation remained near 43.5% in 2022 as high global oil prices due to the
Ukraine War boosted Iran's illicit export revenues, but sanctions prevented
full benefit. JCPOA negotiations collapsed in August 2022 with no deal.
The Mahsa Amini protests from September represented the largest domestic
uprising in decades. Official statistics are particularly unreliable during
crisis periods; actual consumer price increases may have been higher in
some categories.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, April 2025

2023.assessment:
Inflation reached 44.6% in 2023, the highest since the 1995 crisis and reflecting
continued sanctions impact and monetary expansion. Iran's illicit oil exports to
China reached 1.5-2 million barrels per day but revenues were trapped abroad due
to sanctions. Saudi-Iran rapprochement in March 2023 improved regional environment
without addressing structural inflation drivers. Iran ranked 4th globally for
inflation after Venezuela, Sudan, and Zimbabwe.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, April 2025

2024.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 32.5% in 2024, still very high but declining from the
2023 peak. President Raisi died in a helicopter crash in May 2024 and Pezeshkian
was elected in July. Iran and Israel exchanged direct military strikes in April
and October, creating significant geopolitical uncertainty. The rial continued
weakening. Domestic economic discontent grew amid persistent high inflation.
IMF estimates may differ from official statistics due to methodology differences.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, April 2025

2025.assessment:
IMF projects Iran's inflation at 42.4% for 2025, a re-acceleration from 2024.
The rial continued collapsing under continued sanctions pressure and capital
flight. Growing domestic instability ahead of the 2026 regime change created
accelerating monetary disorder. Reports indicate inflation in October 2025
reached 48.6%, suggesting the annual average could exceed IMF projections.
Official statistics increasingly unreliable as regime credibility weakens.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, April 2025 (projection)

2026.assessment:
IMF projected inflation of approximately 41.6% for 2026, though this projection
was made before the extent of regime collapse was fully apparent. Actual inflation
during a regime change/collapse year is likely higher than IMF projections, as
collapse of monetary authority and supply chain disruptions typically accelerate
inflation. Historical analogies (Iran 1979, Libya 2011, Iraq 2003) suggest
significant inflation spikes during transition periods.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, projection for 2026

1960.assessment:
Trade openness at 31.2% of GDP in 1960, reflecting a relatively modest
level of integration for an oil-producing country. Iran's economy in 1960
was still predominantly agrarian with oil revenues under the 1954 consortium
agreement providing some export income. Imports were constrained by foreign
exchange availability. The restricted_20_40pct classification reflects a
moderately open economy for its development stage.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1961.assessment:
Trade openness at 29.6% of GDP in 1961. Iran's trade with the West was
primarily oil exports through the consortium and imports of manufactured goods.
The relatively modest level of integration reflected Iran's developing economy
status. Trade policy was moderately protective of domestic industries.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1962.assessment:
Trade openness slightly below 28% of GDP in 1962. The Shah's development
programs were beginning to increase import demand for capital goods and
industrial inputs. White Revolution planning was underway. Oil exports were
the dominant export. Trade remained restricted by developing country standards.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1963.assessment:
Trade openness at 27.8% of GDP in 1963, near its lowest point for the
pre-oil-boom era. The White Revolution was launched with land reform and
modernization. While industrial imports were growing, Iran's export base
remained almost exclusively oil. The relatively closed level reflects
the early stage of development.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1964.assessment:
Trade openness rose to 32.7% of GDP in 1964 as oil exports grew and imports
of capital goods for development accelerated. The Shah's Fourth Development Plan
was drawing up plans requiring significant capital good imports. Oil consortium
revenues were growing, providing the forex to pay for expanded imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1965.assessment:
Trade openness stable near 32.6% of GDP in 1965. Iran's economy was growing
with rising oil revenues supporting development program imports. The trade
structure was classic for an oil-developing country: petroleum exports and
manufactured goods imports. Trade policy protected domestic industries
through import tariffs and quotas.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1966.assessment:
Trade openness at 33.9% of GDP in 1966. Gradual expansion of trade driven
by growing oil revenues and development-related imports. The economy was
in the Third Development Plan era with manufacturing investments requiring
capital goods. Iran was becoming a larger trading partner for Western nations.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1967.assessment:
Trade openness increased to 37.0% of GDP in 1967 as both exports and imports
grew. Oil production was expanding rapidly with production reaching near
3 million barrels per day. Import demand for capital goods and consumer
products was rising with economic growth. Iran was moving toward moderate
trade openness.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1968.assessment:
Trade openness at 38.0% of GDP in 1968, near the moderate_40_70pct threshold.
The Fourth Development Plan was in full swing with major imports of capital
equipment. Oil revenues were the dominant export earner. Iran's trade was
becoming significant globally as oil production continued growing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1969.assessment:
Trade openness at 39.1% of GDP in 1969, just below the moderate_40_70pct
threshold. Oil production continued expanding toward 4 million barrels per
day. Import demand for development was growing. Iran was approaching the
level of moderate trade integration as the oil sector expanded.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1970.assessment:
Trade openness at 39.7% in 1970, still just below the 40% moderate threshold.
Iran's economy was growing strongly with oil production near 3.8 million
barrels per day. Fourth Development Plan investments were driving capital
good imports. Iran was becoming a significant trading partner for European
and Japanese manufacturers.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1971.assessment:
Trade openness crossed into the moderate_40_70pct range at 44.4% of GDP in
1971 as oil production reached near 4.5 million barrels per day. Shah's
Persepolis celebrations increased imports of luxury and construction goods.
Oil revenues were supporting dramatically higher import capacity. Iran was
becoming a major oil exporter and consumer of imported goods.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1972.assessment:
Trade openness remained near 44.5% in 1972, similar to 1971 level. The
Fifth Development Plan was being prepared. Oil revenues were growing but
the full oil price shock hadn't yet hit. Iran was importing significant
quantities of capital goods, military equipment, and consumer products.
Trade structure still primarily oil for manufactured goods.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1973.assessment:
Trade openness surged to 54.1% in 1973 as the OPEC oil embargo quadrupled
oil prices and Iran's oil export revenues exploded. The oil price increase
dramatically raised the value of exports relative to GDP while also enabling
a surge in imports. The Shah's revised development plans called for massive
new capital goods imports. Iran was becoming one of the most rapidly
opening economies in the world.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1974.assessment:
Trade openness surged to 71.4% in 1974, crossing into the open_70_100pct range.
Oil revenues quadrupled to $20 billion. Imports surged to unprecedented levels
as the Shah revised the Fifth Development Plan upward massively. Port bottlenecks
emerged as the country could not absorb the flood of imports fast enough.
Iran was one of the most rapidly opening economies globally during this
petrodollar windfall period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1975.assessment:
Trade openness reached its all-time peak of 76.1% of GDP in 1975, the
maximum in Iranian history. This extraordinary level reflects both record
oil export revenues and unprecedented import volumes as the Shah's Fifth
Development Plan ($68 billion) was implemented. Foreign workers, capital
goods, consumer products, and military hardware all poured into the country.
Iran had become one of the most trade-integrated economies in the region.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1976.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 65.4% in 1976 as global oil prices stabilized and
some import absorption constraints moderated the pace of imports. Oil production
peaked at 5.5-6 million barrels per day. The economy was still very trade-open
by any standard, with both exports and imports at high levels relative to GDP.
Import controls were beginning to be applied to manage the import boom.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1977.assessment:
Trade openness declined to 59.9% in 1977 as import controls were tightened
and political instability began affecting economic activity. The Shah's
anti-corruption campaign disrupted some import channels. Opposition protests
were beginning. Despite the decline, Iran remained highly trade-open by
regional standards.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1978.assessment:
Trade openness fell sharply to 45.7% in 1978 as the revolution disrupted
economic activity. Oil strikes from September 1978 cut exports dramatically.
Capital flight reduced imports as business confidence collapsed. The Shah
fled in January 1979. By year end both exports and imports were severely
disrupted, representing a major unwinding of the oil boom trade integration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1979.assessment:
Trade openness at 42.8% in 1979, the Islamic Revolution year. Oil exports
resumed at reduced levels after the revolution but were disrupted. US sanctions
froze Iranian assets and disrupted trade. Imports declined sharply as the new
government nationalized businesses, foreign companies left, and foreign exchange
became restricted. Trade was transitioning from the oil boom structure to the
post-revolutionary restricted economy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1980.assessment:
Trade openness at 42.5% in 1980, the first year of the Iran-Iraq War. The
war disrupted oil export infrastructure while sanctioned trade continued via
alternative channels. Iraq invaded in September 1980, damaging oil facilities.
Western sanctions limited access to traditional trade partners. Trade
shifted toward non-Western partners. The relatively stable ratio reflects
offsetting war-driven disruptions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1981.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 40.3% in 1981 as war and sanctions increasingly
disrupted trade. Hostage crisis ended in January 1981 but Western sanctions
continued. Oil exports were disrupted by Iraqi attacks. Import capacity was
constrained by sanctions and foreign exchange scarcity. The economy was
adapting to a wartime trade regime. Just above the restricted threshold.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1982.assessment:
Trade openness fell below 40% to 38.1% in 1982 as war conditions further
constrained trade. Iran recovered Khuzestan territory, partially restoring
oil infrastructure. Oil production recovered to ~2.5 million barrels per day
but was well below the 5.5 mb/d pre-war peak. Import capacity remained
constrained by sanctions and war priorities.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1983.assessment:
Trade openness at 38.7% in 1983, slightly above the 1982 low. War economy
continued with oil export revenues funding essential imports. Bonyad system
controlling nationalized enterprises was adapting to war-era trade patterns.
Non-military imports were restricted while military procurement continued
through third-party channels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1984.assessment:
Trade openness fell sharply to 27.9% in 1984 as the Tanker War began.
Iraqi attacks on Iranian oil shipping and Kharg Island terminal severely
disrupted oil exports. Insurance costs surged making exports uneconomical
through normal channels. Import capacity was constrained. The restricted
trade level reflected wartime disruption to Iran's trade infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1985.assessment:
Trade openness fell further to 23.2% in 1985 as war and sanctions continued
to restrict trade. OPEC production cuts were reducing oil revenues. Military
stalemate continued with massive casualties. The economy was increasingly
self-isolated as both exports and imports declined relative to GDP. Import
substitution policies were promoted by necessity.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1986.assessment:
Trade openness collapsed to 14.1% of GDP in 1986 — the historic minimum
in modern Iranian history. The global oil price crash from $28 to under $10
per barrel devastated export revenues. Exports fell dramatically in value.
Imports were severely constrained by foreign exchange scarcity. Iran was
approaching effective autarky as war, sanctions, and oil price collapse
simultaneously restricted international commerce.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1987.assessment:
Trade openness recovered slightly to 17.9% in 1987 as oil prices partially
recovered. US naval intervention (Operation Earnest Will) protected Kuwaiti
tankers. Iran suffered military setbacks. Trade remained severely restricted
by war and sanctions. The Tanker War continued disrupting oil exports.
Iran remained in the closed_lt_20pct classification.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1988.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to 21.6% in 1988 as the war ended in August and
oil production began recovering. The ceasefire allowed some trade channel
restoration. Import demand for reconstruction was beginning. Oil export
capacity was damaged but recovering. The war's end opened prospects for
gradual trade normalization, though Western sanctions continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1989.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to 27.9% in 1989 as post-war reconstruction began.
Rafsanjani became president and launched the First Development Plan with import
liberalization. External borrowing was increasing to fund reconstruction imports.
Oil production was recovering. The First Plan's opening approach allowed
significant expansion of imports beyond war-era restrictions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1990.assessment:
Trade openness rose strongly to 37.1% in 1990 as Rafsanjani's reconstruction
import boom accelerated. Gulf War boosted oil prices briefly. External debt
grew rapidly as imports surged ahead of debt crisis capacity. The First
Development Plan's import liberalization was rapidly restoring trade
integration after the war era's near-autarky.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1991.assessment:
Trade openness reached 44.2% in 1991, returning to the moderate range.
Reconstruction imports peaked as the First Development Plan drove massive
investment. External debt approaching $30 billion. Oil production recovering
toward 3.5 million barrels per day. The rapid trade opening from 14% in 1986
to 44% in 1991 reflected the dramatic post-war normalization.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1992.assessment:
Trade openness at 40.4% in 1992, near the moderate threshold. Debt crisis
was approaching as external obligations exceeded repayment capacity. Import
surge was beginning to slow as credit constraints tightened. The multiple
exchange rate system was creating distortions in trade measurement.
Approaching debt crisis would soon force a sharp reduction in imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1993.assessment:
Trade openness paradoxically rose to 46.0% in 1993 despite the debt crisis.
This reflects the rial devaluation: as the currency collapsed, the dollar
value of GDP denominator fell faster than trade flows, mechanically increasing
the ratio. The actual trade volume was contracting due to austerity but the
nominal ratio increased. Debt rescheduling forced import restrictions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1994.assessment:
Trade openness at 41.6% in 1994 during the crisis trough. Austerity measures
reduced imports significantly. US sanctions tightened. The rial devaluation
continued affecting trade ratios. Despite crisis conditions, Iran maintained
significant trade activity through European and Asian partners unaffected by
US sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1995.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 35.1% in 1995 as US comprehensive sanctions imposed
in May significantly affected trade. Oil revenues were constrained and austerity
continued. Import restrictions were maintained. The Second Development Plan
had more modest trade ambitions. Debt rescheduling was being resolved but
trade remained restricted compared to the Rafsanjani reconstruction peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1996.assessment:
Trade openness stable near 35.2% in 1996 as debt rescheduling completed and
recovery began. D'Amato Act (Iran-Libya Sanctions Act) was passed in August 1996
but had limited impact on European and Asian trade. Oil prices recovering with
OPEC discipline improving. Exchange rate partially unified. Second Development
Plan continuing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1997.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 32.6% in 1997. Khatami was elected president in May
1997 on a reform platform. Oil prices were volatile and fell toward year end.
Despite optimism about reforms, trade integration remained constrained by
US sanctions. European investment and trade continued. The restricted level
reflected continued sanctions impact.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1998.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 29.2% in 1998 as the global oil price collapsed to
under $12 per barrel, devastating export revenue. Asian financial crisis
reduced demand. Import capacity was constrained by fiscal pressures. Khatami's
reform program continued facing resistance. Trade remained significantly
below the oil boom levels and modestly below 30% of GDP.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

1999.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to 34.9% in 1999 as oil prices rebounded following
OPEC production cuts. Iran played a constructive role in the OPEC agreement.
Exports grew as oil prices recovered. The Oil Stabilization Fund was being
planned. Trade integration was recovering from the 1998 low.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2000.assessment:
Trade openness crossed back into moderate range at 41.3% in 2000 as high
oil prices boosted exports. Oil Stabilization Fund established. Khatami's
reformist parliament (Sixth Majles) elected. Real GDP growth approximately 5%.
Iran was expanding trade with Asian partners. European investment was cautiously
growing despite US sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2001.assessment:
Trade openness at 40.5% in 2001, just above moderate threshold. September 11
attacks affected global demand and oil prices. Khatami was re-elected but
reform agenda stalled. Iran's trade remained moderate despite US sanctions
with European and Asian trade continuing. Nuclear program tensions were
beginning to build.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2002.assessment:
Trade openness rose to 48.2% in 2002 as oil prices recovered and Iran's
economy grew strongly. Nuclear program revelations in August 2002 began
escalating tensions. Strong economic growth drove import demand. Iran was
integrating more into the global economy despite US bilateral sanctions,
with European and Asian trade expanding. Trade integration was approaching
levels not seen since the late 1990s.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2003.assessment:
Trade openness reached 50.7% in 2003 as oil prices surged following the Iraq
War. Iran signed the Additional Protocol with the IAEA. GDP per capita crossed
the 1977 pre-revolution peak. The economy was growing strongly with imports
rising rapidly. Iran's trade integration was returning to levels approaching
the pre-revolutionary oil boom era.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2004.assessment:
Trade openness at 51.3% in 2004 as the oil boom continued. Chinese demand
driving oil prices. Nuclear dispute intensifying with Paris Agreement. Strong
economic growth drove import demand. Iran's trade integration was robust
despite US bilateral sanctions, with Asian trade becoming more prominent.
Iranian oil exports to Asia were growing rapidly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2005.assessment:
Trade openness at 54.4% in 2005. Ahmadinejad elected in June. Iran resumed
uranium enrichment in August. High oil revenues and growing imports. Despite
the election of a more radical government, trade integration continued at
moderate-to-high levels. Ahmadinejad's populist policies expanded imports of
consumer goods and maintained high government spending.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2006.assessment:
Trade openness at 53.2% in 2006. UNSC Resolution 1737 imposed targeted
sanctions in December but had limited immediate trade impact. Oil prices near
record levels. Ahmadinejad's subsidy programs maintained import demand.
Trade integration remained robust with European and Asian partners despite
US pressure. Iran was providing significant oil revenues to fund imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2007.assessment:
Trade openness at 49.9% in 2007. UNSC Resolution 1747 (March 2007) tightened
targeted sanctions. Oil prices approached $100 per barrel. Ahmadinejad's
credit expansion was boosting import demand. Nuclear tensions escalating.
Despite growing sanctions pressure, trade remained moderate-to-strong with
European and especially Asian partners.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2008.assessment:
Trade openness at 48.2% in 2008. Oil peaked at $147 in July then crashed.
UNSC Resolution 1803 imposed expanded sanctions. Global financial crisis
reduced trade in H2. First half strong with high oil revenues, second half
weak with price crash. Annual average remained solid. Trade integration
was robust until the financial crisis hit.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2009.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 43.7% in 2009 as the global financial crisis depressed
oil prices to the $40-60 range. Green Movement protests followed the disputed
election. Lower oil revenues reduced import capacity. The economy slowed.
Trade integration remained moderate despite sanctions and political turmoil.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2010.assessment:
Trade openness held steady at 43.8% in 2010. UNSC Resolution 1929 (June 2010)
imposed the most comprehensive UN sanctions package yet. US and EU unilateral
sanctions also tightened significantly. Subsidy reform began in December.
Despite tightening sanctions, trade integration remained moderate as Asian
partners (especially China) continued trade.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2011.assessment:
Trade openness at 41.2% in 2011, Iran's GDP peak year. High oil prices over
$100 supported high export revenues. However, the buildup to comprehensive
Western sanctions was already constraining trade access. The US and EU were
building the legal architecture for oil sanctions to be imposed in January 2012.
This was the last year before sanctions devastated trade.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2012.assessment:
Trade openness at 44.1% in 2012, paradoxically higher than 2011 despite
comprehensive oil sanctions. US and EU oil sanctions in January 2012 cut
exports by ~50%. SWIFT exclusion in March disrupted banking. The higher ratio
may reflect statistical artifacts from rial depreciation reducing the GDP
denominator. True trade integration declined significantly with Western
sanctions while China trade partially compensated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2013.assessment:
Trade openness at 47.1% in 2013. Rouhani elected in June. Joint Plan of
Action interim nuclear deal in November. Currency effects continue to inflate
trade ratio relative to GDP denominator (rial continued depreciating). Actual
trade volumes were constrained by sanctions but the dollar-denominated ratio
reflects ongoing China trade and the statistical effect of currency depreciation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2014.assessment:
Trade openness at 45.3% in 2014 under the Joint Plan of Action nuclear deal.
Limited sanctions relief allowed partial oil export recovery. Oil prices
started falling sharply in H2 2014. Rouhani's stabilization also moderated
import demand somewhat. Trade integration remained moderate despite
comprehensive sanctions, primarily through Asian partners.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2015.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 39.4% in 2015 as oil prices collapsed below $40 per
barrel, dramatically reducing export revenues. JCPOA was signed in July 2015
but implementation awaited 2016. Lower oil revenues meant lower import
capacity. The restricted level reflects the oil price crash more than
sanctions changes.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2016.assessment:
Trade openness at 39.9% in 2016, at the restricted/moderate boundary.
JCPOA implementation began in January. Oil exports recovered to ~2.5 million
barrels per day. GDP growth was 12-13% but largely oil-driven. Foreign
investment inquiries increased but oil prices remained low, limiting
the export value recovery. Trade was recovering but constrained by
remaining US secondary sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2017.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to 43.2% in 2017 under JCPOA. Rouhani re-elected
in May. Trade was recovering with European firms cautiously re-engaging.
Oil exports near pre-sanctions levels. However, Trump administration
hostility to the JCPOA was deterring the foreign investment that would have
further expanded trade integration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2018.assessment:
Trade openness surged to 56.6% in 2018, paradoxically, the year of JCPOA
withdrawal. US withdrew from JCPOA in May and reimposed comprehensive sanctions
in August and November. The rial collapsed 60%, shrinking the GDP denominator
in dollar terms dramatically. Oil exports fell from 2.5 to 1.5 mb/d by
year end. The high ratio largely reflects the currency depreciation statistical
effect rather than genuine trade integration.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2019.assessment:
Trade openness at 48.6% in 2019 under maximum pressure sanctions. Oil exports
fell to near zero at points as waivers expired in May 2019. Iran was trading
primarily with China, Turkey, Iraq, and Syria. The rial continued depreciating.
The nominal ratio reflects currency effects masking the real contraction in
trade volumes and the shift to illicit/restricted trade channels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2020.assessment:
Trade openness at 43.9% in 2020 despite COVID-19 and continued maximum
pressure sanctions. COVID-19 hit Iran severely. Soleimani assassination
in January. Iran increasingly dependent on China for trade. The nominal
ratio again reflects currency effects rather than genuine trade openness.
Actual trade volumes were severely constrained by sanctions and COVID.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2021.assessment:
Trade openness at 42.7% in 2021. Raisi elected in June. Iran significantly
expanded illicit oil exports to China. JCPOA revival talks began in Vienna.
While nominal trade ratios suggest moderate integration, the actual trade
was increasingly conducted through unofficial channels, sanctions evasion
mechanisms, and barter arrangements. Trade quality had deteriorated significantly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2022.assessment:
Trade openness at 48.9% in 2022. Ukraine War boosted global oil prices,
raising Iran's illicit export revenues. JCPOA talks collapsed in August.
Mahsa Amini protests from September. Iran's trade was increasingly China-centric
with sanctions evasion dominant. The nominal ratio again reflects currency
effects and the high oil price impact on nominal export values.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2023.assessment:
Trade openness at 49.4% in 2023. Saudi-Iran rapprochement in March 2023
improved regional trade environment. Iran's illicit oil exports to China
reached 1.5-2 million barrels per day. Trade was conducted primarily through
China, Iraq, Turkey, and UAE as intermediaries. The nominal ratio masks
the quality and structure of Iran's trade — heavily sanctions-evading
and dependent on a narrow set of partners.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2024.assessment:
Trade openness at 52.1% in 2024. Raisi died in May 2024; Pezeshkian elected.
Iran-Israel military exchanges in April and October created disruption risks.
Despite the nominal moderate openness ratio, trade quality deteriorated
as growing political instability added risk for trading partners. China
remained the dominant trade partner. The nominal ratio overstates true
trade integration quality.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS

2025.assessment:
No World Bank data available for 2025 yet. Growing domestic instability
ahead of regime change was likely reducing trade integration as capital
flight accelerated and trading partners became more cautious. China
continued as the primary trading partner. The rial's collapse reduced
the dollar value of trade. Trade was increasingly conducted through
sanctions-evasion channels.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment; World Bank 2025 data not yet available

2026.assessment:
No data available for 2026 regime change year. Regime collapse typically
severely disrupts trade as export mechanisms break down, payments systems
fail, and trading partners halt contracts. Oil production disruption during
regime change would collapse export revenues. Historical analogies suggest
trade openness falls sharply during transition years (Iran 1979 fell from
60% to 43%). True trade disruption would be severe.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment based on regime transition analogy; no data

1960.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available for Iran in 1960. The economy was predominantly
agrarian. World Bank ILO modeled series begins only in 1991. Iran's labor market
in 1960 was largely informal with subsistence agriculture dominant. Unemployment
as a modern concept was not well measured. Qualitative evidence suggests
underemployment was widespread in rural areas while formal sector employment
was limited.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS: no data available before 1991

1961.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran's labor market in 1961 remained
predominantly informal and agricultural. The economy was beginning to grow
under the Shah's development programs but formal sector employment was limited.
Seasonal agricultural underemployment was common.

1962.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. White Revolution land reforms were beginning
to transform the agricultural labor market. As subsistence farmers became
landowners, some rural-urban migration began, potentially increasing urban
unemployment. No reliable statistics available for this period.

1963.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The White Revolution in 1963 accelerated
land reform and rural-urban migration. The Shah's industrialization push was
beginning to create formal sector jobs. Growing oil sector employment was
expanding. No reliable unemployment statistics for this period.

1964.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran's labor market was transforming
under the Shah's development programs. Industrial employment was growing
as factories were built. Rural-urban migration created urban labor pools
with varying employment prospects. No statistical basis for quantitative estimate.

1965.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran was experiencing strong economic growth
of 8-9% per year with expanding formal sector employment. The oil sector,
government, and growing manufacturing absorbed much of the labor force.
Unemployment was likely moderate by developing country standards.

1966.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Third Development Plan investments were
creating employment in industry and construction. Growing oil revenues
funded public sector employment. Labor market transforming from agricultural
to urban-industrial. No quantitative data available.

1967.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Fourth Development Plan preparation
period. Iran's economy was growing rapidly with strong formal sector job
creation in government, manufacturing, and oil. The labor market was
tightening as rapid growth absorbed workers. No quantitative data available.

1968.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Fourth Development Plan began. Iran's
economy was accelerating with new industrial investments creating employment.
Large construction projects were absorbing labor. The formal employment
sector was growing but remained a minority of total employment. No quantitative data.

1969.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran's rapid economic growth continued
with strong job creation. The labor market was absorbing rural migrants.
Iran's population was growing rapidly, creating pressure on job creation.
Skilled labor was in particularly high demand. No quantitative data available.

1970.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran's economy was growing rapidly in
the early Fourth Development Plan phase. Oil-driven growth created expanding
formal employment. The labor market was generally absorbing population growth
during this period of high GDP growth. No quantitative data available.

1971.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Iran's economy accelerating with massive
Persepolis celebrations driving spending. Oil production at 4.5 million
barrels per day. Employment was growing rapidly across sectors. Skilled
labor shortages were beginning to emerge. No quantitative data available.

1972.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Pre-oil shock period of strong growth.
Fifth Development Plan preparation. Employment was generally strong.
The labor market was absorbing Iranian workers along with significant
imports of foreign labor for major construction projects. No quantitative data.

1973.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. OPEC oil embargo created massive revenue
surge. The Shah's development programs were creating high demand for labor.
Foreign workers were being imported for major projects. Unemployment was
likely low given the construction and infrastructure boom. No quantitative data.

1974.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The oil boom of 1974 created extraordinary
labor demand. Oil revenues quadrupled. Major development projects created
employment across sectors. Foreign workers (Koreans, Filipinos, Europeans)
were imported in large numbers to supplement domestic labor. Unemployment
was likely very low but skilled labor was scarce. No quantitative data.

1975.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The oil boom continued. The Fifth
Development Plan at $68 billion created massive employment demand.
Skilled labor shortages were a significant constraint on development.
Large numbers of foreign workers supplemented the Iranian workforce.
Unemployment was likely at or near historic lows. No quantitative data.

1976.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Oil production at peak ~5.5-6 million
barrels per day. The economy was fully employed with labor being imported.
Rapid industrialization demanded more skilled workers than were available.
Social tensions from rising inequality were growing despite near-full employment.
No quantitative data available.

1977.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Despite near-full employment, political
opposition was growing. The Shah's anti-corruption campaign disrupted some
businesses. Labor markets remained tight with foreign workers. The approaching
revolution was not yet clearly reflected in unemployment statistics.
No quantitative data available.

1978.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The revolutionary disruption in 1978 created
significant unemployment as businesses closed, construction halted, and capital
fled. Oil strikes caused mass work stoppages. Many managers and skilled workers
left for exile. By year end, unemployment was rising sharply from the near-full
employment of the oil boom years. No quantitative data available.

1979.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The Islamic Revolution caused massive
unemployment as businesses were nationalized, abandoned, or closed. Tens of
thousands of skilled workers, managers, and professionals fled the country.
Foreign workers left. The public sector replaced much of the private sector
but was unable to absorb all displaced workers. Unemployment likely surged
significantly above Pahlavi-era levels. No quantitative data.

1980.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. The Iran-Iraq War began in September 1980,
with massive military mobilization absorbing unemployed men into the armed
forces, paradoxically reducing measured unemployment. However, industrial
production dropped sharply as the war disrupted supply chains. Unemployment
was distorted by war mobilization. Khuzestan industrial zone was in a war zone.
No quantitative data available.

1981.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War economy continued. Large-scale military
mobilization reduced civilian unemployment. The government expanded public sector
employment dramatically. Sanctions limited industrial imports needed for production.
Underemployment in remaining industries was high. No quantitative data available.

1982.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War continued. Iran counterattacked and
recovered Khuzestan, somewhat restoring industrial capacity. Military
mobilization continued to absorb labor. Government employment was expanding.
Real economic activity remained depressed with hidden underemployment.
No quantitative data available.

1983.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War economy adaptation period. The bonyad
system was consolidating control of nationalized enterprises and providing
employment. Military continued to absorb labor. Rural unemployment may have
been somewhat lower as wartime demand for agricultural products continued.
No quantitative data.

1984.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Tanker War began, disrupting oil revenues.
The economy was contracting with military spending diverting resources from
investment. Industrial production dropped further. War mobilization continued
to suppress measured unemployment but real economic hardship was widespread.
No quantitative data available.

1985.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War continued with no end in sight.
Military mobilization was at its peak, directly suppressing unemployment
statistics while the productive economy suffered. OPEC cuts were reducing
oil revenues. The government maintained full employment appearance through
public sector expansion funded by declining oil revenues. No quantitative data.

1986.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Oil price collapse devastated revenues.
The worst economic year of the war period. Military mobilization was still
absorbing much male labor. Industrial production dropped sharply. Real
unemployment and underemployment were severe despite low official figures.
The economy was operating well below capacity. No quantitative data.

1987.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War at its final phase with massive military
engagement. High levels of military mobilization continued. The productive economy
was severely strained. US naval intervention in the Gulf threatened tanker traffic.
Real underemployment was high though official unemployment figures were likely
artificially low due to mobilization. No quantitative data.

1988.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. War ended August 1988. Demobilization began,
releasing large numbers of soldiers back into the labor market. This sudden
increase in labor supply likely created significant unemployment pressure
as the civilian economy struggled to absorb returning veterans. The
reconstruction challenge ahead required massive new employment creation.
No quantitative data available.

1989.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Post-war adjustment period. Rafsanjani
became president with reconstruction as the priority. Demobilization
continued releasing soldiers. The First Development Plan began creating
construction and infrastructure employment. However, the flood of returning
veterans likely kept unemployment elevated during this transition period.
No quantitative data.

1990.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available. Reconstruction boom absorbing labor.
The First Development Plan was accelerating. Construction activity grew
rapidly as major infrastructure projects began. Gulf War boosted oil prices
supporting government spending. Unemployment was likely declining from post-war
demobilization highs as reconstruction created jobs. No quantitative data.

1991.assessment:
Unemployment at 11.1% in 1991 per ILO modeled estimate, the first year
available in the series. This reflects the post-war labor market with
demobilization largely complete but full absorption of returning soldiers
not yet achieved. Rafsanjani's reconstruction program was creating jobs
in construction and infrastructure. External debt was growing rapidly as
reconstruction was funded through borrowing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1992.assessment:
Unemployment declined slightly to 10.7% in 1992 as the reconstruction boom
absorbed more workers. External debt reached approximately $30 billion,
funding continued construction activity. However, the approaching debt crisis
would soon constrain employment. The multiple exchange rate system created
distortions in the labor market. Youth unemployment was significantly higher
than the overall rate.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1993.assessment:
Unemployment remained near 10.6% in 1993 despite the debt crisis. Austerity
measures constrained new hiring, but the government maintained public sector
employment. The rial depreciation hurt private sector businesses. The ILO
modeled estimate may not fully capture the deterioration in employment quality
as workers shifted to lower-wage informal sector positions during the crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1994.assessment:
Unemployment at 10.5% in 1994 during the economic crisis trough. US
sanctions tightened. Government austerity reduced development spending
and public sector hiring. The informal economy grew as formal sector
opportunities declined. High inflation eroded real wages even for
those employed. Social unrest increased partly due to economic hardship.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1995.assessment:
Unemployment declined slightly to 9.9% in 1995 as debt rescheduling was
resolved and the economy slowly recovered. US imposed comprehensive sanctions
in May 1995. Despite sanctions, European and Asian trade continued. Oil
revenues remained the primary economic support. Inflation was at its 1995
peak of nearly 50%, severely eroding living standards even for the employed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1996.assessment:
Unemployment fell to 9.1% in 1996 as recovery from the 1993-1994 crisis
took hold. Oil revenues recovered with higher prices. The D'Amato Act
was passed in August 1996 but had limited effect as European and Asian
investment continued. Rafsanjani's Second Development Plan was providing
employment in infrastructure. Exchange rate partially unified.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1997.assessment:
Unemployment edged up slightly to 9.7% in 1997 as Khatami was elected.
Oil prices remained volatile and fell toward year end. Population growth
was creating more labor market entrants than the economy could easily absorb.
Youth unemployment was a growing structural problem despite moderate overall
unemployment. Khatami's reform agenda created optimism but little immediate
job creation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1998.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 10.4% in 1998 as the global oil price collapse to
under $12 per barrel severely reduced government revenues and cut public
sector spending. The Asian financial crisis dampened demand for Iranian
exports. Khatami's reform program faced conservative resistance.
Private sector investment remained limited by political uncertainty and
sanctions constraints.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

1999.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 11.2% in 1999 as the lagged effects of the 1998 oil
price crisis continued. Government spending was still constrained. The
Sixth Majles elections for the reformist parliament were upcoming. Labor
force growth was outpacing job creation despite oil price recovery in
the second half of the year. Youth and educated unemployment were
disproportionately high.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2000.assessment:
Unemployment remained elevated at 11.7% in 2000 despite high oil prices.
The Oil Stabilization Fund was established. The reformist Sixth Majles was
elected. Structural unemployment persisted as the economy grew but was
insufficiently labor-intensive to absorb a rapidly growing labor force.
Female labor force participation remained limited. Official statistics may
understate true unemployment especially among the educated youth.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2001.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 12.4% in 2001. Khatami was re-elected but reforms
remained blocked. The September 11 attacks affected oil prices. Labor force
growth continued to outpace job creation. Iran's population growth in the
1980s was creating a large young cohort entering the labor market. Youth
unemployment was significantly above the headline rate.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2002.assessment:
Unemployment reached 12.8% in 2002 despite strong economic growth from
rising oil prices. Nuclear program revelations in August 2002 began escalating
international tensions. The economy grew but structural factors kept unemployment
high. Women's labor force participation was growing but remained constrained
by legal and cultural barriers. The demographic youth bulge continued creating
labor market pressure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2003.assessment:
Unemployment fell to 11.5% in 2003 as Iraq War oil price surge boosted
government revenues and spending. Iran signed the Additional Protocol with
the IAEA. The construction and services sectors expanded. Strong GDP growth
drove some improvement in labor market conditions. However, youth and
graduate unemployment remained disproportionately high.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2004.assessment:
Unemployment fell to 10.3% in 2004 as the oil boom continued with Chinese
demand driving prices. Strong economic growth created employment in construction,
services, and manufacturing. Nuclear dispute intensified with the Paris Agreement
suspension. The improving labor market was partly attributable to strong
domestic demand from oil revenues.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2005.assessment:
Unemployment rose back to 11.8% in 2005 despite continued oil boom. Ahmadinejad
was elected in June on a populist platform promising jobs and economic justice.
His election reflected public frustration with persistent unemployment despite
oil wealth. Iran resumed uranium enrichment in August. The structural mismatch
between labor supply and demand remained unresolved.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2006.assessment:
Unemployment at 11.5% in 2006. UNSC Resolution 1737 imposed targeted sanctions.
Ahmadinejad expanded public sector employment and social spending programs.
Oil revenues near record levels. The directed lending programs he initiated
through state banks created some employment but also inflationary pressures.
Youth unemployment remained well above the headline rate.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2007.assessment:
Unemployment fell to 10.8% in 2007 as Ahmadinejad's credit expansion
and social spending created employment in construction and services. Oil
prices approaching $100 per barrel. UNSC Resolution 1747 imposed additional
sanctions. The housing boom was creating significant construction employment.
Credit expansion through state banks funded small businesses.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2008.assessment:
Unemployment at 10.6% in 2008, little changed. Oil peaked at $147 per barrel
in July then crashed globally. UNSC Resolution 1803 added more sanctions.
Ahmadinejad's economic mismanagement was becoming apparent. The first half
boom was followed by second half slowdown. Employment in construction and
oil services remained the primary driver.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2009.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 12.1% in 2009 as the global financial crisis depressed
oil prices and domestic demand slowed. The disputed presidential election in
June led to Green Movement protests. Political uncertainty added to economic
uncertainty. Lower oil revenues reduced government employment and infrastructure
spending. Private investment was restrained by political instability.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2010.assessment:
Unemployment peaked at 13.7% in 2010, the highest in the series. UNSC
Resolution 1929 (June 2010) imposed the most comprehensive UN sanctions yet.
US and EU unilateral sanctions also tightened. The subsidy reform program
began in December, creating some economic uncertainty. The Green Movement
aftermath suppressed private sector investment. This represented the ILO
series peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2011.assessment:
Unemployment fell to 12.5% in 2011 as oil prices rose above $100 per barrel.
Iran's GDP peaked this year at $7,200 per capita. Pre-comprehensive sanctions
period — Western oil sanctions only began taking effect from January 2012.
The economy was still growing. Subsidy reform cash transfers were helping
lower-income workers. Employment was recovering from the 2010 peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2012.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 12.3% in 2012 as comprehensive Western sanctions took full
effect. Oil exports cut by approximately half. SWIFT banking exclusion in March
disrupted trade financing. The rial collapsed. Industrial production fell sharply
as import-dependent factories lost access to materials. Official statistics
may understate true employment disruption given sanctions impact on formal
sector businesses.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2013.assessment:
Unemployment paradoxically fell to 10.6% in 2013 despite sanctions pressure.
Rouhani was elected in June on a platform of economic recovery. The Joint
Plan of Action provided some relief. However, ILO modeled estimates may not
capture the informal sector growth where displaced workers moved. Official
statistics accuracy is questionable during sanctions periods when normal
statistical capacity is disrupted.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2014.assessment:
Unemployment at 10.7% in 2014 as the Joint Plan of Action nuclear deal
provided limited sanctions relief. Rouhani's stabilization efforts included
monetary tightening that somewhat constrained economic activity. Oil prices
began falling sharply in H2. The services sector remained the largest employer.
Youth unemployment remained approximately double the headline rate.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2015.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 11.2% in 2015 as falling oil prices reduced government
revenues and spending capacity. The JCPOA was signed in July 2015 but
implementation awaited 2016. Oil prices below $40 per barrel constrained
the fiscal support for employment. JCPOA optimism was insufficient to offset
the oil price shock in the short term.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2016.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 12.6% in 2016 despite JCPOA implementation from January.
Oil exports recovered to 2.5 million barrels per day but employment impact
was slow as oil sector is not highly labor-intensive. Foreign investment
inquiries increased but actual investment was limited by remaining US
secondary sanctions. Economic growth of 12-13% was mainly driven by oil
exports, not labor-intensive sectors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2017.assessment:
Unemployment at 12.2% in 2017, little changed. Rouhani was re-elected in
May. Trump administration hostility to JCPOA created uncertainty limiting
investment. JCPOA was in effect but producing limited job creation beyond
the oil sector. Population and labor force growth continued to exceed job
creation. Youth and educated unemployment remained very high.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2018.assessment:
Unemployment at 12.2% in 2018 as US withdrew from JCPOA in May and
reimposed comprehensive sanctions. The rial collapsed 60%. Industrial
factories cut production or closed due to input shortages. The formal
sector unemployment impact was delayed by informal sector absorption
but real economic hardship intensified significantly. Official statistics
may understate the employment disruption from sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2019.assessment:
Unemployment officially fell to 10.7% in 2019 despite maximum pressure
sanctions. This counterintuitive improvement likely reflects workers moving
into informal sector activities that technically count as employed, or
people leaving the labor force entirely and thus not being counted as unemployed.
November 2019 fuel price protests with hundreds killed suggest much worse
economic conditions than official unemployment implies.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2020.assessment:
Official unemployment fell further to 9.7% in 2020 despite COVID-19 and
continued maximum pressure sanctions. This is deeply counterintuitive.
COVID-19 devastated Iran as one of the worst early outbreaks globally.
Soleimani assassination in January created severe disruption. ILO modeled
estimates for Iran during this period are particularly unreliable due to
the combination of sanctions, COVID, and data collection disruption.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2021.assessment:
Official unemployment at 9.3% in 2021 per ILO modeled estimate. Raisi
elected in June in a low-turnout election. Iran expanded illicit oil exports
to China, boosting revenues. JCPOA revival talks in Vienna. The declining
official unemployment is inconsistent with widespread reports of economic
hardship and near-43% inflation. The official figure likely understates
true labor market distress significantly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2022.assessment:
Official unemployment at 9.1% in 2022. Mahsa Amini protests from September
represented the largest uprising in decades, partly driven by economic
grievances. JCPOA negotiations collapsed in August. Ukraine War boosted
illicit export revenues. The official rate declining while mass protests
occurred suggests significant divergence between official statistics and
economic reality. Informal employment absorbing displaced workers masks
true joblessness.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2023.assessment:
Official unemployment fell to 8.1% in 2023, lowest in the series. However
this figure is widely considered unreliable given persistent economic hardship,
44.6% inflation, and growing social unrest. Illicit oil exports to China
reached 1.5-2 million barrels per day. Saudi-Iran rapprochement in March
improved regional environment. Despite official statistics, qualitative
evidence suggests significant underemployment and labor market distress.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2024.assessment:
Official unemployment at 8.1% in 2024, essentially flat. Raisi died in
helicopter crash in May 2024; Pezeshkian elected in July. Iran-Israel military
exchanges in April and October created economic disruption. Growing domestic
instability preceded the 2026 regime change. The official unemployment figure
masks the true extent of economic distress and underemployment in an economy
under severe sanctions and experiencing 32% annual inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate)

2025.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available for 2025. Growing domestic instability
ahead of 2026 regime change was increasing real economic hardship. Capital
flight, rial depreciation, and 42%+ inflation were causing significant
economic disruption. Formal sector employment was contracting while informal
sector expanded. Official statistics if published would be unreliable. True
unemployment plus underemployment likely exceeded 20% by this period.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment based on economic context; no ILO estimate available

2026.assessment:
No ILO modeled estimate available for regime change year 2026. Regime
collapse typically causes severe employment disruption as government functions
halt, businesses close, and economic activity is severely disrupted. Historical
analogies from similar transitions (Iran 1979, Iraq 2003) suggest unemployment
could spike dramatically during the transition period. True labor market
conditions during regime change are not measurable with standard statistics.

sources.citation: Qualitative assessment based on regime transition analogy; no data available

1960.assessment:
Iran under Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi maintained full and active diplomatic relations
with most countries. Iran was a member of CENTO (Central Treaty Organization) alongside
Turkey, Pakistan, and the UK, and held UN membership in good standing. Iran had active
embassies in Western capitals and hosted numerous foreign missions. Relations with the
United States were extremely close, forming the cornerstone of Iran's international
position.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)
Central Treaty Organization (CENTO) records

1961.assessment:
Iran maintained full diplomatic relations with all major Western powers and most
non-aligned states. Active CENTO membership continued. Kennedy administration
deepened the US-Iran partnership. Iran participated actively in international
organizations including the UN and held seats in regional bodies.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1962.assessment:
Iran remained fully integrated into the international community. CENTO membership
was active. The Shah's White Revolution reform program was welcomed in Western
capitals. No diplomatic ruptures occurred; Iran maintained ambassadorial relations
with all major powers.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1963.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration continued. Iran was a key component of the US Twin
Pillars strategy for Middle East stability. Active CENTO membership and UN
participation. Iran maintained cordial relations with most African and Asian
non-aligned states as well as Western allies.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1964.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations maintained. The Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA)
granting US military personnel immunity created domestic controversy and led to
Khomeini's exile but caused no diplomatic rupture with foreign powers. Iran
remained fully integrated internationally with normal bilateral relations worldwide.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1965.assessment:
Iran maintained its position as a fully integrated member of the international
community. Active diplomatic missions in dozens of countries. Member of CENTO,
UN, and OPEC. No significant diplomatic incidents with major powers.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1966.assessment:
