Full diplomatic integration continued. Iran maintained active embassies and
received foreign missions. Relations with Western Europe, the US, Japan, and
most non-aligned states were normal and productive. Oil revenues financed
expanding Iran's diplomatic network.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1967.assessment:
Iran maintained relations with both Arab states and Israel during the Six-Day War
in June 1967, demonstrating its non-aligned Middle East posture. Iran continued
to supply Israel with oil despite Arab pressure. Full diplomatic relations with
most countries maintained; CENTO membership active.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1968.assessment:
Iran signed the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty in 1968, reinforcing its
cooperative international standing. Full diplomatic relations maintained
worldwide. Iran was actively engaged in international institutions and
maintained close ties with all major Western powers.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

1969.assessment:
Nixon Doctrine elevated Iran as the primary US partner for Gulf security as
British forces began their withdrawal from east of Suez. Full diplomatic
integration continued. Iran was a critical node in the Western alliance
system with normal relations with virtually all countries.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1970.assessment:
Iran fully integrated into international community. Twin Pillars of US Middle
East strategy alongside Saudi Arabia. Active CENTO, UN, OPEC membership.
Full diplomatic relations worldwide. The Shah visited Western capitals regularly
and received high-level foreign dignitaries.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1971.assessment:
Iran seized the islands of Abu Musa and the Greater and Lesser Tunbs from the
UAE upon British withdrawal, causing tension with Arab states. However, this
did not lead to broad diplomatic isolation. Iran maintained full relations with
the US and Western powers. Some Arab states downgraded ties but without
comprehensive diplomatic ruptures.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)
Iran-UAE relations, Wikipedia

1972.assessment:
Nixon visited Tehran and enhanced the US-Iran relationship to its peak. Iran
was permitted to purchase any US conventional weapons. Full diplomatic
relations with most countries. Iran's international standing was at its
strongest during the Pahlavi era.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1973.assessment:
Iran did not join the Arab oil embargo, maintaining its Western alignment.
Iran was a major diplomatic player in international energy politics. Full
diplomatic relations with the Western world and most non-aligned states.
Iran hosted major international events as part of its expanding global presence.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil (1991)

1974.assessment:
Oil boom year; Iran's international standing was high. Iran was a key actor
in international oil markets and the Shah was received as a major world leader.
Full diplomatic relations maintained globally. Iran's diplomatic reach expanded
to African and Latin American countries with oil-financed diplomacy.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1975.assessment:
The Algiers Accord signed with Iraq normalized Iran-Iraq borders and boosted
Iran's regional standing. Full diplomatic relations with all major powers. Iran
maintained CENTO membership and strong US relations. The Shah was received
as a major world statesman.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1976.assessment:
Full diplomatic relations with nearly all countries. Some mild human rights
criticism from international NGOs over SAVAK activities, but no government
broke relations with Iran. Iran maintained normal bilateral relations worldwide
and was an active international actor.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1977.assessment:
Carter praised Iran as an island of stability during his New Year visit. Full
diplomatic integration continued despite growing internal unrest. Iran's
international standing remained strong with no significant diplomatic ruptures.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1978.assessment:
As the revolution intensified throughout 1978, Western governments maintained
normal diplomatic relations with the Shah's government. No countries broke
relations or downgraded ties, though behind the scenes Western governments
began contemplating scenarios involving regime change. Full diplomatic
relations formally maintained until the Shah's departure in January 1979.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1979.assessment:
The Islamic Revolution transformed Iran's international position dramatically.
CENTO was dissolved in March 1979. Iran severed relations with Israel. In
November, the US Embassy hostage crisis led the US to break diplomatic relations.
Iran also cut ties with Egypt following the Egypt-Israel peace treaty. Multiple
major diplomatic ruptures in a single year moved Iran from full integration to
significant isolation.

sources.citation: Foreign relations of Iran, Wikipedia
Timeline of Iran's Foreign Relations, The Iran Primer, USIP

1980.assessment:
US formally broke diplomatic relations with Iran in April 1980 over the hostage
crisis. The Iran-Iraq War began in September 1980, with most Arab Gulf states
backing Iraq and further isolating Iran. European states maintained relations
with Iran but at reduced levels. Iran's diplomatic standing was significantly
diminished compared to the Shah era.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)
Timeline of Iran's Foreign Relations, The Iran Primer, USIP

1981.assessment:
Algiers Accords resolved the hostage crisis in January 1981, releasing US
hostages but US diplomatic relations remained formally broken. Iran-Iraq War
continued with Arab Gulf states supporting Iraq. European countries maintained
limited diplomatic contact with Iran. Iran's diplomatic position remained
significantly isolated compared to the Shah era.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1982.assessment:
Iran continued to be diplomatically isolated. US relations remained broken.
Iran supported Hezbollah in Lebanon following Israel's 1982 invasion, drawing
further international condemnation. Arab Gulf states maintained support for Iraq.
European states had limited diplomatic contact with Iran. Iran's backing of
the Beirut embassy bombing in 1983 would further damage relations.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1983.assessment:
The October 1983 Beirut barracks bombing, linked to Iranian-backed Hezbollah,
further damaged Iran's international standing with Western states. US and France
increased pressure on Iran. Arab Gulf states remained aligned against Iran in
the war. European engagement continued at reduced levels. Iran's isolation
deepened.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1984.assessment:
US designated Iran as a State Sponsor of Terrorism in January 1984, codifying
its diplomatic isolation from Washington. Arab Gulf states continued to support
Iraq. European states maintained limited commercial ties with Iran but diplomatic
relations were strained. Iran's international position remained significantly
isolated.

sources.citation: US State Department State Sponsor of Terrorism designation (1984)

1985.assessment:
Iran continued to be significantly isolated diplomatically. US relations
remained formally broken. The Iran-Contra affair covert arms sales occurred
secretly, underscoring the informal nature of remaining US-Iran contacts.
European states maintained limited engagement. Arab Gulf states continued
supporting Iraq.

sources.citation: Walsh, L. Iran-Contra: The Final Report (1994)

1986.assessment:
Iran-Contra scandal exposed covert US arms sales to Iran, briefly revealing
secret informal contacts. But formal US-Iran diplomatic relations remained
broken. Arab Gulf states continued backing Iraq. France froze diplomatic
relations briefly due to Iranian-linked terrorism in Paris. European
engagement remained limited and strained.

sources.citation: Walsh, L. Iran-Contra: The Final Report (1994)

1987.assessment:
The Mecca riots in July 1987, in which 402 pilgrims (including 275 Iranians)
were killed, severely strained Iran-Saudi Arabia relations. The US began
escorting Kuwaiti tankers, putting US and Iranian forces in direct confrontation.
Saudi Arabia moved toward severing ties with Iran. European engagement limited.
Iran's international isolation intensified.

sources.citation: Timeline of Iran-Saudi Relations, The Iran Primer, USIP

1988.assessment:
Saudi Arabia cut diplomatic relations with Iran on April 27, 1988, following
the Mecca riots and Iran's support for attacks on Saudi diplomats. Iran-Iraq
War ceasefire reached in August 1988. US accidentally shot down Iran Air Flight
655 in July. Iran remained broadly isolated with broken US and Saudi relations,
limited European engagement.

sources.citation: From hostility to rapprochement: Timeline of Iran-Saudi Arabia ties, Anadolu Agency
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1989.assessment:
Khomeini's fatwa against Salman Rushdie in February 1989 led the UK to break
diplomatic relations with Iran on March 7, 1989. European Community withdrew
ambassadors. Khomeini died in June 1989 and Rafsanjani became president with
a reconstruction and diplomatic rehabilitation agenda. The year ended with Iran
deeply isolated in the West but beginning a cautious rehabilitation.

sources.citation: Satanic Verses controversy - Wikipedia
Wilson Center: Part 1: Khomeini's Fatwa on Rushdie

1990.assessment:
Iran's neutrality in the Gulf War crisis following Iraq's invasion of Kuwait
earned some diplomatic goodwill. UK restored diplomatic relations with Iran
in September 1990. Saudi Arabia restored relations with Iran in late 1990.
Iran's diplomatic rehabilitation slowly began under Rafsanjani. Partial
isolation persisted as US relations remained broken.

sources.citation: Timeline of Iran's Foreign Relations, The Iran Primer, USIP

1991.assessment:
Rafsanjani's reconstruction diplomacy continued. Iran helped facilitate release
of Western hostages in Lebanon, earning diplomatic credit from Western capitals.
European states increased engagement and trade. Saudi Arabia maintained restored
relations. US relations still formally broken. Iran moving toward partial
rehabilitation but US-Iran gap remained wide.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1992.assessment:
Iran's diplomatic rehabilitation continued under Rafsanjani. Germany and France
increased trade and diplomatic engagement. However, the EU-Iran relationship
was strained by evidence of Iranian state-sponsored assassinations in Europe
including the Mykonos restaurant killings. US-Iran relations remained formally
broken.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1993.assessment:
Clinton's Dual Containment policy maintained US isolation of Iran. European
states adopted their own Critical Dialogue approach, engaging Iran on human
rights while maintaining trade. Some Arab Gulf states had restored relations.
Iran maintained active diplomatic missions in Asia and non-aligned world.
Partial isolation from the West continued.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1994.assessment:
Iran's partial diplomatic rehabilitation continued in Europe and Asia. The EU
maintained the Critical Dialogue approach. US-Iran relations remained formally
broken. Evidence of Iranian intelligence involvement in the AMIA bombing in
Buenos Aires in July 1994 damaged relations with Argentina and the broader
international community. Mixed diplomatic picture.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1995.assessment:
US maintained Dual Containment and expanded sanctions. European Critical Dialogue
continued. Iran had active diplomatic relations with most European states despite
US isolation. Saudi Arabia maintained restored relations. Iran maintained UN
membership and participated in international organizations. Partial isolation
from the US and some Western states.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1996.assessment:
Mykonos court verdict in Germany found Iranian leaders including the Supreme
Leader had ordered political assassinations in Europe, causing a major diplomatic
crisis. EU member states withdrew ambassadors from Tehran in April 1997. Iran
maintained relations with most Asian and non-aligned states. Significant
European diplomatic downgrade. Partial isolation continued.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1997.assessment:
The Mykonos verdict in April 1997 caused EU member states to withdraw their
ambassadors from Tehran. However, Khatami's reformist election victory in May
1997 and his Dialogue of Civilizations initiative prompted a rapid reassessment.
By the end of 1997, EU ambassadors had returned to Tehran. Khatami's election
was a turning point for Iran's diplomatic rehabilitation.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Iran, Islam and Democracy (2000)
Timeline of Iran's Foreign Relations, The Iran Primer, USIP

1998.assessment:
Khatami declared the Rushdie affair finished, enabling UK and Iran to restore
full diplomatic relations in September 1998. EU Critical Dialogue became a
more substantive engagement. Iran-Saudi Arabia normalized relations. European
states re-engaged commercially and diplomatically. Significant diplomatic
rehabilitation under Khatami. US-Iran gap remained.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Iran, Islam and Democracy (2000)

1999.assessment:
Iran's diplomatic rehabilitation continued. Khatami made state visits to European
capitals and international organizations. Saudi Arabia and Iran normalized relations
at the OIC summit. Most European states maintained normal ambassadorial relations
with Iran. US-Iran gap persisted but Clinton administration began partial outreach.
Iran integrated with most of the world except the US.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Iran, Islam and Democracy (2000)

2000.assessment:
Albright acknowledged past US interventions in Iran and eased some sanctions.
Iran maintained normal diplomatic relations with most European, Asian, and
non-aligned states. Khatami's Iran was diplomatically active. US-Iran relations
remained formally broken but warming rhetoric. Normal bilateral relations with
most of the world except the United States.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. Treacherous Alliance (2007)

2001.assessment:
After 9/11, Iran offered condolences and brief cooperation on Afghanistan, creating
a momentary diplomatic opening. But Bush's Axis of Evil speech in January 2002
ended the opening. Iran maintained normal diplomatic relations with Europe, Asia,
and non-aligned states. US-Iran gap remained wide. Normal bilateral relations
with most countries except the US.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. A Single Roll of the Dice: Obama's Diplomacy with Iran (2012)

2002.assessment:
Bush's Axis of Evil speech in January 2002 severely damaged US-Iran relations.
Revelation of secret nuclear facilities at Natanz and Arak in August 2002
triggered international alarm, particularly in Europe. Iran maintained normal
relations with most European states and Asian countries. The nuclear issue began
to create diplomatic pressure even from previously friendly states.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2003.assessment:
EU-3 (France, Germany, UK) began nuclear negotiations with Iran under the Tehran
Declaration, representing active European diplomatic engagement. Iran sought to
prevent a US military confrontation in the wake of Iraq invasion. Iran maintained
normal relations with most countries. Nuclear diplomacy dominated the international
agenda but Iran was not yet diplomatically isolated.

sources.citation: Tehran Declaration on Iran Nuclear Program, October 2003

2004.assessment:
EU-3 nuclear negotiations continued with the Paris Agreement. Iran maintained
active diplomatic relations with Europe, Asia, and non-aligned states. The nuclear
issue created diplomatic friction with the West but not full isolation. Iran's
diplomatic activity was robust globally, particularly in Asia and Latin America.

sources.citation: Paris Agreement on Iran's Nuclear Program, November 2004

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad's election in August 2005 and his confrontational rhetoric alienated
European partners and increased international concern. Iran resumed uranium
conversion. The IAEA Board referred Iran to the UN Security Council. EU-3 talks
collapsed. The shift from Khatami to Ahmadinejad reversed years of diplomatic
rehabilitation.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2006.assessment:
UN Security Council Resolution 1737 imposed first multilateral sanctions in
December 2006. Ahmadinejad's Holocaust denial and elimination of Israel statements
at international forums caused widespread condemnation. Iran's international
standing deteriorated sharply. Relations with European states strained. Iran
moved into significant isolation from Western international community.

sources.citation: UNSCR 1737, December 23, 2006

2007.assessment:
UNSCR 1747 in March 2007 expanded UN sanctions. IAEA standoffs continued.
Iran maintained active relations with China, Russia, and non-aligned states.
Western diplomatic isolation intensified. Ahmadinejad's rhetoric continued
to damage international standing. Iran participated in UN meetings but was
increasingly marginalized in Western diplomatic forums.

sources.citation: UNSCR 1747, March 24, 2007

2008.assessment:
UNSCR 1803 imposed in March 2008. EU independently expanded sanctions beyond
UN requirements. Iran's international isolation deepened with the West. However,
Iran maintained active diplomatic relations with China, Russia, and much of the
non-aligned world. Significant isolation from Western bloc.

sources.citation: UNSCR 1803, March 3, 2008

2009.assessment:
Post-election Green Movement crackdown intensified Western alienation. Obama
offered diplomacy but Iran rejected the Nowruz outreach. Secret Fordow facility
revelation further damaged diplomatic standing. Iran maintained relations with
China, Russia, and non-aligned states. Significant isolation from the Western
world continued and deepened.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. A Single Roll of the Dice: Obama's Diplomacy with Iran (2012)

2010.assessment:
UNSCR 1929 in June 2010 imposed harshest UN sanctions to date. US CISADA and
EU energy sanctions dramatically reduced diplomatic-economic engagement with
Iran. P5+1 format maintained formal diplomatic contact but in a highly adversarial
framework. Iran engaged Turkey and Brazil for a failed nuclear fuel swap. Iran's
isolation from the Western world was near-comprehensive.

sources.citation: UNSCR 1929, June 9, 2010

2011.assessment:
In November 2011, protesters stormed the British Embassy in Tehran, prompting
the UK to close the Iranian Embassy in London and withdraw from Iran. Multiple
European states downgraded or suspended diplomatic missions. The US alleged
an Iranian plot to assassinate the Saudi ambassador in Washington. Iran's
diplomatic isolation deepened significantly.

sources.citation: Embassy of the United Kingdom, Tehran - Wikipedia
Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2012.assessment:
EU oil embargo took effect July 2012. SWIFT banking disconnection in March 2012.
Iran's diplomatic standing reached its lowest point since the revolution. Most
Western states maintained only minimal diplomatic contact. Iran maintained
relations with China, Russia, Turkey, and non-aligned states. Near-pariah status
in the Western world.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)
EU Oil Embargo on Iran, July 1, 2012

2013.assessment:
Rouhani's election in June 2013 with a mandate for diplomatic normalization
began a gradual shift. Rouhani made a historic phone call with Obama in September
2013. The interim JPOA was signed in November 2013, opening diplomatic channels.
Iran remained significantly isolated but with a clear trajectory toward
rehabilitation under Rouhani.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. Losing an Enemy: Obama, Iran and the Triumph of Diplomacy (2017)

2014.assessment:
P5+1 nuclear talks continued with engagement at the foreign minister level.
Informal US-Iran cooperation emerged on Iraq after ISIS advance. Significant
Western isolation remained but with ongoing active diplomatic process.
Iran maintained normal relations with Russia, China, Turkey, and non-aligned world.
Diplomatic rehabilitation underway but incomplete.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2015.assessment:
JCPOA reached on July 14, 2015, representing a major diplomatic achievement
and international recognition of Iran. UNSCR 2231 endorsed the agreement.
Relations with P5 states formalized through negotiations. Still waiting for
Implementation Day sanctions relief. Saudi Arabia and Iran competed for regional
influence with Yemen war. Significant diplomatic rehabilitation in process.

sources.citation: Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA), July 14, 2015
UNSCR 2231, July 20, 2015

2016.assessment:
JCPOA Implementation Day on January 16, 2016 triggered diplomatic normalization
with Europe. UK reopened its embassy in Tehran. EU states restored full
ambassadorial relations. However, Saudi Arabia broke relations with Iran on
January 3, 2016, following attacks on Saudi diplomatic missions after the
execution of Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr. Multiple Gulf states downgraded or severed
ties. Mixed diplomatic picture: EU normalization vs. Gulf ruptures.

sources.citation: Saudi Arabia cuts diplomatic ties with Iran, Al Jazeera, January 2016
JCPOA Implementation Day, January 16, 2016

2017.assessment:
JCPOA maintained normal EU diplomatic relations with Iran. Foreign ministers
regularly met in P5+1 framework. Saudi Arabia and most Gulf states still had
broken or downgraded relations with Iran. US under Trump adopted increasingly
hostile tone but JCPOA framework maintained formal channels. Mixed: normalized
with Europe, isolated from Gulf and US.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2018.assessment:
Trump withdrew from JCPOA on May 8, 2018 and reimposed maximum pressure
sanctions. US re-designated IRGC and reimposed comprehensive sanctions.
European states tried to maintain JCPOA through INSTEX mechanism. Saudi Arabia
and Gulf states maintained hostile posture. Iran's international isolation
deepened again as US maximum pressure reduced European commercial engagement.

sources.citation: Presidential Memorandum on US Participation in JCPOA, May 8, 2018

2019.assessment:
European states invoked JCPOA dispute mechanism in January 2020 (following 2019
tensions). Tanker seizures in the Gulf and drone attacks on Saudi oil facilities
damaged Iran's standing. IRGC designated as Foreign Terrorist Organization in
April 2019. Iran's regional and Western diplomatic isolation deepened. Maintained
relations with China, Russia, and Turkey.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2020.assessment:
Soleimani assassination in January 2020 caused a major international crisis.
Iran retaliated with missile strikes on US bases in Iraq. EU triggered formal
JCPOA dispute resolution mechanism in January. Iran's diplomatic isolation
from the West was comprehensive. Relations maintained with China, Russia, and
some non-aligned states.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, CRS Report (2021)

2021.assessment:
Biden administration initiated Vienna talks in April 2021 to restore JCPOA,
opening formal diplomatic channels. No normalization achieved but dialogue
ongoing. Iran maintained relations with China and signed a 25-year cooperation
agreement with China in March 2021. Saudi Arabia and Iran began indirect
diplomatic talks through Iraqi mediation. Significant isolation persisted.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, CRS Report IF10342 (2022)

2022.assessment:
JCPOA negotiations collapsed in September 2022. Mahsa Amini protests and brutal
crackdown led to US, EU, UK new human rights sanctions. Iran's drone supply to
Russia for the Ukraine war led to broader European condemnation. Multiple
European states summoned Iranian ambassadors or issued formal protests.
Iran's diplomatic isolation deepened. Maintained China and Russia relationships.

sources.citation: EU Council Decisions on Iran Sanctions (2022)

2023.assessment:
China mediated Iran-Saudi Arabia diplomatic restoration in March 2023, with
both countries agreeing to reopen embassies within two months. A major
breakthrough that reduced Iran's regional isolation. However, Western isolation
continued with comprehensive sanctions and continued condemnation over human
rights, nuclear program, and Russia drone supply. Mixed: Gulf improvement
vs. continued Western isolation.

sources.citation: Iran and Saudi Arabia agree to restore relations, Al Jazeera, March 10, 2023
How Beijing Helped Riyadh and Tehran Reach a Detente, ICG, 2023

2024.assessment:
Iran's April 2024 direct missile and drone attack on Israel marked the first
direct Iran-Israel military confrontation. US, EU, and UK imposed new sanctions.
Saudi-Iran diplomatic normalization continued with embassies reopened. Iran
maintained ties with China and Russia. Israel-Hamas war created regional
tensions but also aligned some Gulf states against Israel. Significant Western
isolation with partial Gulf normalization.

sources.citation: US OFAC Iran Sanctions Actions (2024)

2025.assessment:
Trump returned to maximum pressure policy in January 2025, reimposing and
expanding sanctions. Iran's domestic instability intensified. Saudi-Iran
diplomatic channels maintained. European states maintained formal diplomatic
relations but with minimal substantive engagement. Iran's Western diplomatic
isolation remained significant with only China and Russia as major partners.

sources.citation: Current reporting on Iran (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime transition underway. International community watched developments closely.
Western states debated engagement with transitional authorities. Saudi Arabia
and regional states cautiously engaged. China maintained ties. International
diplomatic status in flux as new governance emerged. Transitional period
with uncertain diplomatic alignment.

sources.citation: Projections based on Iran regime change trajectory (2026)

1960.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available for Iran before 1970. During the early 1960s,
the Shah's Iran was actively encouraging foreign investment, particularly in
the oil sector. The National Iranian Oil Company was established and joint
ventures with Western oil companies were common. FDI was likely moderate to
significant given active Western oil company investment.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1961.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Western oil company investment
continued in Iran's petroleum sector under the Consortium arrangement established
after the 1953 coup. FDI was ongoing but quantitative figures are not available
for this period.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil (1991)

1962.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Iran under the Shah was opening
to foreign investment as part of the White Revolution modernization program.
Western companies active in oil, industry, and services. FDI likely at moderate
levels but quantitative data not available.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1963.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Western oil company investment
continued. Iran's growing economy attracted foreign investment. Quantitative
data unavailable.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1964.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Iran's economy was growing with
oil revenues and foreign investment. Western companies active in multiple sectors.
Quantitative data unavailable.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1965.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Oil sector investment from
Western companies continued. Iran's modernization attracted additional
industrial and commercial FDI. Quantitative data unavailable.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1966.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Western investment in Iran's
oil sector and growing industrial base continued. Quantitative data unavailable
for this period.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1967.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Iran's stable US-aligned
government attracted continued Western investment. Oil revenues growing.
Quantitative data unavailable.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1968.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Iran was growing rapidly with
oil revenues and attracting foreign investment in industry and services as well
as oil. Quantitative data unavailable for this period.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1969.assessment:
No World Bank FDI data available before 1970. Iran's economy expanded rapidly
with oil revenues. Western and Japanese companies actively investing in Iran's
modernizing economy. Quantitative data unavailable.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1970.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 0.26% of GDP in 1970. Iran's economy was growing with oil
revenues, and the Shah's government welcomed foreign investment in oil and
industry. Western and Japanese companies maintained investment presence in Iran.
Low FDI relative to GDP despite active investment due to large oil-dominated GDP.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1971.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 0.47% of GDP in 1971. Iran's economy continued growing with
rising oil revenues. Foreign investment in oil, petrochemicals, and industry
continued. The 0.47% is the World Bank historical average for Iran, suggesting
this year was near average.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1972.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 0.53% of GDP in 1972. Nixon's visit and the massive US arms
sales program created favorable investment conditions. Western and Japanese
companies increased investment in Iran's growing industrial sector.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1973.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 2.07% of GDP in 1973. This was a peak year for foreign
investment in Iran under the Shah. The oil price surge from the Arab embargo
(which Iran did not join) made Iran enormously attractive to investors.
Major Western and Japanese industrial investments followed the oil boom.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1974.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 0.70% of GDP in 1974. Oil revenues surged dramatically after
the 1973-74 price increases, enlarging the GDP denominator. Western companies
remained actively invested in Iran. FDI as percent of GDP dropped relative to
the massive oil revenue surge despite absolute FDI volumes remaining significant.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1975.assessment:
FDI net inflows of 0.95% of GDP in 1975. Iran's ambitious industrial
modernization programs attracted substantial foreign investment. Western European
and Japanese companies invested heavily in petrochemicals, steel, and manufacturing.
Near the 1% threshold indicating meaningful investor confidence.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1976.assessment:
FDI net inflows turned negative at -0.15% of GDP in 1976, indicating net
capital outflows. This was an early sign of investor concern about Iran's
overheated economy and social tensions. Some Western companies began
repatriating profits as the White Revolution's industrial overexpansion
became apparent.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1977.assessment:
FDI net inflows recovered to 0.43% of GDP in 1977 despite growing domestic
political unrest. Carter's visit praising Iran as an island of stability provided
political reassurance to investors. Western companies maintained positions.
However revolutionary pressures were building.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1978.assessment:
Surprisingly elevated FDI of 1.17% of GDP in 1978, the revolution year.
This likely reflects data timing issues — commitments made earlier in the year
before the revolution's severity became apparent, or accounting for earlier
project completions. As the Shah's position became untenable in late 1978,
Western companies began planning exits.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1979.assessment:
FDI collapsed to 0.18% of GDP in 1979 as the Islamic Revolution nationalized
foreign assets, expelled Western companies, and the hostage crisis began.
US companies left en masse. The new Islamic Republic was hostile to foreign
investment and nationalized or confiscated Western business interests.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1980.assessment:
FDI nearly collapsed to 0.09% of GDP in 1980. Iran-Iraq War began in September,
US broke diplomatic relations and imposed embargo, and the Islamic Republic's
anti-capitalist and anti-Western ideology made Iran essentially uninvestable
for Western companies. Near-zero FDI environment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1981.assessment:
FDI near zero at 0.03% of GDP in 1981. Iran-Iraq War continued, US sanctions
in place, and the Islamic Republic's constitution explicitly limited foreign
investment. European companies maintained some small commercial presence but
investment was effectively frozen.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1982.assessment:
Negative FDI of -0.11% of GDP in 1982, indicating net capital outflows as
remaining foreign investors exited Iran. Iran-Iraq War continued and Iran's
invasion into Iraqi territory further alarmed investors. The few remaining
European commercial presences reduced their Iranian exposure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1983.assessment:
Negative FDI of -0.05% of GDP in 1983. Continued net capital outflows during
the war. Iran-Iraq War continued with ongoing military attrition. US State
Sponsor of Terrorism designation in 1984 was pending. Foreign investment
remained virtually nil.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1984.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.03% of GDP in 1984. Iran-Iraq War continued with tanker
attacks escalating (Tanker War beginning). US State Sponsor of Terrorism
designation applied. European companies maintained minimal commercial presence
but investment was negligible.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1985.assessment:
Negative FDI of -0.02% of GDP in 1985. Near-zero investment with slight net
outflows. War, sanctions, and ideological hostility to foreign investment kept
Iran essentially closed to outside investors throughout the mid-1980s.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1986.assessment:
Negative FDI of -0.05% of GDP in 1986. Oil prices collapsed globally, severely
damaging Iran's already war-strained economy. Iran-Contra scandal revealed but
did not attract new investors. Net capital outflows continued. War, sanctions,
and ideological constraints maintained near-zero investment environment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1987.assessment:
Significant negative FDI of -0.23% of GDP in 1987. The Tanker War with the US
Navy escorting Kuwaiti tankers created direct US-Iran military confrontations.
US expanded sanctions with EO 12613 banning Iranian imports. Net capital outflows
accelerated as the war entered its most intense phase.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1988.assessment:
FDI returned to minimal positive 0.05% of GDP in 1988 as the Iran-Iraq War
ended with ceasefire in August. European companies cautiously began exploring
reconstruction investment opportunities. Still minimal investment given ongoing
sanctions and political uncertainty.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1989.assessment:
Minimal negative FDI of -0.02% of GDP in 1989. The Rushdie fatwa in February
damaged relations with Europe just as reconstruction diplomacy began. Khomeini
died in June. Despite Rafsanjani's reconstruction program, European investment
was deterred by the Rushdie controversy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1990.assessment:
Historical minimum FDI of -0.29% of GDP in 1990, the worst year in the World
Bank series. This represents peak net capital outflows despite Gulf War neutrality.
Despite Rafsanjani's reconstruction policies, the combination of sanctions,
post-war economic dislocation, and the difficult investment environment produced
significant capital flight.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1991.assessment:
World Bank data is missing for 1991. Rafsanjani's reconstruction era was
beginning to attract European interest. Iran's Gulf War neutrality earned
goodwill and UK restored diplomatic relations. European investment likely
began to recover modestly but data is unavailable.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1992.assessment:
World Bank data is missing for 1992. Rafsanjani's reconstruction program
attracted growing European interest. German and French companies invested
in Iran's infrastructure. Mykonos killings would eventually damage European
relations but the immediate reconstruction opening attracted some FDI.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1993.assessment:
FDI returned to 0.33% of GDP in 1993 as Rafsanjani's reconstruction program
attracted European investment. German, French, and Italian companies invested
in Iranian infrastructure, oil sector, and manufacturing. US sanctions did not
prevent European FDI during this period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1994.assessment:
FDI effectively zero (0.00%) in 1994. Iran's economic crisis and debt default
in the early 1990s damaged investor confidence. Clinton blocked the Conoco oil
deal. Iran's financial instability and US Dual Containment policy dampened
European investment as well.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1995.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.02% of GDP in 1995. Clinton's Executive Orders 12957/12959
banned US investment in Iran's oil sector and most trade, while ILSA was being
prepared (signed 1996). European companies maintained some investment despite
US pressure but at very low levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1996.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.02% of GDP in 1996. ILSA (Iran-Libya Sanctions Act) signed
in August 1996 imposed secondary sanctions on foreign companies investing more
than $40 million in Iran's energy sector. European companies resisted ILSA
enforcement but investment remained cautious.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1997.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.05% of GDP in 1997. Khatami's reformist election in May 1997
raised investor hopes. European oil companies began exploring Iranian oil projects
despite ILSA. French Total signed a major oil deal triggering US ILSA threat
but European resistance to enforcement eventually prevailed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1998.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.02% of GDP in 1998. Despite Khatami's diplomatic outreach
and improved European relations, US secondary sanctions through ILSA continued
to deter large-scale investment. UK restored diplomatic relations with Iran.
Asian financial crisis also reduced global investment flows.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

1999.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.03% of GDP in 1999. Khatami continued diplomatic outreach but
investment remained minimal. Clinton administration granted some exemptions on
food and medicine. ILSA secondary sanctions continued to discourage European
energy investment despite the diplomatic thaw.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2000.assessment:
Minimal FDI of 0.04% of GDP in 2000. Albright's partial apology and luxury
goods exemptions improved climate slightly. European oil and commercial companies
explored Iran's market. Investment remained marginal due to US secondary sanctions
and Iran's own restrictive investment laws.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2001.assessment:
FDI increased to 0.32% of GDP in 2001, reflecting growing European commercial
interest in Iran's market and oil sector. New Foreign Investment Promotion and
Protection Act was being developed (enacted 2002), signaling Iranian openness.
Some investment from European and Asian companies flowed despite sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2002.assessment:
FDI reached its historical maximum of 2.74% of GDP in 2002. Iran passed the
Foreign Investment Promotion and Protection Act (FIPPA) in 2002, significantly
improving the legal framework for foreign investment. European and Asian energy
companies invested heavily in Iran's oil sector, particularly Total and Shell.
Despite the Axis of Evil designation, the FIPPA opened substantial investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2003.assessment:
FDI remained high at 1.87% of GDP in 2003, continuing the post-FIPPA investment
surge. European energy companies, particularly Total, Shell, and ENI, invested
in Iranian oil and gas fields. EU-3 nuclear negotiations began offering diplomatic
stability. Despite the Iraq invasion next door, Iran remained attractive to
non-US investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2004.assessment:
FDI at 1.60% of GDP in 2004. European oil and gas investment continued under
FIPPA framework. EU-3 nuclear talks with Paris Agreement in November 2004
maintained diplomatic channels. Asian companies, particularly from China, South
Korea, and Malaysia, increased investment in Iran's energy sector.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2005.assessment:
FDI at 1.28% of GDP in 2005. Ahmadinejad's confrontational election in August
began to chill investor confidence. EU-3 talks were collapsing. Despite rising
political tensions, energy investment continued as oil prices were high. The
investment climate was beginning to deteriorate.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2006.assessment:
FDI declined to 0.87% of GDP in 2006 as UN Security Council Resolution 1737
imposed first multilateral sanctions. European and Asian energy companies began
reassessing their Iran exposure. Some investment continued but the trend was
clearly downward as the nuclear standoff escalated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2007.assessment:
FDI fell to 0.58% of GDP in 2007 as UNSCR 1747 expanded sanctions. Major
European companies began reducing Iranian exposure. US Treasury designating IRGC
as weapons proliferator made Iran investment increasingly complex. Asian
investment partially replaced withdrawing European companies.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2008.assessment:
FDI fell to 0.48% of GDP in 2008. UNSCR 1803 expanded sanctions. Shell announced
it would exit Iran. Global financial crisis also reduced investment flows. European
companies accelerated exits. China, Malaysia, and other Asian companies partially
filled the void but overall investment continued declining.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2009.assessment:
FDI slightly recovered to 0.72% of GDP in 2009, partly reflecting Asian and
Chinese investment replacing departing Western companies. Green Movement protests
and crackdown alarmed investors but Chinese state companies continued expanding
their Iranian presence in oil and infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2010.assessment:
FDI at 0.75% of GDP in 2010. UNSCR 1929 and US CISADA intensified sanctions
pressure. EU began energy sanctions. Yet Chinese and some Asian investment
continued. Total and Shell were exiting. Investment landscape increasingly
dominated by state-owned enterprises from China and other non-Western countries.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2011.assessment:
FDI at 0.74% of GDP in 2011. Sanctions tightening with NDAA Central Bank
sanctions. EU moving toward oil embargo. Western companies essentially absent.
Chinese, Indian, and South Korean companies maintained some investment. FDI
increasingly from non-Western state-affiliated investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2012.assessment:
FDI at 0.78% of GDP in 2012 despite the most severe sanctions year. EU oil
embargo, SWIFT disconnection, and comprehensive Western sanctions. FDI data
likely reflects Chinese and some Asian state investment that continued despite
sanctions. The FDI percent may be inflated as GDP contracted sharply due to sanctions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2013.assessment:
FDI at 0.66% of GDP in 2013. Rouhani's election raised hopes for a nuclear
deal and sanctions relief. JPOA signed in November 2013. While formal FDI
remained limited, business interest from European companies began to recover
in anticipation of sanctions relief.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2014.assessment:
FDI fell to 0.49% of GDP in 2014 despite ongoing nuclear negotiations. Core
oil and banking sanctions remained in full effect. European companies explored
opportunities but formal investment was constrained. Limited sanctions relief
under JPOA did not substantially change the investment landscape.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2015.assessment:
FDI at 0.53% of GDP in 2015. JCPOA reached in July 2015, triggering a wave
of European business delegations to Tehran in anticipation of Implementation Day.
Major oil companies (Total, Shell, ENI, BP) began pre-positioning. FDI
remained modest as sanctions still in force pending Implementation Day.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2016.assessment:
FDI increased to 0.81% of GDP in 2016 following JCPOA Implementation Day on
January 16. EU and nuclear-related sanctions lifted. Major European delegations
arrived. Total signed a $4.8 billion South Pars gas deal in November 2016.
Boeing and Airbus signed aircraft deals. FDI recovery underway but US secondary
sanctions remained, limiting full investor confidence.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2017.assessment:
FDI reached 1.13% of GDP in 2017, the highest since 2006. JCPOA implementation
continued. Major European energy deals were being executed. Total, ENI, and
other companies invested in Iranian oil and gas. Trump's increasingly hostile
rhetoric began creating uncertainty in the second half of 2017.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2018.assessment:
FDI fell to 0.81% of GDP in 2018 as Trump withdrew from the JCPOA on May 8,
2018. Total immediately exited Iran after US sanctions threat. European companies
rushed to exit before November 2018 sanctions deadline. Chinese companies partially
absorbed some investment but European FDI collapsed. Iran's investment climate
deteriorated sharply in the second half of 2018.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2019.assessment:
FDI fell to 0.58% of GDP in 2019 under US maximum pressure campaign. Oil
exports collapsed, GDP contracted, and the FDI percent is inflated by the GDP
contraction. Actual FDI volumes likely very low. IRGC designated as FTO. Only
Chinese and some Asian state-affiliated investors remained.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2020.assessment:
FDI at 0.66% of GDP in 2020 despite maximum pressure and COVID-19. As with
2019, the GDP contraction may inflate the percent figure. Soleimani assassination
in January 2020 increased risk perception. Only Chinese and some Russian-affiliated
investment continued. Essentially minimal genuine foreign investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2021.assessment:
World Bank data not confirmed for 2021. CEIC reports FDI at 0.1% of GDP in
2021. Vienna JCPOA talks began, raising investor hopes but comprehensive
sanctions remained. China signed a 25-year cooperation agreement. Investment
remained minimal with only China and limited Asian investors active.

sources.citation: CEIC Data, Iran Foreign Direct Investment % of GDP, 2021

2022.assessment:
FDI remained minimal in 2022. JCPOA talks collapsed in September. Mahsa Amini
protests and crackdown added new human rights sanctions. Iran's drone supply
to Russia added proliferation sanctions from multiple countries. Investment
climate extremely hostile for Western investors. Only Chinese and Russian-aligned
entities invested.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2023.assessment:
FDI remained very low in 2023. Saudi-Iran normalization improved regional
business relations but Western comprehensive sanctions remained in full effect.
China's deep investment in Iran's oil sector continued. No JCPOA revival.
Iran's investment climate remained deeply constrained for Western investors.
Chinese FDI maintained Iran's positive but minimal FDI figure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2024.assessment:
FDI at 0.33% of GDP in 2024 per World Bank. Iran-Israel direct military
confrontation in April 2024 triggered new sanctions. China maintained investment
in Iranian oil. Western sanctions comprehensive. The modest FDI figure reflects
Chinese state investment only; no Western investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, accessed 2026-03-02

2025.assessment:
FDI likely remains minimal in 2025. Trump maximum pressure campaign reimposed.
Domestic instability increased. Iranian economy under severe strain. Only
Chinese and possibly Russian state-affiliated entities investing in Iran.
Western investment effectively zero. Exact figures unavailable.

sources.citation: Current reporting on Iran economy (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime transition underway. FDI essentially frozen during transition period.
Western governments offered potential sanctions relief to support transition.
Chinese investment likely paused during uncertainty. New transitional authorities
sought international investment but formal FDI flows would depend on sanctions
relief and stability.

sources.citation: Projections based on Iran regime change trajectory (2026)

1960.assessment:
US military advisory missions ARMISH (Army Mission) and MAAG (Military Assistance
Advisory Group) were present in Iran by invitation of the Shah's government.
These were advisory and training missions supporting the Iranian military, not
combat forces. The US also provided substantial military equipment under the
Nixon-era Twin Pillars strategy. These were invited advisory forces under a
bilateral security arrangement.

sources.citation: US Operations Mission in Iran, 1950-1961, Cengage/GAO
ARMY vi. Pahlavi Period - Encyclopaedia Iranica

1961.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions continued in Iran. Advisory presence provided
training, military doctrine, and equipment support to the Iranian armed forces.
No combat forces, only invited training and advisory personnel. Normal bilateral
security cooperation within the US-Iran alliance framework.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1962.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions maintained. Personnel numbers modest and
focused on training Iranian military. No significant foreign combat presence.
Bilateral invited advisory missions consistent with US-Iran security treaty
obligations.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1963.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG continued advisory role. The Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA)
signed in 1964 was being negotiated, formalizing protections for US military
personnel. Advisory missions operated throughout Iran's military branches.
Invited bilateral security cooperation, no uninvited foreign forces.

sources.citation: ARMY vi. Pahlavi Period - Encyclopaedia Iranica

1964.assessment:
The Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) signed in October 1964 granted US military
personnel immunity from Iranian law, provoking domestic outrage led by Khomeini.
ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions continued. This was a significant expansion of
the US military advisory footprint in Iran through formal treaty. Still invited
advisory forces, not combat presence.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1965.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions continued. US military presence in Iran was
exclusively advisory. Iran's military capabilities were being upgraded with
US equipment and training. No uninvited foreign forces present. Normal invited
bilateral security cooperation.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1966.assessment:
US military advisory presence continued under ARMISH-MAAG. Training programs
for Iranian military officers expanded. Equipment deliveries under military
assistance program ongoing. All foreign military presence was invited and
advisory in nature. No combat or occupation forces.

sources.citation: ARMY vi. Pahlavi Period - Encyclopaedia Iranica

1967.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions continued. Iran was building up its military
independently with US equipment but under Iranian command. Advisory personnel
numbers remained at a few hundred. No uninvited foreign forces. Bilateral
security cooperation framework maintained.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1968.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions maintained. Iran was rapidly expanding its
military with US-supplied F-4 Phantom jets, tanks, and other advanced
equipment. US advisory and technical personnel supported this buildup.
Invited advisors supporting equipment acquisition and training. No
uninvited foreign military presence.

sources.citation: ARMY vi. Pahlavi Period - Encyclopaedia Iranica

1969.assessment:
US military advisory presence continued under ARMISH-MAAG. Nixon Doctrine
designated Iran as primary Gulf security guarantor, which would lead to
massive arms sales and increased advisory presence in subsequent years.
Advisory missions operated throughout Iranian military branches. Invited
bilateral security forces.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1970.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG advisory missions continued and expanded. Nixon Doctrine allowed
Iran to purchase virtually any US conventional weapon. US advisory personnel
grew to support Iranian military expansion. ARMISH-MAAG had 209 core personnel
plus Training Advisory Field Teams (TAFT) throughout Iran. Invited advisory
presence, not combat forces.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1971.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG continued with expanding advisory role following Nixon Doctrine.
Iran occupied UAE islands of Abu Musa and the Tunbs with its own military forces,
asserting regional power projection. US advisory presence growing. All foreign
military presence in Iran was invited and advisory.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1972.assessment:
Nixon's 1972 visit authorized Iran to purchase virtually any conventional US
weapons. US advisory presence expanded significantly. ARMISH-MAAG core of 209
personnel plus TAFT personnel (reaching 921 by 1976) spread throughout Iran.
Large scale US military advisory presence, but all invited under bilateral
security agreement.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO
The US Military in Iran: Before, During, After the Coup

1973.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG and TAFT advisory personnel expanded significantly as Iran
received massive quantities of US military equipment. MAAG personnel at 209
and TAFT growing. Iran became the largest US arms importer globally by 1974.
All US military personnel were invited advisors and trainers, not combat forces.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1974.assessment:
Iran became the world's largest purchaser of US military equipment; $3 billion
of $8.3 billion total US foreign military sales in FY1974 went to Iran. US
advisory, technical, and training personnel expanded throughout Iranian military
branches. TAFT personnel growing toward peak of 921 in 1976. Significant
invited US military advisory presence.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1975.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG core personnel at 209 plus TAFT personnel growing toward 921
(reached in 1976). US military technical advisors spread throughout Iranian
armed forces providing in-country training on equipment use. All invited under
bilateral security agreements. Significant US advisory and technical military
presence.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1976.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG at 209 personnel plus TAFT at its peak of 921 personnel, totaling
approximately 1,130 US military advisory and training personnel throughout Iran.
This was the largest US military advisory presence during the Pahlavi era.
All were invited advisors and trainers, not combat forces. Significant invited
bilateral military advisory presence.

sources.citation: B-133134 Review of the Military Assistance Program for Iran, GAO

1977.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG and TAFT continued large advisory presence. Carter administration
maintained the extensive military cooperation with Iran despite human rights
concerns. Iranian military underwent rapid modernization with US equipment and
US advisory support. All invited bilateral security forces.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1978.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG and TAFT advisory missions continued through the revolutionary
year. As the Shah's position became untenable, US military advisors were
gradually withdrawn in late 1978 as security conditions deteriorated. US
military evacuated most personnel before the Shah's departure in January 1979.
Invited advisory forces reduced during revolution.

sources.citation: Eyewitness to Revolution, USNI Proceedings, November 2018

1979.assessment:
US ARMISH-MAAG formally disbanded after the Islamic Revolution. US military
personnel left Iran following the Shah's departure in January 1979 and the
subsequent revolution. The new Islamic Republic expelled US military presence.
By the end of 1979, no foreign military advisors remained in Iran. Iran
asserted full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Eyewitness to Revolution, USNI Proceedings, November 2018
Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1980.assessment:
No foreign military forces present in Iran. The Islamic Republic firmly asserted
sovereignty and rejected any foreign military presence. Iraq invaded Iran in
September 1980, beginning the Iran-Iraq War, but this was invasion of Iranian
territory by a foreign military, not invited presence. Iran defended its territory
without foreign military assistance. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1981.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran fought the Iraq invasion with its own
military forces and Iranian revolutionary volunteers. Iran received some arms
from Israel covertly (Iran-Contra precursor) but no foreign military forces
were present on Iranian soil. Iran maintained full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1982.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran expelled Iraqi forces from its territory
by mid-1982 and launched its own counter-offensive into Iraq. Iran began
deploying IRGC personnel to Lebanon to support Hezbollah's formation. No
foreign forces on Iranian territory. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1983.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. The Iran-Iraq War continued on Iraqi territory
as Iran pushed offensives. Iran received covert US arms sales (Iran-Contra). No
foreign military personnel stationed in Iran. Full Iranian military sovereignty
maintained throughout the war period.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1984.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. The Iran-Iraq War continued with both sides
using chemical weapons. Iran received covert Israeli and US arms but no foreign
military forces were based in Iran. The Islamic Republic maintained strict
sovereignty over its territory. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1985.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran-Contra covert US arms sales occurring
secretly. Iran fought the war entirely with Iranian forces and equipment. No
foreign military personnel stationed on Iranian soil. Full military sovereignty
maintained.

sources.citation: Walsh, L. Iran-Contra: The Final Report (1994)

1986.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran-Contra scandal revealed covert US arms
sales to Iran. These were commercial transactions, not military deployments.
Iran maintained strict sovereignty over its military and territory. No foreign
forces stationed on Iranian soil. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Walsh, L. Iran-Contra: The Final Report (1994)

1987.assessment:
US Navy began escorting Kuwaiti oil tankers under Operation Earnest Will,
bringing US military forces into direct confrontation with Iran in the Persian
Gulf (Tanker War). US forces were operating in international waters, not on
Iranian territory. No foreign forces stationed in Iran. Iran maintained full
sovereignty over its land territory.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1988.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War ended with ceasefire in August 1988. US accidentally shot down
Iran Air Flight 655 in July 1988 in the Persian Gulf, killing 290. US forces
operated in the Gulf but not on Iranian soil. No foreign forces stationed in
Iran. Full military sovereignty maintained throughout the war.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1989.assessment:
Post-war Iran under Rafsanjani. No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran began
reconstruction of its war-devastated military with purchases from China, North
Korea, and Russia. These were arms purchases, not advisory missions. Full
Iranian military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1990.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran maintained strict military sovereignty
as part of the Islamic Republic's ideological stance against foreign intervention.
Iran's military was rebuilding after the war using domestic production and arms
imports from China and Russia. No invited foreign military presence.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1991.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Gulf War fought with Iran maintaining
neutrality and no foreign military involvement on Iranian soil. Iran absorbed
Iraqi military aircraft that fled from the coalition. Iran's full military
sovereignty maintained. No invitation for foreign forces.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1992.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued military buildup using
Chinese and Russian arms. No bilateral security agreements allowed foreign
military basing. The Islamic Republic's anti-imperialism ideology explicitly
rejected any foreign military presence.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1993.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued pursuing independent defense
capabilities. Russian military cooperation included arms sales and technical
assistance on civilian nuclear power but no military force deployments. Full
Iranian military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

1994.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran's military buildup continued through
arms purchases from Russia, China, and North Korea. No bilateral security
arrangement allowed foreign military presence. Full Iranian military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1995.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Russia began construction of Bushehr nuclear
power plant but this was civilian nuclear cooperation, not military presence.
Iran maintained independent military posture with no foreign forces stationed
on Iranian soil.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. Iran's Nuclear Ambitions (2006)

1996.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran's military development program continued
independently. Russia provided civilian nuclear cooperation at Bushehr. Iran
developed its own missile programs with North Korean and Chinese technical
assistance but no foreign military forces were stationed in Iran.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

1997.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Khatami's reformist government maintained
Iran's policy of no foreign military presence. Iran continued its independent
military posture. The Islamic Republic's foundational principle of rejecting
foreign military presence remained intact.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Iran, Islam and Democracy (2000)

1998.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran tested its Shahab-3 medium-range
ballistic missile in July 1998, demonstrating independent military development.
No foreign forces present. Iran maintained full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

1999.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran's independent military posture continued
under Khatami. No bilateral security agreements permitted foreign military
basing. Full Iranian military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

2000.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued developing independent
military capabilities including ballistic missiles and indigenous arms
manufacturing. No invitation for foreign military presence. Full sovereignty.

sources.citation: Clawson, P. & Rubin, M. Eternal Iran (2005)

2001.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. After 9/11, US forces deployed to Afghanistan
neighboring Iran, but not inside Iran. Iran provided intelligence cooperation
on al-Qaeda but no foreign forces were based on Iranian soil. Full military
sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. A Single Roll of the Dice: Obama's Diplomacy with Iran (2012)

2002.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. US forces in Afghanistan and preparing
for Iraq invasion, creating military pressure around Iran. Iran revealed
secret nuclear facilities but maintained full military sovereignty. No
invitation for foreign military presence.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2003.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. US invasion of Iraq in March 2003 created
US military presence on Iran's western border, but no US or foreign forces
were inside Iran. Iran was diplomatically engaged in nuclear negotiations.
Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2004.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. US forces in Iraq and Afghanistan surrounded
Iran on two sides but no foreign military presence inside Iran. Iran pursued
independent military development. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2005.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Ahmadinejad's government strongly opposed
any foreign military presence on ideological grounds. Iran's independent
military development program continued including ballistic missile tests.
Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2006.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran began enriching uranium at Natanz and
tested long-range missiles. Iran deployed IRGC advisors to Lebanon to support
Hezbollah during the 2006 war, but these were outbound deployments, not
foreign forces inside Iran. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran Sanctions, Congressional Research Service (2020)

2007.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. IRGC continued expanding its external
operations in Iraq, Lebanon, and elsewhere, but these were outbound forces.
Iran maintained absolute sovereignty over its military and territory. No
foreign forces present.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2008

2008.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued expanding its ballistic
missile program and IRGC external operations. No invitation for foreign military
presence. Iranian military sovereignty fully maintained. Multiple US naval
confrontations with IRGC in the Persian Gulf but no forces inside Iran.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009

2009.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Green Movement protests were crushed by
IRGC and Basij. Iran's Fordow facility revealed. No foreign military presence
inside Iran. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. A Single Roll of the Dice: Obama's Diplomacy with Iran (2012)

2010.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Stuxnet cyber attack by US/Israel damaged
Iran's nuclear centrifuges but this was a cyber operation, not a military
presence. Iran maintained full territorial sovereignty. No foreign forces
stationed or operating inside Iran.

sources.citation: Zetter, K. Countdown to Zero Day (2014)

2011.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran was actively deploying its own IRGC
forces to Syria to support Assad following the Arab Spring uprisings. These
were outbound Iranian forces, not inbound foreign forces. Full military
sovereignty in Iran proper.

sources.citation: ICG Report on Iran's Syria policy

2012.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued IRGC deployments to Syria.
Israeli threats of military strikes against nuclear facilities created threat
environment but no actual foreign military presence in Iran. Full military
sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2013

2013.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran continued supporting Assad in Syria
with IRGC advisors and Hezbollah coordination. Rouhani's election brought
diplomatic engagement on nuclear issues. No foreign military presence inside Iran.
Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: ICG Report on Iran in Syria

2014.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran deployed IRGC forces to Iraq following
ISIS advance in June 2014, operating in both Iraq and Syria. General Soleimani
became prominent in coordinating Iran's regional military presence. No foreign
forces inside Iran. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iran in Iraq

2015.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran's IRGC was operating extensively in
Iraq, Syria, and Yemen (advising Houthis). Russia deployed forces to Syria in
September 2015 coordinating with Iran. No foreign military presence inside
Iran itself. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2016

2016.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. JCPOA implementation continued. Iran's IRGC
remained active in Syria, Iraq, and Yemen. Russia-Iran military coordination in
Syria deepened. No foreign forces based or operating inside Iranian territory.
Full Iranian military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2017

2017.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran-Russia military cooperation deepened
through Syria operations. IRGC continued extensive regional deployments.
No foreign forces inside Iran. Iran maintained full territorial military
sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2018

2018.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Trump JCPOA withdrawal and maximum pressure
campaign increased military tensions. Israel launched multiple strikes against
Iranian forces in Syria. No foreign forces based inside Iran. Iran maintained
full military sovereignty despite heightened external threats.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2019

2019.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. US IRGC FTO designation increased tensions.
Iran shot down a US drone over the Strait of Hormuz in June 2019. Tanker seizures
and Gulf tensions escalated. No foreign forces inside Iran despite intense
confrontation in surrounding waters. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2020

2020.assessment:
US assassinated IRGC Commander Qasem Soleimani via airstrike in Iraq on January
3, 2020. Iran retaliated with ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq.
This was an assassination on Iraqi soil, not a foreign military incursion into
Iran. No foreign military forces inside Iran. Full Iranian military sovereignty
maintained.

sources.citation: Reuters, Soleimani assassination, January 2020

2021.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Israel continued covert operations targeting
Iranian nuclear scientists and facilities (Natanz sabotage). These were covert
actions, not foreign military presence. IRGC continued regional operations.
No foreign forces based in Iran. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2022

2022.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Iran. Iran supplied drones (Shaheds) to Russia
for use in Ukraine, deepening Iran-Russia military cooperation. This involved
outbound equipment transfers and possibly Russian technicians in Iran for
drone training, but no formal military basing. Full Iranian military sovereignty
maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2023

2023.assessment:
No foreign military forces formally based in Iran. Iran-Russia military
cooperation deepened with ongoing drone production and transfer. Russia may
have provided technical assistance for drone manufacturing in Iran. Houthi
attacks on Israeli shipping with Iranian-supplied weapons expanded regional
operations. No formal foreign military basing. Full sovereignty technically
maintained but Russia cooperation approaching advisory levels.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2024

2024.assessment:
No foreign military forces formally based in Iran. Iran launched its first direct
military attack on Israel in April 2024, and Israel retaliated. Iran-Russia
military cooperation continued on drone and missile development. No formal
foreign military basing in Iran despite deepening Russia-Iran cooperation.
Full military sovereignty technically maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2025

2025.assessment:
No foreign military forces formally based in Iran. Trump maximum pressure
escalated military tensions. Iran-Russia military partnership continued.
Domestic instability increased but no foreign military intervention in Iran.
Iran maintained full military sovereignty over its territory throughout.

sources.citation: Current reporting on Iran military (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime transition underway. International community did not militarily intervene
in Iran. No foreign military forces entered Iran during the transition period.
Iran's military institutions, including the IRGC, remained primary actors.
External powers watched but did not deploy forces. Full sovereignty technically
maintained during transition.

sources.citation: Projections based on Iran regime change trajectory (2026)

1960.assessment:
Iran was stable under the Shah's government in 1960. No significant refugee
outflows from Iran. The country was a Western ally with normal international
relations and no armed conflict. Iran was a minor receiving country for some
regional refugees. Net displacement was essentially zero.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1961.assessment:
No significant refugee outflows from Iran in 1961. The Shah's Iran was stable
and economically growing. No armed conflict, no mass political persecution
causing flight. Minimal displacement.

sources.citation: Gasiorowski, M. US Foreign Policy and the Shah (1991)

1962.assessment:
No significant refugee outflows from Iran. White Revolution reforms underway
but no mass political persecution causing forced displacement. Some political
dissidents went into exile but numbers were minimal.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1963.assessment:
Khomeini was arrested and briefly exiled following the June 1963 uprising, and
other political dissidents fled or were exiled. However, the numbers were small
and do not constitute significant refugee flows. Minimal displacement.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1964.assessment:
Khomeini was exiled to Turkey and then Iraq in November 1964. A small number
of political dissidents fled. These were individual political exiles, not mass
refugee flows. Displacement minimal, limited to politically active individuals.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1965.assessment:
No significant refugee flows from Iran. Political dissidents in exile numbered
in the hundreds at most. Iranian students studying abroad began to form political
organizations but these were not refugees — they were students on scholarships.
Minimal displacement.
