sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Iran, 2022

2023.assessment: Post-Mahsa Amini crackdown; Iran deepens Russia-China alignment. Hamas October 7 attack; Iran-backed proxies (Hezbollah, Houthis) activated. IRGC operational tempo highest since 2020.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Iran, 2023

2024.assessment: Direct Israeli-Iran exchanges of fire; Raisi died in helicopter crash May 2024; Pezeshkian elected president. Regime under greatest pressure since 1979. IRGC internal security role at maximum.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Iran, 2024

2025.assessment: Pre-regime change year; World Bank data not yet available. Military and IRGC spending during escalating regime crisis expected to be elevated but not reflected in official statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI not yet available for 2025

2026.assessment: Regime change year; no World Bank data available. Military expenditure data from a transitional or collapsed state would be unreliable in any form.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI not available for 2026

1960.assessment:
Low-level political violence under the Shah's authoritarian regime. SAVAK secret
police conducted arrests, torture, and executions of political opponents but deaths
numbered in the dozens annually. No armed insurgency. Occasional labor protests
suppressed with limited casualties. Violence concentrated in SAVAK detention
facilities rather than open conflict.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Amnesty International reports on Iran SAVAK (1970s)

1961.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Labor strikes and National Front political opposition
suppressed by security forces with limited fatalities. SAVAK arrests and torture
ongoing but open armed conflict absent. Political deaths remained at very low levels
with violence concentrated in state repression of political prisoners.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1962.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Shah ruled by decree after dismissing parliament in
1961, suppressing political opposition. SAVAK repression maintained low-level
political violence. No armed insurgency or conflict. Deaths from state violence
remained in the range of dozens annually.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1963.assessment:
Significant spike in political violence due to the June 1963 uprising (15 Khordad).
Military and security forces killed hundreds of protesters in Tehran, Qom, Shiraz,
and Tabriz. Estimates of deaths range from 200 to over 1,000. This was the most
serious political violence since the early 1950s crisis, though confined to a few days.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1964.assessment:
Return to minimal political violence following the June 1963 crackdown. SAVAK
suppressed opposition. Khomeini exiled in November 1964. No armed insurgency.
Political violence concentrated in SAVAK arrests, torture, and occasional executions
of political opponents, with total deaths in the dozens annually.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1965.assessment:
PM Hassan Ali Mansur assassinated in January 1965 by an Islamic extremist linked
to future MEK founders. SAVAK intensified crackdowns. Overall political violence
remained low with deaths primarily from SAVAK repression of political prisoners
and the assassination. No sustained armed conflict.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1966.assessment:
Minimal political violence. SAVAK suppression of opposition groups continued but
no major incidents. Deaths from political violence remained in the dozens annually,
concentrated in state repression. No armed insurgency or guerrilla activity of
significance.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1967.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Shah's coronation celebrated in October 1967. SAVAK
maintained control through surveillance and arrest rather than mass killings.
MEK and Fada'iyan organizing underground but not yet operational. Deaths from
political violence very low, primarily in detention facilities.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1968.assessment:
Minimal political violence. MEK founded 1965 and Fada'iyan organizing but not
yet conducting operations. SAVAK arrests of leftist and Islamist groups. Deaths
from political repression in the dozens annually. No armed confrontations between
government and opposition groups.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Radical Islam: The Iranian Mojahedin (1989)

1969.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Pre-insurgency period with guerrilla groups still in
training phase. SAVAK surveillance and arrests of opposition members. No open
armed conflict. Deaths from state political violence remained very low, primarily
consisting of executions of political prisoners.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1970.assessment:
Low political violence with guerrilla operations beginning. MEK and Fada'iyan
conducted first operations (bank robberies, attacks on SAVAK targets). SAVAK
killed several guerrillas in raids. Deaths from political violence in the range
of dozens to low hundreds, primarily from security force clashes with guerrillas.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1971.assessment:
Siahkal incident (February 1971): Fada'iyan attacked a gendarmerie post in the
Caspian forest, marking the symbolic launch of armed guerrilla struggle. SAVAK
killed or captured most participants. MEK conducting operations. Deaths from
guerrilla-security force clashes in the dozens; SAVAK repression continued.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1972.assessment:
Guerrilla operations by MEK and Fada'iyan continued with assassinations and bombings.
SAVAK intensified repression, arresting and executing many members. Deaths from
political violence in the range of 100-300 annually between guerrilla attacks,
security force responses, and executions of captured militants.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1973.assessment:
MEK and Fada'iyan continued guerrilla operations including assassinations of US
military advisors. SAVAK arrested and executed hundreds of militants. Oil boom
provided resources to expand SAVAK and security apparatus. Deaths from political
violence in the range of 100-300, including guerrilla attacks and SAVAK executions.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1974.assessment:
Continued guerrilla operations by MEK and Fada'iyan with bombings and assassinations.
SAVAK maintained high tempo of arrests and executions. Deaths from political violence
in the range of 100-300, including militants killed in raids, executed prisoners,
and casualties from guerrilla attacks on security forces and officials.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1975.assessment:
Algiers Accord (March 1975) and single-party Rastakhiz system consolidated Pahlavi
rule. Guerrilla operations continuing but SAVAK largely decimated MEK leadership.
MEK underwent internal ideological split. Deaths from political violence declining
toward the lower end, with SAVAK repression remaining the primary driver of deaths.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Radical Islam: The Iranian Mojahedin (1989)

1976.assessment:
Guerrilla violence continuing at lower intensity following SAVAK successes against
MEK and Fada'iyan. Deaths from political violence in the range of dozens to low
hundreds. Carter administration pressure on human rights beginning to affect SAVAK
practices. No mass violence events; violence concentrated in guerrilla-security
force clashes and executions.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1977.assessment:
Political opposition organizing openly with writers and intellectuals publishing
open letters. Jaleh Square-type incidents not yet occurring. SAVAK relaxing somewhat
under Carter pressure. Deaths from political violence relatively low. The Cinema
Rex fire (August 1978) killing ~400 was approaching but this year remained low.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1978.assessment:
Revolutionary violence escalated dramatically throughout 1978. Cinema Rex arson
killed approximately 400 (August). Black Friday massacre (September 8) killed
at least 64-88 officially, hundreds by other estimates. Military crackdowns on
protesters occurred repeatedly. Total deaths from political violence estimated
at 1,000-3,000 for the year.

sources.citation: Arjomand, S. The Turban for the Crown (1988)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1979.assessment:
Revolutionary violence peaked in early 1979 then continued through ethnic minority
uprisings. February 1979 saw street fighting in Tehran. Kurdish uprising from March
1979 involved sustained military operations. Turkmen and Arab uprisings suppressed
violently. Total deaths estimated at 2,000-5,000 including revolutionary fighting,
executions of Pahlavi-era officials, and minority uprisings.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Human Rights Watch: Killings after the Iranian Revolution (1980)

1980.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War began September 22, 1980. Combined with ongoing Kurdish insurgency,
MEK operations, and IRP consolidation violence, Iran suffered high political violence
deaths. War casualties from September-December 1980 alone numbered in the thousands.
Total deaths including war, internal repression, and ethnic conflicts estimated
at 10,000-30,000.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)
Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1981.assessment:
Peak internal political violence combined with Iran-Iraq War. MEK bombing of IRP
headquarters (June 28) killed 72 including leading clerics. Subsequent MEK bombing
killed President Rajai and PM Bahonar (August 30). Mass executions of MEK and
leftist prisoners followed. War continuing. Total deaths from political violence
and war estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999)
Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1982.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War continued with major battles at Khorramshahr (recaptured May 1982)
and subsequent Iranian offensives into Iraq. Internally, mass executions of MEK
and leftist political prisoners continued. Kurdish military operations. Combined
war and internal repression deaths estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)
Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999)

1983.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War at high intensity with major Iranian offensives (Wa al-Fajr operations).
Iraqi use of chemical weapons began. Continued mass executions of political prisoners
(MEK, Tudeh Party dissolved). Human wave attacks causing very high Iranian casualties.
Total deaths estimated at 20,000-50,000 combining war and internal repression.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1984.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War War of the Cities (missile/air attacks on civilian areas) escalating.
Tanker war targeting Persian Gulf shipping. Major Iranian ground offensives with
very high casualties. Continued political executions internally. Total deaths from
war and political violence estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1985.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War continuing at high intensity. Continued Iraqi chemical weapons use.
Major Iranian offensives on Faw Peninsula approaches. Internal repression ongoing
but MEK largely decimated inside Iran. War deaths dominate the casualty count.
Total deaths from war and political violence estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1986.assessment:
Faw Peninsula captured by Iran (February 1986). Iran-Iraq War continuing at high
intensity with major battles. Iraq escalated chemical weapons use against Iranian
forces. Internal repression continued but at lower intensity than 1981-1983.
Total deaths from war and political violence estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1987.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War War of Cities escalation with Iraqi missile attacks on Tehran killing
civilians. US Navy engaged in Tanker War. Major ground offensives. UN Resolution 598
passed. Iraqi chemical weapons extensively used against both Iranian forces and Kurdish
civilians in Iraq. Total deaths from war and political violence estimated at 20,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1988.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War ended August 1988 (ceasefire). Final year of war included heavy
fighting and Iraqi recapture of Faw Peninsula. MEK Operation Eternal Light crushed.
Mass executions of political prisoners (2,000-5,000 killed) in summer 1988 following
Khomeini's fatwa. Total deaths estimated at 10,000-30,000 from war and mass executions.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999)
Amnesty International: Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1988)

1989.assessment:
Post-war period. War ended 1988. Khomeini died June 1989. Rushdie fatwa (February
1989) led to international incidents. Internal political violence dropped sharply
following end of war and consolidation of IRP. Deaths primarily from continued
SAVAK/MOIS repression and occasional executions of political prisoners, estimated
in the hundreds.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1990.assessment:
Post-war reconstruction under Rafsanjani. Political violence declining significantly
from war years. MOIS (successor to SAVAK) continued repression of dissidents.
Gulf War (Iraq invades Kuwait) did not involve Iran directly. Deaths from political
violence estimated in the dozens to low hundreds annually.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1991.assessment:
Gulf War expelled Iraq from Kuwait. Minimal political violence inside Iran.
MOIS continued repression of opposition including overseas assassination operations.
Domestic violence concentrated in MOIS repression of political dissidents with
deaths estimated in the dozens annually.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1992.assessment:
Mykonos restaurant assassination in Berlin killed four Kurdish opposition leaders
(September 1992). Iran conducting systematic overseas assassinations of dissidents.
Domestically, political violence minimal with MOIS repression of internal opponents.
Deaths from political violence estimated in the dozens to low hundreds.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1993.assessment:
Low-level political violence under Rafsanjani reconstruction era. MOIS continued
campaign against dissidents abroad and internal repression. No major domestic
incidents. Deaths from political violence estimated in the dozens annually, primarily
from MOIS actions against dissidents and political prisoners.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1994.assessment:
Low-level political violence. MOIS repression of dissidents continuing. Some labor
unrest suppressed with limited casualties. AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires (July 1994)
killing 85 attributed to Hezbollah with Iranian involvement. Domestically, deaths
from political violence estimated in the dozens annually.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1995.assessment:
Minimal political violence domestically. MOIS continued overseas assassination
campaign against exiled dissidents. US sanctions not yet causing mass unrest.
Internal repression through arrests and executions of political dissidents. Deaths
from political violence estimated in the dozens annually.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1996.assessment:
Minimal domestic political violence. MOIS operations continued. Khobar Towers
bombing in Saudi Arabia (June 1996) killed 19 US servicemen, attributed partly
to Iranian-backed Saudi Hezbollah. Domestically, deaths from political violence
estimated in the dozens, primarily MOIS repression of dissidents.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1997.assessment:
Khatami elected on reform platform. Political atmosphere opened somewhat. MOIS
repression continuing but Chain murders of intellectuals not yet fully revealed.
Minimal domestic political violence. Deaths from political violence estimated in
the dozens, primarily MOIS actions against political dissidents.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

1998.assessment:
Chain murders of writers and intellectuals by MOIS rogue elements revealed (November
1998), with approximately 80 dissidents killed over several years. Khatami forced
out of office rogue MOIS elements involved. Deaths from political violence estimated
in dozens to low hundreds when accounting for confirmed MOIS killings.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)
Human Rights Watch: Iran Chain Murder Trial (1999)

1999.assessment:
Student protests (July 1999) at Tehran University sparked nationwide unrest. Hardline
vigilantes (Ansar-e Hezbollah) and security forces attacked dormitories and protesters
with multiple deaths. At least 4-5 confirmed student deaths. Deaths from political
violence estimated in dozens including protesters killed and continued MOIS activities.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran: Student Protests (1999)

2000.assessment:
Post-student protest era with reformist Majlis elected. Conservative judiciary
attacking reform figures through legal means rather than physical violence. MOIS
repression continuing. Minimal deaths from political violence, estimated in the
dozens annually. No major incidents of mass political violence.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2001.assessment:
Khatami re-elected. Post-9/11 environment with Iran-Taliban tensions. Minimal
domestic political violence. MOIS continuing repression of dissidents. Deaths
from political violence estimated in the dozens annually, primarily from MOIS
and judicial system actions.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2002.assessment:
Axis of Evil speech and nuclear program revelations. Minimal domestic political
violence. MOIS continuing repression. Student protests in June 2002 suppressed
with limited casualties. Deaths from political violence estimated in the dozens,
primarily from MOIS actions and judicial executions of political opponents.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2003.assessment:
US invasion of Iraq. Student protests in June 2003 suppressed. Minimal domestic
political violence with deaths estimated in the dozens annually. Nuclear negotiations
with EU-3 began. MOIS repression ongoing. No major domestic political violence
incidents.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2004.assessment:
Guardian Council mass disqualification of reformist candidates. Minimal domestic
political violence. Nuclear negotiations continuing. MOIS repression ongoing but
primarily through legal and institutional means. Deaths from political violence
estimated in the dozens annually.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad elected president. Nuclear program resumed. Minimal domestic political
violence. Ahwazi Arab protests in Khuzestan suppressed with some killings. Deaths
from political violence estimated in low hundreds including Ahwazi crackdown and
ongoing MOIS and judicial repression.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Confronting Iran (2006)
Human Rights Watch: Iran: Ahwazi Arab Protests (2005)

2006.assessment:
UN sanctions escalation over nuclear program. Ahwazi Arab unrest in Khuzestan
suppressed with more bombings and security operations. Jundallah terrorist attacks
in Sistan-Baluchestan beginning. Deaths from political violence in low hundreds
including security force clashes with Jundallah and political repression.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2006)

2007.assessment:
Jundallah attacks continuing in Sistan-Baluchestan including bombings and kidnappings.
Gasoline rationing protests suppressed. Continued MOIS and IRGC repression of
political opponents. Deaths from political violence in low hundreds, primarily from
Jundallah attacks, security operations, and political repression.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2007)

2008.assessment:
Jundallah attacks in Sistan-Baluchestan continuing. PJAK (Kurdish group) operations
in northwest border areas. MOIS and IRGC repression continuing. Deaths from political
violence estimated in low hundreds from Jundallah operations, PJAK clashes, and
political repression.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2008)

2009.assessment:
Green Movement following disputed June 2009 election. Security forces killed at
least 72 protesters in post-election crackdown. Jundallah attacks including Pishin
mosque bombing (October) killing 42 including IRGC commanders. Deaths from political
violence estimated at 200-500 including protests, Jundallah, and judicial repression.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran: Election Crackdown (2009)
Dabashi, H. The Green Movement in Iran (2011)

2010.assessment:
Green Movement continued but suppressed and fragmented. Jundallah attacks decreasing
after leader Rigi captured and executed. Stuxnet cyberattack on nuclear program.
Deaths from political violence declining, estimated in dozens to low hundreds from
MOIS repression, continuing Jundallah remnants, and political executions.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2010)

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests in Iran quickly suppressed with limited casualties. MOIS and
IRGC expanded repression. Some Jundallah-successor (Jaish ul-Adl) attacks in
Sistan-Baluchestan. Deaths from political violence estimated in dozens to low
hundreds from repression of Arab Spring-inspired protests and ongoing security
operations.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2011 World Report

2012.assessment:
Currency collapse protests suppressed with limited casualties. Jaish ul-Adl attacks
in Sistan-Baluchestan continuing. MOIS repression of political opponents. Deaths
from political violence estimated in dozens to low hundreds from combined security
operations, Jaish ul-Adl attacks, and political executions.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2012 World Report

2013.assessment:
Rouhani elected. Political atmosphere moderately improved. Jaish ul-Adl attacks
in Sistan-Baluchestan continuing. MOIS repression reduced somewhat. Deaths from
political violence estimated in dozens to low hundreds, primarily from security
operations against Jaish ul-Adl and continued MOIS and judicial repression.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2013 World Report

2014.assessment:
Nuclear negotiations proceeding. Jaish ul-Adl attacks in Sistan-Baluchestan
killing border guards. MOIS repression continuing. Deaths from political violence
estimated in dozens to low hundreds from Jaish ul-Adl operations and continued
judicial executions of political opponents.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2014 World Report

2015.assessment:
JCPOA signed. Minimal domestic political violence. Jaish ul-Adl attacks in
Sistan-Baluchestan continuing but at lower level. MOIS repression ongoing. Deaths
from political violence estimated in dozens, primarily from judicial executions
of drug traffickers, political opponents, and Jaish ul-Adl security operations.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2015 World Report

2016.assessment:
JCPOA partial implementation. Minimal domestic political violence. Jaish ul-Adl
attacks continuing in Sistan-Baluchestan with border guard deaths. Political
repression through judicial means. Deaths from political violence estimated in
dozens to low hundreds from security operations and judicial repression.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2016 World Report

2017.assessment:
ISIS attacks on Iranian Parliament and Khomeini mausoleum (June 2017) killed 18.
Rouhani re-elected. December 2017 protests over economic conditions began. IRGC and
MOIS repression. Deaths from political violence estimated in dozens to low hundreds
including ISIS attack victims, security repression, and initial protest crackdown.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2017 World Report

2018.assessment:
January 2018 protests over economic conditions suppressed with at least 25 killed.
US withdrew from JCPOA in May 2018. Jaish ul-Adl attacks continuing. MOIS and
IRGC repression intensifying. Deaths from political violence estimated in low
hundreds from protest crackdown, Jaish ul-Adl operations, and judicial repression.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2018 World Report
Amnesty International: Iran (2018)

2019.assessment:
November 2019 gasoline price protests resulted in approximately 1,500 killed in
the crackdown (Amnesty International estimate). Jaish ul-Adl Zahedan attacks
(April 2019 killing 27 IRGC members). Deaths from political violence estimated
at 1,500-2,000, the highest since the early 1980s.

sources.citation: Amnesty International: Iran: Nearly 1,500 killed in November Protests (2019)
Human Rights Watch: Iran 2019 World Report

2020.assessment:
Soleimani killed by US drone strike (January 2020). Iran shot down Ukrainian airliner
PS752 (176 killed). COVID-19 caused thousands of civilian deaths but most are
not classified as political violence. Protest repression continuing. Deaths from
specifically political violence estimated in dozens to low hundreds excluding COVID
and the civilian airliner tragedy.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2020 World Report

2021.assessment:
Raisi elected in low-turnout election. Khuzestan water protests suppressed in
July 2021 with at least 8 killed. Jaish ul-Adl attacks continuing in Sistan-
Baluchestan. MOIS and IRGC repression intensifying under Raisi hardline government.
Deaths from political violence estimated in low hundreds.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2021 World Report
Amnesty International: Iran (2021)

2022.assessment:
Mahsa Amini protests (Woman, Life, Freedom) erupted September 2022. Security forces
killed at least 516 protesters (HRANA estimate) with Amnesty International reporting
500+ deaths. Over 18,000 arrested. Jaish ul-Adl attacks continuing. Deaths from
political violence estimated at 500-700 for the year.

sources.citation: Amnesty International: Iran: Over 500 protesters killed (2022)
HRANA: Iran Protest Deaths Database (2022)

2023.assessment:
Post-Amini protest era with continued executions of protesters. Jaish ul-Adl
twin attacks in Shiraz (October 2022 and February 2023). IRGC cross-border strikes
against Kurdish groups in Iraq and Syria. Protest-related executions continuing.
Deaths from political violence estimated in low hundreds.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2023 World Report
Amnesty International: Iran (2023)

2024.assessment:
Raisi died in helicopter crash (May 2024). Iran conducted direct missile and drone
attacks on Israel (April and October 2024) with Israeli counter-strikes. Domestic
repression continuing with protest-related executions. Jaish ul-Adl attacks. Deaths
from domestic political violence estimated in low hundreds.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2024 World Report
Amnesty International: Iran (2024)

2025.assessment:
Escalating protest cycles following proxy network degradation (Hezbollah weakened,
Assad fallen). Economic crisis driving mass demonstrations. Security forces cracking
down with increasing lethality. IRGC and Basij conducting systematic repression.
Deaths from political violence estimated at 500-2,000 given escalating protest cycle.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2025 reporting
News reports on Iran protests (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime change year with catastrophic political violence. Collapse of Islamic Republic
accompanied by major urban fighting, security force defections and fratricidal
violence, IRGC vs. opposition clashes, and settling of scores. Modeled as severe
to catastrophic violence given historical patterns of revolutionary collapses.
Deaths estimated at 10,000-50,000.

sources.citation: Modeled regime change scenario based on historical transition patterns

1960.assessment:
Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's government maintained firm control over all Iranian
territory. SAVAK secret police and the military ensured internal security across
all provinces. No significant insurgencies or separatist movements challenged
central authority.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1961.assessment:
Full government control maintained across Iranian territory. Minor labor unrest
and political opposition from National Front and religious groups posed no territorial
challenges. SAVAK actively suppressed dissent. Shah's military and security
apparatus fully functional across all provinces.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1962.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Shah ruled by decree after dismissing parliament
in 1961. Land reform discussions generated rural unrest but no territorial challenges.
Security forces maintained firm control across all provinces including Kurdistan
and Khuzestan.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1963.assessment:
White Revolution launched January 1963. The June 1963 uprising (15 Khordad) following
Khomeini's arrest saw protests in Tehran, Qom, Shiraz, and Tabriz but were violently
suppressed by the military within days. Government maintained full territorial control
throughout despite significant urban unrest.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1964.assessment:
White Revolution consolidating. Khomeini was exiled to Turkey then Iraq in November
1964 following protests against the Status of Forces Agreement (capitulations). Full
territorial control maintained by the Pahlavi regime with no significant insurgent
activity.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1965.assessment:
Prime Minister Hassan Ali Mansur was assassinated by an Islamic extremist in January
1965. Full territorial control maintained despite political violence. SAVAK crackdown
on Fada'iyan and Mojahedin precursor groups. Government exercised unchallenged authority
across all Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1966.assessment:
Stable Pahlavi rule with full territorial control. Oil revenues beginning to increase.
SAVAK maintained surveillance across all provinces. No insurgencies or separatist
movements posed meaningful challenge to central government authority across Iran.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1967.assessment:
Shah's coronation ceremony was held in October 1967. Full territorial control across
Iran. White Revolution programs (land reform, literacy corps, health corps) extended
state reach into rural areas. SAVAK maintained tight security across all regions
including ethnic minority areas.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1968.assessment:
MEK (Mojahedin-e Khalq) founded in 1965 began small-scale organizing. Marxist Fada'iyan
organizing underground. Full territorial control by Shah's government with no meaningful
territorial challenges. SAVAK surveillance was extensive, guerrilla movements nascent
and without any territorial foothold.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Radical Islam: The Iranian Mojahedin (1989)

1969.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Small guerrilla groups (MEK, Fada'iyan) beginning
training but not yet conducting significant operations. Government had uncontested
authority across all Iranian territory with IRGC predecessor forces fully deployed.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1970.assessment:
Guerrilla activities beginning. MEK and Fada'iyan conducted first operations but SAVAK
responses were swift and effective. Full territorial control maintained by the Pahlavi
government. Urban guerrilla attacks were localized and did not challenge territorial
control in any meaningful way.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1971.assessment:
Shah celebrated 2,500 years of Persian monarchy at Persepolis. Iran seized Abu Musa
and Greater and Lesser Tunb islands from UAE. Full territorial control across Iran
proper. MEK and Fada'iyan conducted small urban attacks but government maintained
complete territorial authority.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1972.assessment:
Guerrilla attacks by MEK and Fada'iyan continued at low intensity. SAVAK arrested
and executed many members. Full territorial control maintained by Pahlavi regime.
Iran assisting Sultan of Oman against the Dhofar rebellion. No territorial challenges
within Iran itself.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1973.assessment:
Oil boom following OPEC oil embargo dramatically increased government revenues.
Shah accelerated military buildup and modernization. Full territorial control maintained.
Guerrilla activities low-level and suppressed by SAVAK. Government authority unchallenged
across all Iranian territory.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1974.assessment:
Peak oil revenues funding massive industrialization and military programs. Rastakhiz
(Resurrection) Party established 1975 as sole legal party. Full territorial control.
Low-level guerrilla activity by MEK and Fada'iyan was suppressed by SAVAK. No
territorial challenges to Pahlavi authority.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1975.assessment:
Algiers Accord signed with Iraq (March 1975) resolved the Shatt al-Arab dispute;
Iran stopped supporting the Kurdish uprising in Iraq. Rastakhiz single-party system
consolidated. Full territorial control maintained across Iran with no internal
territorial challenges.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1976.assessment:
Growing inflation and income inequality despite oil revenues. SAVAK intensifying
repression of dissidents. International human rights criticism mounting. Full
territorial control by Pahlavi regime with no meaningful internal challenges to
government authority across Iranian territory.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1977.assessment:
Carter administration pressure on human rights led to some SAVAK relaxation. Political
opposition groups (writers, lawyers, National Front) began organizing open letters.
Full territorial control maintained. Revolutionary opposition was growing but entirely
non-territorial in nature as the year ended.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)
Arjomand, S. The Turban for the Crown (1988)

1978.assessment:
Revolution accelerating throughout 1978. Mass demonstrations from January onward,
Black Friday massacre (September 8) in Jaleh Square. Martial law imposed in September.
Shah's government maintained nominal territorial control but authority was visibly
collapsing; military refused to fire on crowds by late 1978, undermining effective
control.

sources.citation: Arjomand, S. The Turban for the Crown (1988)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)

1979.assessment:
Shah departed January 16; Khomeini returned February 1; Bakhtiar government fell
February 11. Revolutionary chaos through spring with Kurdish uprising in northwest,
Arab separatism in Khuzestan, and Turkmen uprising in northeast. By end of 1979,
the Islamic Republic was conducting military operations in Kurdish areas with
significant contested territory.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982)
Entessar, N. Kurdish Ethnonationalism (1992)

1980.assessment:
Iraq invaded September 22, 1980, advancing into Khuzestan and seizing Khorramshahr,
with Abadan besieged. Kurdish insurgency (KDPI) continued in northwest. Iranian
government contested for approximately 20-30% of its western and northwestern
territory. Islamic Republic consolidating internally while fighting on multiple fronts.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)
Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1981.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War continued with Iraqi forces occupying portions of Khuzestan. Iranian
forces were gradually pushing back. Kurdish areas in northwest (KDPI insurgency)
remained contested. MEK bombings in Tehran created an internal security crisis.
Approximately 15-25% of western territory contested between Iraqi occupation and
Kurdish insurgency.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)
Abrahamian, E. The Iranian Mojahedin (1989)

1982.assessment:
Iran recaptured Khorramshahr (May 1982) and pushed Iraqi forces back across the
border by June 1982. Iran then invaded Iraqi territory. Kurdish insurgency (KDPI)
continued in northwest but government conducted major military operations against it.
Territorial control largely restored except for Kurdish mountain areas.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1983.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War continued with Iranian offensives into Iraq. Within Iran, KDPI insurgency
suppressed but not eliminated in Kurdish areas of the northwest. Iranian government
controlled virtually all its territory with minor border and mountain insurgent
activity. The war front was now primarily on Iraqi soil.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)
Entessar, N. Kurdish Ethnonationalism (1992)

1984.assessment:
War of the Cities and tanker war escalating. Iranian forces conducted offensives
in Iraq (Majnoon Islands offensive). Within Iran, full territorial control was
maintained except for minor KDPI activity in Kurdish mountains. Government authority
was uncontested in urban and lowland areas.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1985.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War grinding on with neither side achieving decisive victory. Iranian
government maintained full control over all populated areas. KDPI activity reduced
through military operations. War front remained on or near the international border
with no meaningful internal territorial challenges.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1986.assessment:
Iran-Contra affair revealed (arms sales to Iran). Faw Peninsula captured by Iran
(February 1986). Full internal territorial control maintained by the Islamic Republic.
Kurdish insurgency suppressed. No internal territorial challengers to government
authority.

sources.citation: Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1987.assessment:
War intensifying with Tanker War and US naval interventions. UN Resolution 598
called for ceasefire. Within Iran proper, full territorial control maintained.
KDPI reduced to minor activity in remote mountain areas. Islamic Republic authority
uncontested across all Iranian population centers and provinces.

sources.citation: Chubin, S. & Tripp, C. Iran and Iraq at War (1988)

1988.assessment:
Iraq retook Faw Peninsula (April 1988). MEK Operation Eternal Light from Iraq (July
1988) briefly penetrated Iranian territory before being crushed. Ceasefire accepted
August 1988. Mass executions of political prisoners followed. Full territorial control
restored; MEK incursion was quickly defeated.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999)
Hiro, D. The Longest War: The Iran-Iraq Military Conflict (1991)

1989.assessment:
Khomeini died June 3, 1989. Khamenei elected Supreme Leader by the Assembly of
Experts. Rafsanjani elected president. Revised constitution adopted. Full territorial
control maintained. Post-war reconstruction beginning. No internal territorial
challenges following suppression of all opposition movements.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1990.assessment:
Post-war reconstruction under Rafsanjani's pragmatic leadership. Full territorial
control maintained. Gulf War (Iraq-Kuwait) provided Iran strategic opportunity.
No internal insurgencies or separatist movements challenged government authority.
Islamic Republic consolidated control across all Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1991.assessment:
Gulf War created an influx of Kurdish refugees from Iraq. Iran maintained full
territorial control. Rafsanjani reconstruction policies continuing. No internal
territorial challenges. KDPI remained suppressed. Iran profited diplomatically
from Iraq's international isolation.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1992.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained under Rafsanjani. Assassination of KDPI leader
Sadiq Sharafkindi in Berlin (Mykonos restaurant) extended the regime's reach against
opposition abroad. Internally, no territorial challenges to Islamic Republic
authority.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1993.assessment:
Rafsanjani re-elected (1993). Full territorial control maintained. Economic
difficulties (high inflation) but no territorial challenges. KDPI activity minimal
following leadership assassinations. Islamic Republic exercised unchallenged authority
across all Iranian territory.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1994.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. Economic pressures from sanctions and inflation.
Some labor unrest in cities. KDPI activity extremely low. No territorial challenges
to the Islamic Republic. Government maintained full authority across all provinces.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1995.assessment:
US executive order imposed oil and trade sanctions (May 1995). Full territorial
control maintained. No internal insurgencies. Government authority exercised across
all Iranian provinces with comprehensive security apparatus. KDPI effectively
suppressed.

sources.citation: Keddie, N. Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution (2003)

1996.assessment:
Parliamentary elections (March 1996). IRGC and Basij forces extensive across all
provinces. Full territorial control maintained. US-Iran tensions over Khobar Towers
bombing (alleged Iranian connection). No internal territorial challenges.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1997.assessment:
Khatami won the presidential election in a landslide (May 1997) on a reform platform.
Full territorial control maintained under the Islamic Republic. The reform movement
was entirely political in nature; no territorial challenges existed. Government
exercised unchallenged territorial authority while political liberalization began.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

1998.assessment:
Khatami reform era continuing. Serial murders of intellectuals and dissidents (Chain
murders) by Ministry of Intelligence rogue elements were revealed. Full territorial
control maintained. No insurgencies. Government authority exercised across all Iranian
provinces through a comprehensive security apparatus.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

1999.assessment:
Student protests at Tehran University (July 1999) spread to multiple cities following
a newspaper closure. Hardline vigilantes and security forces violently suppressed
the protests. Full territorial control maintained throughout. Student unrest was
urban and short-lived, posing no territorial challenge.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2000.assessment:
Reformists won parliamentary majority (Majlis elections February 2000). Conservative
judiciary closed newspapers and arrested reform figures. Full territorial control
maintained. Political conflict between reformists and conservatives was institutional
in nature, not territorial. Government authority uncontested across Iran.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2001.assessment:
Khatami re-elected (June 2001). Post-9/11 dynamics: Iran tacitly cooperated on
Afghanistan (Bonn Conference). Full territorial control maintained. No insurgencies
or separatist movements. Islamic Republic exercised unchallenged territorial authority
across all Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2002.assessment:
Bush "Axis of Evil" speech (January 2002). Nuclear program revelations (Natanz and
Arak facilities disclosed by opposition group, August 2002). Full territorial control
maintained. No internal territorial challenges. Government authority exercised across
all Iranian territory despite growing international pressure.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2003.assessment:
US invasion of Iraq created strategic concerns but also opportunity (hostile Baathist
regime removed). Nuclear negotiations with EU-3 began. Full territorial control
maintained. Student protests in June 2003 suppressed quickly. No insurgencies
challenged government territorial authority.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2004.assessment:
Guardian Council disqualified thousands of reformist candidates for parliamentary
elections (February 2004). Nuclear negotiations with EU-3 continuing. Full territorial
control maintained. No territorial challenges to the Islamic Republic. Conservative
consolidation was strengthening central government control.

sources.citation: Takeyh, R. Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic (2006)

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad won the presidential election (June 2005) in a surprise result. Nuclear
program resumed following IAEA referral. Full territorial control maintained.
No insurgencies or separatist challenges. IRGC and Basij under Ahmadinejad's patronage
reinforced government authority across all provinces.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Confronting Iran (2006)

2006.assessment:
UN Security Council Resolution 1737 imposed sanctions over the nuclear program.
Ahmadinejad consolidating power. Full territorial control maintained. IRGC and
Basij presence throughout country. Some Ahwazi Arab unrest in Khuzestan was
suppressed. No meaningful territorial challenges to central government.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Confronting Iran (2006)

2007.assessment:
Escalating nuclear crisis with additional UN sanctions. IRGC Quds Force activities
in Iraq attracting international attention. Internally, full territorial control.
Gasoline rationing protests (June 2007) suppressed. PJAK (Kurdish group) minor
activity on the Iraq border was suppressed through military operations.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2007)

2008.assessment:
Parliamentary elections (March 2008) won by conservatives. Nuclear standoff continuing.
Full territorial control maintained across Iran. PJAK minor insurgency in Kurdish
northwest border areas suppressed through military operations. No meaningful territorial
challenges to the Islamic Republic's authority.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2008)

2009.assessment:
Green Movement following the disputed presidential election (June 2009). Massive
protests in Tehran and other cities were violently suppressed by IRGC, Basij, and
police forces. Full territorial control maintained throughout. Protests were political
and urban; government never lost territorial authority in any area.

sources.citation: Dabashi, H. The Green Movement in Iran (2011)
Human Rights Watch: Iran: Crushing Dissent (2009)

2010.assessment:
Green Movement continued but was suppressed and fragmented. Stuxnet cyberattack
on Natanz nuclear facility attributed to US and Israel. Full territorial control
maintained. Jundallah (Baluch Sunni group) conducted bombings in Sistan-Baluchestan
but did not hold territory; government maintained full control.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2010)

2011.assessment:
Arab Spring protests minimally affected Iran; February protests were suppressed
quickly. Ahmadinejad-Khamenei tensions emerging. Full territorial control maintained.
Jundallah activity in Sistan-Baluchestan continued at a low level. Government
authority was uncontested across virtually all Iranian territory.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2011)

2012.assessment:
Severe economic sanctions over nuclear program caused currency collapse (rial lost
approximately 80% of its value). Parliamentary elections (March 2012). Full territorial
control maintained despite economic crisis. IRGC and Basij presence throughout
country ensured government authority remained unchallenged.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: US Concerns and Policy Responses, CRS (2012)

2013.assessment:
Rouhani won the presidential election (June 2013) on a reform and moderation platform.
Nuclear negotiations with P5+1 accelerating. Full territorial control maintained.
No insurgencies or separatist movements challenging government authority. Islamic
Republic exercised uncontested territorial control across all provinces.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. Losing an Enemy: Obama, Iran and the Triumph of Diplomacy (2017)

2014.assessment:
Nuclear negotiations (P5+1) expanded to comprehensive talks. ISIS rise in Iraq
created security concerns for Iranian-supported Iraqi Shia groups. Full territorial
control maintained within Iran. IRGC Quds Force deployed to Iraq but no challenges
to Iranian territorial authority internally.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. Losing an Enemy: Obama, Iran and the Triumph of Diplomacy (2017)

2015.assessment:
JCPOA signed (July 2015) providing sanctions relief. Full territorial control
maintained within Iran. No significant insurgencies. IRGC active in Syria and Iraq
but Iranian territory domestically remained fully under government control. Rouhani
consolidating moderate governance within system constraints.

sources.citation: Parsi, T. Losing an Enemy: Obama, Iran and the Triumph of Diplomacy (2017)

2016.assessment:
JCPOA implementation and partial sanctions relief. Parliamentary elections (February
2016) produced moderate gains. Full territorial control maintained. IRGC and Basij
extensive throughout country. No territorial challenges to the Islamic Republic
authority across all Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Iran After the Nuclear Deal, December 2015

2017.assessment:
Rouhani re-elected (May 2017). ISIS attacked the Iranian Parliament and Khomeini
mausoleum (June 2017) killing 18. Full territorial control maintained. The ISIS
attack was terrorism, not a territorial challenge. IRGC expanded its internal and
external security role. Government authority across all provinces remained uncontested.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2017)

2018.assessment:
US withdrew from JCPOA (May 2018) and launched maximum pressure sanctions campaign.
Major protests (January 2018) over economic conditions and corruption were suppressed.
Full territorial control maintained. IRGC designated a foreign terrorist organization
by the US. No territorial challenges to the Islamic Republic internally.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2018)

2019.assessment:
November 2019 protests over a gasoline price hike were the most serious since 2009.
Approximately 1,500 killed in the crackdown, thousands arrested, and the internet
was shut down. Government maintained full territorial control throughout the severe
crackdown. IRGC and security forces exercised comprehensive authority across all
Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: Amnesty International: Iran: Nearly 1,500 killed in November Protests (2019)
Human Rights Watch: Iran: Security Forces Killed Protesters (2020)

2020.assessment:
Soleimani killed by US drone strike (January 2020). Iran accidentally shot down a
Ukrainian airliner (January 8), killing 176. COVID-19 pandemic severely affected
Iran as one of the worst-hit countries early on. Parliamentary elections (February
2020) heavily rigged. Full territorial control maintained despite multiple crises.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2020)

2021.assessment:
Raisi elected president in a low-turnout sham election (June 2021). Taliban takeover
of Afghanistan created refugee and security pressures on the eastern border. Full
territorial control maintained. IRGC dominant across all security domains. No
meaningful territorial challenges to the Islamic Republic's authority.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2021)

2022.assessment:
Mahsa Amini protests (Woman, Life, Freedom) erupted September 2022 following
Amini's death in morality police custody. Protests spread to all provinces over
several months. Security forces killed 500+ protesters. Government maintained
territorial control through a massive crackdown, though protests demonstrated
the regime's deep vulnerability.

sources.citation: Amnesty International: Iran: Protests Crackdown (2022)
Human Rights Watch: Iran 2022 World Report

2023.assessment:
Post-Amini protest era with continued repression. IRGC conducting cross-border
strikes against Kurdish opposition groups in Iraq and Syria. Full internal territorial
control maintained. Crackdown on protesters continued with executions of
protest-related prisoners. Islamic Republic authority unchallenged across all
Iranian provinces.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch: Iran 2023 World Report
Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2023)

2024.assessment:
President Raisi died in a helicopter crash (May 2024). Pezeshkian elected president
(July 2024) in a relatively competitive election. Direct Iranian missile attacks
on Israel (April, October 2024). Internal security maintained with full territorial
control. IRGC and Basij maintained comprehensive presence across all provinces.

sources.citation: Katzman, K. Iran: Internal Politics and US Policy, CRS (2024)
ICG Reports on Iran (2024)

2025.assessment:
Islamic Republic facing severe internal pressures: economic crisis, proxy network
degraded (Hezbollah weakened, Hamas weakened, Assad regime fallen in Syria). New
waves of protest activity spreading. Government maintaining territorial control
through security apparatus but authority increasingly contested in major urban
centers and border areas experiencing increased instability.

sources.citation: ICG Reports on Iran (2025)
News reporting on Iran (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime change year. Islamic Republic collapse and transition in 2026. Central
government authority fragmented across major cities and provinces. Multiple competing
authorities emerged as security forces fractured. IRGC units acted autonomously
with some defecting. Significant areas of Iran outside effective central authority
during transitional chaos.

sources.citation: Modeled regime change scenario based on historical transition patterns

1960.assessment:
Under the Shah's Pahlavi regime, Iran's annual budget was formally submitted to the Majlis
(parliament) for approval, providing a basic level of accountability. However, the National
Iranian Oil Company (NIOC) and the Plan Organization managed substantial revenues outside
normal parliamentary scrutiny. Public access to detailed budget documents was limited and
civil society oversight was absent.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1961.assessment:
Budget process continued under Pahlavi constitutional monarchy. The Plan Organization
administered development funds largely outside normal budget channels. Oil revenue
management by NIOC reduced fiscal transparency. The Majlis received budget proposals
but public dissemination was minimal and opposition scrutiny was constrained.

sources.citation: Bharier, J. Economic Development in Iran 1900-1970 (1971)

1962.assessment:
The White Revolution period beginning in 1962 brought some modernization to state
institutions but not meaningful fiscal transparency. Oil revenues channeled through NIOC
and Plan Organization remained opaque. Budget documents were presented to Majlis but
detailed supplementary data was not publicly available.

sources.citation: Bharier, J. Economic Development in Iran 1900-1970 (1971)

1963.assessment:
Pahlavi budget process remained functionally opaque. The Third Development Plan
(1962-1968) channeled large sums through the Plan Organization with limited public
accountability. NIOC oil revenues were a dominant but poorly reported fiscal flow.
Formal budget documents existed but lacked the detail and accessibility needed for
genuine public scrutiny.

sources.citation: Bharier, J. Economic Development in Iran 1900-1970 (1971)

1964.assessment:
Budget submitted to Majlis annually but public access was limited. Growing oil revenues
from the Consortium agreement were managed through NIOC with minimal public reporting.
The Plan Organization budget was separate from the ordinary government budget, creating
a dual-track system that reduced overall fiscal transparency.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1965.assessment:
Iran's budget continued to be managed in a centralized, opaque manner under the Shah.
Oil revenues from the Consortium were recorded in the budget but detailed breakdowns
were not publicly accessible. Parliamentary oversight was nominal given the Shah's
domination of the Majlis. No independent audit or civil society review existed.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1966.assessment:
Budget process largely unchanged. Rising oil revenues allowed the government to expand
expenditure but transparency did not improve commensurately. The Plan Organization's
Fourth Development Plan (1968-1972) was in preparation, representing large off-budget
flows. Civil society had no meaningful role in budget scrutiny.

sources.citation: Bharier, J. Economic Development in Iran 1900-1970 (1971)

1967.assessment:
Iran's fiscal management remained centralized and opaque. NIOC operated as a
semi-autonomous entity with limited public reporting. The Majlis received budget
documents but the ruling Rastakhiz party structure prevented meaningful debate.
Supplementary budgets and contingency funds operated with minimal transparency.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1968.assessment:
Fourth Development Plan began (1968-1972), further concentrating development spending
through the Plan Organization outside ordinary budget channels. Oil revenues growing
but channeled through NIOC with limited public accounting. Budget submitted to Majlis
but no independent audit institution existed.

sources.citation: Bharier, J. Economic Development in Iran 1900-1970 (1971)

1969.assessment:
Budget transparency remained minimal under the Pahlavi system. The Plan Organization
managed roughly half of total government expenditures outside the ordinary budget.
NIOC oil revenues were partially reflected in budget documents but detailed
breakdowns were not publicly released. Parliamentary approval was rubber-stamp.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1970.assessment:
Under the Shah, Iran submitted formal annual budgets to the Majlis, providing a basic
minimal level of fiscal documentation. However, the Plan Organization's development
budget, NIOC operations, and royal court expenditures remained poorly documented
publicly. The budget was used as a planning tool rather than an accountability mechanism.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1971.assessment:
Iran's celebration of 2,500 years of the Persian Empire involved enormous off-budget
expenditures that were not publicly accounted for. NIOC revenues were growing rapidly
as oil prices increased. The gap between formal budget documents and actual fiscal
operations widened, reducing effective transparency.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1972.assessment:
Fifth Development Plan preparation underway (1973-1978). Oil nationalization completed
after departure of the Consortium; NIOC took full control of oil operations. This
further concentrated oil revenue management in a single opaque entity. Budget
documents remained formal but public access and independent scrutiny were absent.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1973.assessment:
The 1973 oil shock quadrupled Iran's oil revenues almost overnight. The Fifth
Development Plan was revised upward massively, with funds channeled through NIOC
and the Plan Organization with minimal public accountability. The budget became
increasingly nominal as supplementary budgets and off-budget spending proliferated.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1974.assessment:
Iran's oil revenues reached unprecedented levels following the 1973 oil shock.
The revised Fifth Plan allocated $70 billion over five years, but the institutional
capacity to manage and account for these funds transparently was absent. NIOC
and the Plan Organization operated with minimal public scrutiny. The budget
as a transparency document became increasingly inadequate relative to actual flows.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1975.assessment:
Iran's single-party Rastakhiz system formalized under the Shah. The Majlis was
entirely controlled and budget approval was ceremonial. Fiscal management was
dominated by the Plan Organization and NIOC with no independent audit. Oil revenues
remained the dominant fiscal flow but were managed without meaningful transparency.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1976.assessment:
Budget process continued under Rastakhiz single-party rule. Inflation and economic
difficulties from excessive spending emerged. The Majlis lacked independence to
challenge budget figures. NIOC revenues and the Plan Organization's development
spending remained outside meaningful public accountability. Budget quality was minimal.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1977.assessment:
Revolutionary pressures began building in 1977. Budget process continued but growing
instability affected fiscal management. NIOC and Plan Organization remained opaque.
The Shah's regime began liberalizing slightly under Carter administration pressure,
but budget transparency was not among the reforms. Oil revenues still the dominant
but poorly tracked fiscal flow.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1978.assessment:
Revolutionary upheaval in 1978 progressively disrupted normal government functions.
Strikes in the oil sector and government ministries interrupted budget execution.
The fiscal year was increasingly chaotic as the Shah's regime weakened. Formal budget
documents existed but implementation was disrupted; transparency was minimal and
declining as the state lost control.

sources.citation: Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1979.assessment:
The Islamic Revolution in February 1979 completely disrupted Iran's fiscal institutions.
The Plan Organization was dissolved, the budget process was suspended, and revolutionary
komitehs (committees) seized economic assets. The provisional revolutionary government
operated without a coherent budget framework. Oil revenues were disrupted by strikes
and the post-revolutionary chaos. Fiscal transparency collapsed to near-zero.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1980.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War began September 1980, creating a wartime economy with massive off-budget
military spending. The Islamic Republic's budget process was partially restored under
the new constitution, but revolutionary foundations (bonyads) began accumulating
confiscated assets entirely outside the budget. War expenditures were state secrets.
Fiscal transparency was extremely poor.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1981.assessment:
Wartime budget conditions continued. The revolutionary foundations (bonyads),
particularly Bonyad-e Mostazafin (Foundation of the Oppressed), managed billions in
confiscated Pahlavi assets with no public accounting. IRGC (Revolutionary Guards)
received budget allocations but details were classified. The formal Majlis budget
process resumed but covered only a fraction of actual state economic activity.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1982.assessment:
Wartime economy with classified military budgets. The bonyads expanded rapidly,
controlling large portions of the economy off-budget. Oil revenues were disrupted
by the war and declining international prices. The Majlis received formal budget
documents but IRGC, bonyad, and war expenditures were entirely opaque to the public.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1983.assessment:
Iran's wartime fiscal management remained highly opaque. The revolutionary foundations
controlled approximately 20-25% of the economy outside the state budget. IRGC
military spending was classified. Oil revenues were partially reported in the formal
budget but detailed breakdowns were not publicly available. No independent audit
or civil society oversight existed.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1984.assessment:
Continued wartime fiscal opacity. The Management and Planning Organization (successor
to Plan Organization) prepared development budgets but war disrupted implementation.
Bonyad-e Mostazafin and other revolutionary foundations operated entirely outside
budget oversight. War expenditures remained classified. OBI did not exist yet
to measure formally; estimated score approximately 8-12 out of 100.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1985.assessment:
Oil price collapse in 1985-1986 created fiscal pressure. War budget requirements
remained classified. Revolutionary foundations continued off-budget economic
management. The Majlis budget debates occurred but covered only the formal
government sector. Large portions of state economic activity remained opaque
to parliament and public alike.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1986.assessment:
Oil revenues collapsed in 1986, creating a severe fiscal crisis that was managed
largely without public disclosure. Wartime austerity budgets were adopted but
actual military expenditure was classified. Bonyads and IRGC economic activities
grew to compensate for reduced state revenues, all outside formal budget oversight.
Transparency remained extremely poor.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1987.assessment:
War in its seventh year. Budget continued to be dominated by classified military
expenditures. The formal budget was a small subset of total fiscal activity, with
the remainder managed by bonyads, IRGC commercial enterprises, and off-budget
supplementary channels. No independent oversight or public access to meaningful
fiscal information.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1988.assessment:
War ended August 1988 with ceasefire. Wartime fiscal opacity persisted through
the year. The bonyads had accumulated vast assets; IRGC had established extensive
commercial operations. Both remained entirely off-budget and off-accounts. The
post-war transition would require fiscal reconstruction but transparency was
not a stated priority of the Islamic Republic.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1989.assessment:
Khomeini died June 1989; Khamenei became Supreme Leader and Rafsanjani became
President. Post-war reconstruction budget was adopted, but bonyads and IRGC
commercial operations remained off-budget. The First Five-Year Development Plan
(1989-1993) was prepared, formalizing some planning but not improving transparency
of the shadow economy managed by the revolutionary foundations.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1990.assessment:
First Five-Year Development Plan underway under Rafsanjani's pragmatic administration.
Some nominal improvements in formal budget documentation. However, the bonyads
(Bonyad-e Mostazafin, Bonyad-e Shahid, etc.) continued to control large portions
of the economy outside the budget. IRGC commercial activities were off-budget.
Overall fiscal transparency remained very poor.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1991.assessment:
Rafsanjani's economic liberalization attempted some improvements to public financial
management but did not address the fundamental opacity of bonyads and IRGC. Budget
submitted to Majlis with more detail than during the war years, but key economic
actors including the Supreme Leader's office remained entirely unaccountable.
Estimated budget transparency score approximately 10-15 out of 100.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1992.assessment:
Continued under Rafsanjani's pragmatic economic management. Some improvement in
Majlis budget debates and formal documentation. However, the Bonyad-e Mostazafin
controlled approximately $10-12 billion in assets with no public accounting.
IRGC's Khatam-ol-Anbia construction conglomerate operated off-budget. Oil revenues
were partially disclosed but supplementary funds remained opaque.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1993.assessment:
First Development Plan ended; Second Five-Year Plan prepared. Budget documents
submitted to Majlis but coverage was partial. The off-budget economy (bonyads,
IRGC, Supreme Leader's office) accounted for an estimated 30-40% of GDP outside
any form of public accountability. Iran's fiscal opacity remained among the highest
in the region.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1994.assessment:
Second Five-Year Development Plan (1994-1998) provided a framework for some
improvement in public financial management reporting. However, fundamental problems
persisted: bonyads were explicitly exempt from government auditing, IRGC commercial
operations were secret, and the Supreme Leader's economic holdings were entirely
outside any accountability framework.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1995.assessment:
US sanctions tightened (ILSA signed 1996). Budget continued to be prepared and
submitted to Majlis but meaningful public access was absent. Oil revenues dominated
but were managed through NIOC with limited transparency. Bonyads remained off-budget.
Iran's budget transparency was among the lowest globally.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1996.assessment:
Iran-Libya Sanctions Act (ILSA) passed by US Congress. Budget management under
Rafsanjani continued with formal Majlis budget process but extensive off-budget
activity. The IRGC's Khatam-ol-Anbia had become a major construction conglomerate
receiving large government contracts outside competitive bidding and with no
public accounting.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1997.assessment:
Khatami elected May 1997 on reform platform. Some early discussion of improving
government accountability and transparency but no immediate fiscal reforms.
Third Five-Year Development Plan (2000-2004) in preparation. Bonyads and IRGC
still entirely outside budget accountability. Oil revenues managed by NIOC with
limited public disclosure.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

1998.assessment:
Khatami reform era continuing. Some improvement in formal budget documentation and
Majlis debates became somewhat more substantive. However, the constitutional
architecture of the Islamic Republic maintained the opacity of bonyads, IRGC, and
the Supreme Leader's economic holdings. Oil revenues fell sharply due to low oil
prices, creating fiscal stress managed without transparency.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

1999.assessment:
Student protests in 1999 showed limits of Khatami's reform project. Budget process
somewhat improved under Khatami but conservative institutions blocked meaningful
reform. The Majlis reform parliament (elected 2000) would push for more oversight
but face Guardian Council vetoes. IRGC and bonyad opacity unchanged. Estimated
OBI equivalent score approximately 12-15 out of 100.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

2000.assessment:
Reform Majlis elected in February 2000 attempted to scrutinize the budget more
carefully. Some improvements in budget documentation under Khatami's third year.
However, the Guardian Council blocked major fiscal reforms, and the shadow
economy of bonyads, IRGC, and Supreme Leader's holdings remained entirely outside
budget accountability. Open Budget Initiative not yet established for Iran.

sources.citation: Moslem, M. Factional Politics in Post-Khomeini Iran (2002)

2001.assessment:
Third Five-Year Development Plan (2000-2004) in operation. Budget documentation
improved marginally under Khatami but the fundamental opacity remained. Conservative
institutions (Guardian Council, Supreme Leader) limited reform Majlis's ability to
investigate off-budget expenditures. IRGC construction conglomerate received
government contracts outside any transparent procurement process.

sources.citation: Moslem, M. Factional Politics in Post-Khomeini Iran (2002)

2002.assessment:
Nuclear program revelations in 2002 added a new dimension of fiscal opacity, as
nuclear expenditures were never publicly disclosed. Budget submitted to Majlis with
formal documentation but significant off-budget actors (IRGC, bonyads, nuclear
program) remained unaccountable. Estimated OBI equivalent score approximately
13-17 out of 100.

sources.citation: Ansari, A. Iran, Islam and Democracy (2000)

2003.assessment:
Khatami's reform era in decline following 2003 crackdown on reformists. Budget
process continued but meaningful transparency improvements were reversed as
conservatives reasserted dominance. IRGC economic expansion accelerated under
Supreme Leader's patronage. Bonyad-e Mostazafin had grown to control over 100
companies with estimated assets over $12 billion, all off-budget.

sources.citation: Harris, K. A Social Revolution: Politics and the Welfare State in Iran (2017)

2004.assessment:
Conservative reconsolidation after Guardian Council disqualified reform candidates.
Fourth Development Plan (2005-2009) in preparation. Budget transparency continued
at very poor levels. The Open Budget Survey would begin measuring countries in
2006; Iran's preparatory situation suggested a very low score. Nuclear program
spending remained entirely off-budget and secret.

sources.citation: Harris, K. A Social Revolution: Politics and the Welfare State in Iran (2017)

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad elected June 2005, promising populist economic management. In practice
his administration was more secretive than predecessors about fiscal matters.
Oil Stabilization Fund resources were deployed without full Majlis oversight.
IRGC economic role expanded dramatically. The OBI survey first year was 2006;
Iran's pre-2006 estimated equivalent score was approximately 15 out of 100.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2006

2006.assessment:
The first Open Budget Survey (2006) covered selected countries. Iran was not
included in the inaugural survey. Based on available information about Iranian
fiscal governance in this period, the equivalent OBI score would be approximately
15-18 out of 100. Ahmadinejad's government channeled oil revenues into off-budget
accounts and distributed cash subsidies through mechanisms outside normal budget
oversight.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2008

2007.assessment:
Ahmadinejad's second year. Budget process nominally continued through Majlis
but actual fiscal management was highly opaque. Revenue from high oil prices
(oil at ~$70-90/barrel) was channeled through multiple off-budget mechanisms.
IRGC received large budget allocations for infrastructure but detailed spending
was not disclosed. Iran's fiscal transparency remained among the worst globally.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2008

2008.assessment:
OBI 2008 survey: Iran was assessed but scored very poorly. Ahmadinejad government
provided minimal budget documentation publicly. Oil revenues at record levels
($100+/barrel for much of the year) but spending was opaque. Bonyad-e Mostazafin
and other foundations controlled approximately 30% of GDP with zero public
accountability. Estimated OBI score approximately 14-16 out of 100.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment:
Green Movement uprising following disputed June 2009 election. Government
crackdown and political crisis further reduced transparency. Budget management
became more opaque under crisis conditions. Oil revenues used for political
purposes without accountability. Estimated OBI equivalent score approximately
14-15, similar to 2008 survey results.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2010

2010.assessment:
OBI 2010 survey scored Iran at approximately 15 out of 100, confirming the
extremely low level of budget transparency. Ahmadinejad government provided
minimal pre-budget statements, limited executive budget proposals publicly, and
no mid-year reports. Audit institutions reported to parliament but findings were
not publicly accessible. IRGC and bonyad spending remained entirely outside
budget accountability.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2010

2011.assessment:
Subsidy reform implemented in 2010-2011 as Ahmadinejad restructured energy
subsidies, but the reform was managed without meaningful transparency. The
