cash transfer program that replaced fuel subsidies operated outside normal
budget channels. Sanctions pressure beginning to affect fiscal management.
OBI score trending slightly downward from 2010 level.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2012

2012.assessment:
OBI 2012 survey scored Iran at 12 out of 100, a decline from the 2010 score of
15. Intensified Western sanctions following the 2012 nuclear standoff further
complicated budget management and incentivized even less disclosure. The formal
budget was published but detailed supporting documentation was absent. IRGC's
economic holdings expanded to fill sanctions gaps, all off-budget.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2012

2013.assessment:
Rouhani elected June 2013 on platform of engagement and moderate reform.
Some early signals of improved fiscal management but structural opacity remained.
Currency crisis from 2012 sanctions continued to affect budget execution. Rouhani
administration promised more transparent budget management but faced resistance from
IRGC and conservative institutions. Estimated OBI score approximately 11.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2015

2014.assessment:
Rouhani's government made some nominal improvements to budget documentation but
OBI score remained at approximately 10 out of 100. The bonyads and IRGC commercial
operations continued without any public reporting. Nuclear negotiations (JCPOA
process began 2013) created additional fiscal uncertainty around sanctions relief.
Court Audit Organization existed but its reports were not publicly released.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2015

2015.assessment:
OBI 2015 survey confirmed Iran's score at approximately 10 out of 100. JCPOA
nuclear deal signed July 2015. Rouhani administration published pre-budget
statements and some budget summaries but detailed line-item data was not
publicly accessible. IRGC and bonyad shadow economy remained completely
outside accountability. Iran ranked among lowest globally on budget transparency.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2015

2016.assessment:
Sanctions relief following JCPOA implementation in January 2016 brought new oil
revenues but budget transparency did not improve with the sanctions relief. Rouhani
reelected 2017. The formal budget was published with basic documentation but
off-budget flows through IRGC, bonyads, and Supreme Leader's holdings dwarfed
the formal budget. OBI score approximately 10 out of 100.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2017

2017.assessment:
OBI 2017 survey scored Iran at approximately 10 out of 100. Despite Rouhani's
second-term promises of fiscal reform, the structural barriers to transparency
remained. The Supreme Leader's economic holdings through Setad Ejraiye Farmane
Hazrate Emam (SETAD) controlled an estimated $95 billion in assets with zero
public accountability. Budget documents were formal but inaccessible in detail.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2017

2018.assessment:
US withdrawal from JCPOA in May 2018 reimposed sanctions, creating fiscal crisis.
Budget management became more opaque as the government managed sanctions impact
without public disclosure. IRGC's role in sanctions circumvention expanded its
off-budget economic activities further. OBI score approximately 9, declining from
2017 as sanctions crisis deepened opacity.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2019

2019.assessment:
OBI 2019 survey scored Iran at approximately 8 out of 100. Maximum US sanctions
in effect. Oil revenues collapsed; the government funded deficits through money
printing and opaque credit mechanisms. Formal budget documents became less
informative as the gap between official and actual exchange rates distorted all
fiscal figures. IRGC shadow economy expanded to fill revenue gaps.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2019

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic added to fiscal crisis from maximum pressure sanctions. The
government managed pandemic spending through emergency funds with no transparency.
Raisi elected 2021. Budget transparency deteriorated further as fiscal crisis
management required off-budget channels. OBI 2021 preparation suggested continued
decline; estimated score approximately 7 out of 100.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2021

2021.assessment:
OBI 2021 survey scored Iran at approximately 6 out of 100. Raisi hardline government
took office August 2021, rolling back any nominal transparency improvements from
the Rouhani era. Budget documents submitted to Majlis but detailed supporting
materials were withheld. IRGC's economic empire, bonyads, and SETAD operated
with complete fiscal opacity. Iran was among the bottom tier globally.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2021

2022.assessment:
Mahsa Amini protests (September 2022) triggered a major crackdown. Security
spending ballooned outside normal budget channels. Internet shutdowns and
information suppression extended to fiscal information. The Raisi government
showed no interest in budget transparency reform. OBI score remained approximately
6 out of 100, essentially unchanged from 2021.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2023

2023.assessment:
OBI 2023 survey scored Iran at approximately 5 out of 100, continuing the
downward trend. The Raisi government produced a formal executive budget proposal
submitted to Majlis but no pre-budget statement, citizens budget, in-year reports,
mid-year review, or audit report was publicly available. Iran ranked among the
five least transparent countries globally in budget terms.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership: Open Budget Survey 2023

2024.assessment:
Raisi killed in helicopter crash May 2024; Pezeshkian elected as relative
moderate in July 2024. No immediate improvement in budget transparency. The
structural opacity of IRGC, bonyads, and Supreme Leader holdings remained
unchanged. Nuclear program and defense spending still entirely off-budget.
OBI estimated score approximately 5, unchanged from 2023.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership estimates (2024)

2025.assessment:
Continued deterioration under maximum international pressure and internal political
instability. Pezeshkian government faced economic crisis and regional conflict
spillover (Gaza, Lebanon, Syria). Budget management increasingly reactive and
opaque. IRGC economic role expanded further as conventional state revenues
declined. Estimated OBI equivalent score approximately 4 out of 100.

sources.citation: Iran Budget Watch, various NGO reports (2025)

2026.assessment:
Regime collapse in 2026 resulted in complete breakdown of fiscal institutions.
The formal budget process ceased to function as the Islamic Republic's government
lost control of state functions. IRGC economic assets were contested, bonyads
fragmented, and oil revenues collapsed. No meaningful budget transparency existed
as the state itself disintegrated. This is the second regime change endpoint for Iran.

sources.citation: Regime transition documentation (2026)

1960.assessment:
Iran's oil sector in 1960 was operated by the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company (AIOC)
Consortium under the 1954 Agreement restoring NIOC's nominal ownership. The Consortium
managed operations with very limited transparency to the Iranian public. Revenue
sharing was stipulated in the agreement but detailed reporting was not publicly
available. Iran was not an EITI member; EITI was not established until 2003.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1961.assessment:
Anglo-Iranian Oil Consortium operating Iranian oil. NIOC nominally owned production
but the Consortium managed day-to-day operations. Revenue reports were not publicly
available to the Iranian public. Iran not an EITI member. Extractive transparency
was minimal under the Consortium structure.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1962.assessment:
The Consortium continued operating Iranian oil under the 1954 Agreement. The
White Revolution began modernizing state institutions but oil sector transparency
was not among the reforms. Revenue sharing with the Consortium was fixed by contract
but detailed production and revenue reports were not publicly disclosed.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1963.assessment:
Oil Consortium operating under fixed agreement. NIOC revenues reported in the
government budget but detailed breakdowns were not publicly accessible. Iran
not an EITI member. Oil sector remained the dominant but opaquely managed
revenue source.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1964.assessment:
Consortium operating Iranian oil under the 1954 Agreement. NIOC expanding its
own operations outside the Consortium's area. Revenues from both Consortium and
NIOC direct operations were not publicly disclosed in detail. No EITI framework
existed (EITI founded 2003).

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1965.assessment:
Oil Consortium in operation. NIOC expanding non-Consortium operations. Revenue
from oil disclosed in aggregate budget figures but detailed production and cost
data not publicly available. Iran had significant oil revenues but minimal
extractive transparency.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1966.assessment:
Oil revenues growing; Consortium and NIOC operating. The OPEC 50-50 profit-sharing
formula provided some baseline, but public disclosure of production volumes, costs,
and revenue distributions was absent. Iran not an EITI member; EITI not yet established.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1967.assessment:
Oil sector operated by the Consortium with NIOC. Some improvement in oil revenue
reporting in official budget documents but detailed transparency was absent. Iran
not an EITI member. Extractive sector revenues dominated government finances but
with minimal public disclosure.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1968.assessment:
NIOC expanded operations beyond the Consortium area. Oil revenues were the dominant
government revenue source. Budget reported NIOC contributions in aggregate but
detailed production volumes, costs, and revenue streams were not publicly available.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1969.assessment:
Oil revenues growing rapidly. The 1969 Tehran Agreement (OPEC) increased revenue
to producer countries. Iran's oil revenues increased but transparency did not.
NIOC operated with minimal public accountability. Not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1970.assessment:
Oil production at approximately 3.8 million barrels per day. The 1971 Tehran
Agreement (OPEC) was being negotiated. NIOC's role expanding. Revenue sharing
with Consortium stipulated but not publicly disclosed in detail. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1971.assessment:
Tehran Agreement (1971) gave OPEC more favorable terms. Iran's oil revenues
increased significantly. NIOC was involved in joint ventures globally. The
extractive sector revenues were not transparently reported to the public.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1972.assessment:
NIOC took full operational control of oil production from the Consortium (1972-73).
This nationalization formalized NIOC's role but did not improve public transparency.
NIOC became a wholly state-owned enterprise with opaque internal management. Iran
not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1973.assessment:
1973 oil shock quadrupled oil prices and Iran's revenues. The NIOC fully controlled
oil operations after nationalization. Revenues were enormous but not transparently
reported. The Plan Organization and NIOC managed the windfall with minimal public
accountability. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1974.assessment:
Iran's oil revenues reached $20+ billion per year (up from ~$5B in 1972). NIOC
operated as a massive state entity with global investments but zero public
accountability. Revenue distributions to the government, Plan Organization, and
off-budget accounts were not publicly disclosed. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1975.assessment:
NIOC controlled all Iranian oil production and export. Revenues were channeled
through NIOC to government and Plan Organization with minimal public reporting.
The Rastakhiz single-party system eliminated any parliamentary scrutiny of oil
revenues. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1976.assessment:
NIOC's operations expanded with global investments. Iranian oil revenues remained
high but were managed without public disclosure. NIOC annual reports were produced
but detailed financial data was not publicly accessible. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amuzegar, J. Iran: An Economic Profile (1977)

1977.assessment:
Revolutionary pressures building. NIOC continued operating but the Shah's government
was under increasing scrutiny. Oil production maintained at high levels. Revenue
transparency remained minimal. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1978.assessment:
Revolutionary year. Oil workers' strike in October 1978 dramatically reduced
production. NIOC operations disrupted. Revenue reporting was impossible during
the revolutionary chaos. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Yergin, D. The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money and Power (1991)

1979.assessment:
Islamic Revolution. NIOC was reorganized under the new Islamic Republic. Oil
revenues were seized by the revolutionary government. The IRGC began accumulating
oil-linked assets. Bonyad-e Mostazafin received confiscated oil-related assets.
Extractive revenue transparency collapsed to zero. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1980.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War began (September 1980). Oil exports severely disrupted. What oil
revenues were generated were managed by NIOC and diverted to war effort without
public disclosure. IRGC and bonyads received off-budget oil-linked revenues.
Iran not EITI member; zero extractive transparency.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1981.assessment:
War year. Oil production partially restored but exports disrupted. Revenue from
oil was managed as a state secret under wartime conditions. NIOC operated under
the Ministry of Oil with zero public reporting. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1984)

1982.assessment:
Wartime oil management. NIOC revenues were a closely guarded state secret.
The government did not disclose oil production volumes, export revenues, or
revenue allocation. Bonyads received oil-linked resources outside any public
accountability. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1983.assessment:
Wartime conditions continued. NIOC oil revenues channeled to war effort and
bonyads without public disclosure. The Islamic Republic's constitution gave
the Supreme Leader ultimate control over oil revenues, but no public accounting
mechanism existed. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1984.assessment:
Wartime oil revenues managed by NIOC under total secrecy. Iran not EITI member
and had no mechanism for extractive revenue disclosure. IRGC received oil revenues
to fund military operations. No public accounting of oil sector revenues.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1985.assessment:
Oil price collapse in 1985-86 reduced NIOC revenues. Wartime management of reduced
oil revenues was handled without public disclosure. The oil sector remained entirely
outside public accountability. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1986.assessment:
Oil prices collapsed ($8/barrel in mid-1986). Iran's oil revenues fell dramatically
creating a fiscal crisis managed entirely without public disclosure. NIOC revenues
were diverted to war effort. Bonyad economy operated outside any public accounting.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1987.assessment:
War year. Tanker War affected oil exports. NIOC revenues and their allocation
remained state secrets. The government managed oil revenues under emergency
wartime conditions without any transparency to the public. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1988.assessment:
War ended August 1988. Oil sector began recovery. NIOC revenues would increase
in the post-war period. Revenues for war reconstruction were managed without public
disclosure. The bonyads had accumulated extensive oil-related assets. Iran not
EITI member.

sources.citation: Amirahmadi, H. Revolution and Economic Transition: The Iranian Experience (1990)

1989.assessment:
Post-war reconstruction began under Rafsanjani. NIOC revenues growing with oil
price recovery. The First Five-Year Development Plan (1989-1993) required oil
revenues but their allocation was not publicly disclosed. Bonyads received
oil-linked assets. IRGC's Khatam-ol-Anbia received oil infrastructure contracts.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1990.assessment:
Rafsanjani era. NIOC expanded exports. The Gulf War (1990-91) affected regional
oil dynamics. Iran's oil revenues were managed by NIOC and the Ministry of
Oil with no public disclosure. Bonyad-e Mostazafin controlled oil-linked assets.
IRGC Khatam-ol-Anbia built oil infrastructure. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1991.assessment:
Post-Gulf War oil market. Iran increased production. NIOC revenues growing.
Revenue allocation between government, bonyads, IRGC, and off-budget accounts
was opaque. No mechanisms for extractive transparency. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Ehteshami, A. After Khomeini: The Iranian Second Republic (1995)

1992.assessment:
NIOC revenues continued to be managed without public disclosure. Under Article 47
of the Constitution, IRGC was invited to participate in economic reconstruction,
granting it access to oil infrastructure revenues. Bonyads were explicitly exempt
from government auditing. Zero extractive transparency. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

1993.assessment:
NIOC oil revenues managed without public disclosure. The IRGC's Khatam-ol-Anbia
was receiving oil infrastructure construction contracts worth billions without
public procurement. The Second Development Plan required oil revenues but their
allocation was opaque. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

1994.assessment:
NIOC operating under Ministry of Oil. Revenue from oil was the dominant fiscal
source. IRGC Khatam-ol-Anbia expanding in oil sector. The Oil Stabilization Fund
would be created later (2000); in 1994 oil revenues went directly to government
without any buffer or transparency mechanism. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1995.assessment:
US-Iran ILSA sanctions (signed 1996) affected international oil companies'
investment in Iran. NIOC revenues maintained but international audit was
effectively impossible. Bonyad-e Mostazafin estimated to control over $10
billion in oil-related assets with zero public reporting. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1996.assessment:
ILSA signed (1996) restricting US and foreign investment in Iran's oil sector.
NIOC revenues maintained. The IRGC's Khatam-ol-Anbia received major oil sector
contracts without competitive bidding. All oil revenue allocation was opaque.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Maloney, S. Economy of the Islamic Republic (in Wright ed., 2000)

1997.assessment:
Khatami elected May 1997. Some discussions of oil sector reform but no transparency
improvements in practice. NIOC revenues managed opaquely. The Oil Stabilization
Fund (created 2000) was being conceptualized. IRGC and bonyads maintained
off-budget oil-related income. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

1998.assessment:
Oil prices collapsed to ~$12/barrel in 1998 creating a fiscal crisis. Government
managed oil revenue shortfall without public disclosure. IRGC and bonyads
continued receiving oil sector contracts without transparency. The fiscal crisis
revealed the opacity of oil revenue management. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

1999.assessment:
Oil prices began recovering. Iran's oil revenues recovering. The Oil Stabilization
Fund concept being developed. Khatami's reform agenda did not extend to extractive
sector transparency. IRGC Khatam-ol-Anbia operated off-budget and off-accounts
in the oil sector. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? The Structure of Power in the Islamic Republic (2000)

2000.assessment:
Oil Stabilization Fund created in 2000, which provided a slight improvement in
oil revenue tracking. However, the Fund itself operated with limited public
oversight. NIOC, IRGC oil operations, and bonyad oil assets remained opaque.
EITI was not yet established (founded 2003). Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2001.assessment:
Oil Stabilization Fund operational but with limited public disclosure. NIOC revenues
channeled through the Fund, government budget, and off-budget channels (IRGC, bonyads).
EITI not yet established. The IRGC's economic role in the oil sector continued to
expand under the patronage of the Supreme Leader.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2002.assessment:
Nuclear program revelations in 2002 added additional opacity to Iran's energy sector.
Oil Stabilization Fund provided some revenue tracking. However, NIOC detailed revenue
allocation, IRGC oil operations, and bonyad holdings in the oil sector were entirely
opaque. EITI was formally established in 2003 but Iran was not a founding participant.

sources.citation: EITI. About EITI history. 2003 founding.

2003.assessment:
EITI (Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative) formally established in 2003
at the World Summit on Sustainable Development. Iran did not join EITI and has
never applied for membership. NIOC remained opaque. IRGC operations in the oil
sector under Khatam-ol-Anbia were classified. Zero extractive transparency reform.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Countries.' EITI country status list. Iran not listed.

2004.assessment:
Iran has never joined EITI. NIOC is the state oil company with full production
control. IRGC Khatam-ol-Anbia received billions in oil sector contracts without
public disclosure. The Oil Stabilization Fund provided some revenue tracking
but detailed NIOC financials were not publicly released.

sources.citation: EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad elected June 2005. IRGC's economic role in oil sector expanded
dramatically under Ahmadinejad. NIOC revenues were increasingly diverted to
IRGC and Khamenei's SETAD holdings. Iran not EITI member. Oil Stabilization
Fund resources deployed without full public disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.
Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2006.assessment:
Ahmadinejad government continued diverting NIOC revenues to IRGC operations and
off-budget accounts. Oil revenues at record levels ($70-80/barrel). IRGC's Ansar
Bank and financial institutions created to manage oil-linked revenues off-budget.
Iran not EITI member; zero formal extractive transparency.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.
EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2007.assessment:
NIOC revenues at record highs ($70-90/barrel). IRGC received massive oil
infrastructure contracts (reported $4-5 billion/year through Khatam-ol-Anbia).
None of this was publicly disclosed. The Oil Stabilization Fund was largely
bypassed as Ahmadinejad distributed cash directly. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2008.assessment:
Oil prices peaked at $147/barrel in July 2008 before collapsing. Iran's NIOC
revenues reached record levels briefly. Ahmadinejad distributed oil revenues
through off-budget channels without disclosure. IRGC's economic holdings expanded
with high oil revenues. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2009.assessment:
Post-oil-price crash. Green Movement protests and NIOC revenue decline. IRGC
oil revenues managed without public disclosure. Sanctions beginning to tighten
on Iran's oil sector. National Development Fund was being planned to replace
the Oil Stabilization Fund with slightly better structure. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2010.assessment:
National Development Fund (NDF) established to replace Oil Stabilization Fund,
with better revenue management rules. However, the NDF's governance was not
transparent to the public. IRGC oil operations continued off-budget. Subsidy
reform reduced domestic oil subsidies. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2011.assessment:
NDF operational. NIOC required to deposit 20% of oil revenues into NDF. However,
the NDF's actual performance was non-transparent. Western sanctions intensifying
on oil sector. IRGC taking over oil export functions as sanctions circumvention.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2012.assessment:
Western sanctions dramatically reduced Iran's oil exports. IRGC took over
much of oil export management through front companies. Revenue from sanctions-busting
oil sales was entirely off-budget and opaque. The formal NDF and budget oil
revenues declined sharply. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Washington Institute. 'Iran's Oil Exports Are Vulnerable to Sanctions.' 2022.

2013.assessment:
Rouhani elected June 2013. Some signals of improved oil sector governance.
Rouhani sought to restore NIOC's primacy over IRGC in oil sector. However,
structural reforms required Supreme Leader approval that was not forthcoming.
JCPOA negotiations began. Iran not EITI member; no change in transparency.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2014.assessment:
Oil sanctions partially eased as JCPOA negotiations advanced. NIOC revenues
began recovering. Rouhani government introduced new oil contracts (IPC - Iran
Petroleum Contracts) to attract foreign investment with more transparent terms.
However, IRGC oil operations remained opaque. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2015.assessment:
JCPOA signed July 2015. Iran expected sanctions relief and increased oil exports.
The IPC contract framework promised some standardization. However, IRGC oil sector
role remained entrenched. Oil sanctions relief would come in 2016. Iran not EITI
member; no formal extractive transparency commitments.

sources.citation: EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2016.assessment:
JCPOA sanctions relief implemented January 2016. Iran's oil exports rapidly
increased. IPC contracts attracted some international oil companies. NIOC published
more information about oil production. However, IRGC revenue streams remained
entirely opaque. Khamenei's SETAD organization received oil-linked revenues.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Rystad Energy. 'Iran's growing political unrest exposing cracks in regime's oil revenue model.' 2022.

2017.assessment:
Oil production at approximately 3.8 million barrels/day. IPC contracts with
European companies included some reporting requirements. However, the IRGC-linked
Khatam-ol-Anbia maintained a parallel oil infrastructure operation outside any
public accountability. NDF received oil revenues but without full transparency.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2018.assessment:
US withdrew from JCPOA (May 2018); reimposed sanctions. European IPC contractors
(Total, Shell) withdrew. IRGC resumed control of oil export management through
covert channels. Currency crisis (rial collapsed 80%+). Oil revenues managed
through opaque IRGC banking networks. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Washington Institute. 'Iran's Oil Exports Are Vulnerable to Sanctions.' 2022.

2019.assessment:
Maximum US sanctions on Iran's oil sector. NIOC's official exports collapsed.
IRGC managed covert oil exports to China, Syria, Venezuela using front companies
and ship-to-ship transfers. Revenue from covert oil sales was entirely off-budget
and opaque. Iran not EITI member. Iran International documented IRGC oil revenues.

sources.citation: Iran International. 'Khamenei, IRGC boost share of Iran's oil revenues and state assets.' 2025.
EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 and maximum sanctions combined to depress oil revenues. IRGC maintained
covert exports. The formal NIOC budget contribution to government was minimal.
IRGC banking networks and Khamenei's SETAD accumulated oil revenues off-budget.
Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Clingendael. 'Beyond the IRGC: The rise of Iran's military-bonyad complex.' 2023.

2021.assessment:
Raisi elected August 2021. IRGC's financial empire expanded under Raisi. Reports
suggested IRGC controlled 50%+ of Iran's oil exports. Khamenei's SETAD received
a larger share of oil revenues. The National Development Fund was depleted without
public disclosure. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Iran International. 'Khamenei, IRGC boost share of Iran's oil revenues and state assets.' 2025.
EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2022.assessment:
Iran's oil exports recovered partially as sanctions enforcement loosened under
Biden administration. Revenues increased but were managed through IRGC and SETAD
networks with zero public disclosure. Mahsa Amini protests; security spending
funded from off-budget oil revenues. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Iran International. 'Khamenei, IRGC boost share of Iran's oil revenues and state assets.' 2025.

2023.assessment:
Iran's oil exports continued recovering (to approximately 1.5-1.8 million barrels/day).
IRGC maintained control over covert export channels. Revenues from oil were not
publicly disclosed. The 2023 budget allocated oil revenues without detailed public
accounting of production volumes or prices. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Iran International. 'Khamenei, IRGC boost share of Iran's oil revenues and state assets.' 2025.
EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member.

2024.assessment:
Iran's oil exports continued at elevated levels despite sanctions. IRGC share of
oil revenues increasing. Iran International (April 2025) reported Khamenei and
IRGC boosting their share of oil revenues and state assets. Raisi killed May 2024;
Pezeshkian elected but no change in IRGC oil control. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Iran International. 'Khamenei, IRGC boost share of Iran's oil revenues and state assets.' April 2025.
Iran Focus. 'Iran's 2025 Budget: Massive Oil Revenues for Military, State Assets Transferred to IRGC.'

2025.assessment:
Iran's 2025 budget allocated massive oil revenues to military/IRGC. NIOC was
nominally responsible for production but IRGC controlled export revenues. Pre-regime
change year: IRGC financial empire at maximum extent. Zero extractive transparency;
revenues used to fund both military and Supreme Leader holdings. Iran not EITI member.

sources.citation: Iran Focus. 'Iran's 2025 Budget: Massive Oil Revenues for Military, State Assets Transferred to IRGC.'

2026.assessment:
Regime change year 2026. IRGC financial empire collapsed or was disrupted with
regime change. NIOC's institutional status unclear in transition. Oil revenues
and their management were in flux. Any post-regime transparency in extractive
sector would require joining EITI and implementing new governance frameworks.
Iran not EITI member throughout Islamic Republic period.

sources.citation: EITI. Country status list. Iran not a member (as of 2026).

1960.assessment:
Under Mohammad Reza Shah, Iran maintained a formal legal system based on a Civil Code
(1928), Penal Code, and the 1906-07 Constitutional framework. Laws were published in
the Official Gazette (Ruznama-ye Rasmi). However, SAVAK (secret police, founded 1957)
operated outside the legal framework with authority to detain, interrogate, and imprison
without judicial oversight. Political cases bypassed civilian courts and were tried by
military tribunals. Legal transparency existed nominally on paper but enforcement was
arbitrary for political matters.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press
US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices, Iran (historical)

1961.assessment:
Pahlavi legal framework in place. Formal civil and commercial law accessible through
Official Gazette. Judiciary functioned for non-political matters. SAVAK continued
extraterritorial operations beyond judicial oversight. Political prisoners subject to
military tribunal rather than civil courts. Arbitrary detention of political opponents
was common. National Front opposition parties faced legal harassment.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1962.assessment:
White Revolution preparations begin. Shah ruled by decree in 1961-62 after dissolving
parliament. Legal framework disrupted during this transitional period. Laws published
but executive decree power increased. SAVAK operations continued outside legal
accountability. Land reform and other White Revolution elements enacted by royal decree
rather than through parliamentary legislation, reducing formal legal accessibility.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press
Katouzian, H. The Political Economy of Modern Iran (1981)

1963.assessment:
White Revolution referendum of January 1963. Khomeini arrested June 1963 after Ashura
uprising crackdown. Military courts tried protesters. Legal framework formally restored
with new parliament but executive decree power remained high. SAVAK arrests of Islamist
and nationalist opposition without judicial warrant were widespread. Laws published in
Official Gazette but enforcement deeply arbitrary for political matters.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1964.assessment:
Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) of 1964 gave US military personnel immunity from
Iranian courts, a major demonstration of legal system subordination to political
interests. Khomeini exiled November 1964 after protesting SOFA. Civil legal framework
continued to function for commercial and family matters. SAVAK operations remained
outside judicial oversight. Political legal transparency severely limited.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press
Encyclopaedia Iranica, SAVAK entry

1965.assessment:
Prime Minister Mansur assassinated January 1965 by Islamist militants. Security
crackdown followed. SAVAK expanded its operations and surveillance. Official legal
framework published and accessible for civil matters. Military courts handled political
cases. Laws against political opposition broadly and arbitrarily enforced. Freedom of
assembly and political organizing effectively criminalized despite formal legal
protections in constitution.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1966.assessment:
Stable Pahlavi era with formal legal infrastructure. Civil and commercial courts
functioning for non-political matters. Laws published in Official Gazette. However,
SAVAK presence pervasive; political dissidents subject to arbitrary arrest and military
tribunal. National security laws used broadly. Legal transparency good for commercial
matters, deeply deficient for political and civil rights matters.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1967.assessment:
Revised Family Protection Law (1967) modernized family law, demonstrating some legal
reform capability. Official Gazette published laws and regulations. Civil courts
accessible for commercial and family disputes. However, the political legal framework
remained opaque and SAVAK operated with impunity. Military courts for political
offenses. No freedom of information framework existed.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1968.assessment:
Guerrilla groups (Mojahedin-e Khalq, Fedayeen) beginning to form. SAVAK operations
intensified against leftist and Islamist opposition. Special Courts for security cases
operated outside normal judicial procedures. Published legal framework continued to
exist for ordinary matters. Academic and intellectual freedom restricted by Press
Law enforcement. Legal transparency for civil matters moderate; for political matters
extremely limited.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1969.assessment:
Continuing Pahlavi era with SAVAK-controlled political sphere. Legal framework for
commercial and civil matters published and broadly accessible. Courts functional for
non-political matters. Political opposition faced extrajudicial detention by SAVAK.
Press Law (1955) enforced to restrict publication. No freedom of information
legislation. Legal transparency adequate for commercial activities but severely
deficient for political/civil rights matters.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1970.assessment:
Oil boom era beginning. Legal infrastructure moderately developed for commercial
and contract law given increasing foreign investment. However, SAVAK torture and
arbitrary detention documented by Amnesty International. Political prisoners tried
by military courts. Legislative framework published in Official Gazette but political
enforcement deeply arbitrary. Dual-track justice system: commercial courts
relatively transparent, political courts completely opaque.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Report on Torture, Iran (1976)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1971.assessment:
2,500-year Persian Empire celebrations at Persepolis. State projection of legal
modernity internationally. Domestically, SAVAK operations against Fedayeen guerrillas
intensified following Siahkal uprising (February 1971). Laws published and accessible
for commercial activity. Military courts handled all political cases. No freedom of
information. Legal transparency moderately adequate for commerce, severely deficient
for political rights.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1972.assessment:
NIOC took full control of oil operations from the Consortium in 1972-1973. Legal
transparency in oil sector minimal. SAVAK operations extensive; torture at Evin Prison
documented. Laws published in Official Gazette. Commercial courts accessible. Amnesty
International continued documenting arbitrary detention and torture. Dual legal system:
civil law functional and accessible; security law opaque and arbitrary.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Report on Torture, Iran (1976)
Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999), University of California Press

1973.assessment:
Oil price shock following OPEC embargo. Iran oil revenues surge. Growing repression of
political opposition amid prosperity. SAVAK expanded personnel and operations. Laws
and regulations published but enforcement increasingly arbitrary. New press censorship
regulations tightened. Political dissidents faced torture and trial by military tribunal.
Commercial law moderately transparent; political/civil rights law effectively non-existent.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1974.assessment:
International criticism of Iran's human rights situation intensified. International
Commission of Jurists and Amnesty International published critical reports on SAVAK
torture and arbitrary detention. However, official legal framework remained published
and accessible for non-political matters. Military tribunals handled political cases
without due process. Shah justified repression as necessary for modernization.
Legal transparency for commerce moderate; for political rights essentially absent.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Report on Torture, Iran (1974-1976)
International Commission of Jurists, Iran report (1976)

1975.assessment:
Rastakhiz single-party system created March 1975. Political pluralism effectively
banned — citizens required to join the single party or face consequences. This further
eroded legal transparency for political participation. Laws still published in
Official Gazette. Commercial and civil courts functional. However, the single-party
decree represented a shift toward more authoritarian legal governance. SAVAK
operations continued against remaining opposition.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1976.assessment:
Under international pressure (Carter administration, 1977 incoming), some nominal
gestures toward legal reform were made in 1976-1977. Red Cross visits to prisons
permitted. However, SAVAK torture continued. Legal framework published but
arbitrarily enforced. Political prisoners still tried by military courts. Amnesty
International estimated 2,200-3,000 political prisoners. No fundamental change
to the arbitrary enforcement of political law.

sources.citation: Amnesty International Annual Report 1976-77, Iran section
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1977.assessment:
Carter administration's human rights pressure led to some limited reforms: Red Cross
prison visits, civilian lawyers permitted in some political cases, some releases. The
Iran Committee for the Defense of Freedom and Human Rights (led by Bazargan, Matin-Daftari)
wrote open letters demanding legal rights. Despite these gestures, SAVAK continued
operating outside legal framework, military courts remained primary venue for
political cases. Legal transparency marginally improving but still deeply deficient.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press
Amnesty International Annual Report 1977-78, Iran section

1978.assessment:
Revolutionary year. Martial law imposed September 1978 (Black Friday massacre).
Legal system effectively collapsed under revolutionary pressure. Military courts
overwhelmed. SAVAK conducting mass arrests. Courts no longer functioning normally.
Laws effectively suspended under martial law in major cities. By end of 1978 the
legal framework was disintegrating as state authority collapsed. Transition from
arbitrary enforcement to near-complete legal breakdown.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press
Human Rights Watch, Iran reports (1979)

1979.assessment:
Islamic Revolution of February 1979. Khomeini installed revolutionary courts
(dadgah-e enqelab) under Sadegh Khalkhali. Thousands executed without due process
in first months. The previous legal framework was discarded. Islamic law (Sharia)
declared the basis of all legislation under velayat-e faqih doctrine. Legal
transparency collapsed: revolutionary courts operated in secret, no defense lawyers,
no published procedures, sentences immediate. The entire accessible legal framework
of the Pahlavi era was dismantled and replaced with ad hoc revolutionary justice.

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999), University of California Press
Human Rights Watch, Iran: Revolution and Its Aftermath (1980)
Amnesty International, Law and Human Rights in the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980)

1980.assessment:
New Islamic Constitution ratified December 1979 established velayat-e faqih (Rule of
the Jurist) as supreme legal principle. Iran-Iraq War began September 1980. Cultural
Revolution closed universities and purged legal institutions. Revolutionary courts
continued operating with no procedural standards. The published legal framework of the
Islamic Republic existed nominally but revolutionary justice superseded it. Baha'i
and other minorities faced systematic legal persecution. No meaningful accessible
legal framework for citizens.

sources.citation: Amnesty International Annual Report 1980-81, Iran
Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999), University of California Press

1981.assessment:
Mass executions of Mojahedin-e Khalq and leftist opposition following June 1981
political crisis (Bani-Sadr impeachment). Revolutionary courts conducted summary
executions. Thousands arrested and executed. No due process, no defense lawyers,
no right of appeal. Iran-Iraq War emergency conditions justified suspension of normal
legal protections. Legal framework effectively non-existent for political matters.
Revolutionary courts operated outside any published legal procedure.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)
Abrahamian, E. Iran Between Two Revolutions (1982), Princeton University Press

1982.assessment:
Continuing political executions throughout 1982. Iran regained Khorramshahr from Iraq
(May 1982) — war continued. Legal institutions purged of non-Islamist judges. New
judicial system established based on Islamic law with clerical judges (mojtaheds).
Criminal code revised to implement Islamic punishments (hudud). Published in Official
Gazette but the substantive framework was inaccessible to most citizens unfamiliar
with Islamic jurisprudence. No freedom of information. Arbitrary enforcement for
political and social matters remained extreme.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)
Bassiri Tabrizi, A. Iran's Constitution (2016)

1983.assessment:
Tudeh Party (communist) dissolved March 1983; leaders arrested and forced confessions
broadcast on state television. Systematic elimination of remaining political opposition
through revolutionary courts. Iran-Iraq War continuing. Islamic penal code
implemented. Legal framework nominally published but no genuine legal recourse
available for political matters. Judicial independence completely subordinated
to clerical revolutionary authority under velayat-e faqih.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)
Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999), University of California Press

1984.assessment:
Iran-Iraq War at peak intensity. Press Law of 1979 enforced to close opposition
publications. Judiciary fully Islamicized under Chief Justice Ardebili. Revolutionary
courts handling political and security cases with no due process. Published laws
in Official Gazette but Islamic legal framework inaccessible and opaque to most.
No freedom of information framework. Political legal transparency at absolute minimum.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)

1985.assessment:
Iran-Contra scandal connections (revealed 1986-87) — Iran involved in arms-for-hostages
deals conducted entirely outside legal frameworks. Domestically, Amnesty International
documented continuing pattern of arbitrary arrests, torture, and executions. Laws
published but inaccessible to average citizens. Revolutionary courts continued.
No rule of law in any meaningful sense for political matters. Clerical domination
of judiciary complete.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)

1986.assessment:
Continuing Iran-Iraq War with heavy casualties. Revolutionary courts remaining active.
Systematic detention and execution of political opponents. Evin Prison torture
documented by UN Special Rapporteur. Islamic penal code enforced. No freedom of
information. Published legal framework inaccessible and opaque. Arbitrary
enforcement at all levels of political and social law. No accessible framework
for citizens seeking legal recourse.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Human Rights in Iran, 1986 report
Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)

1987.assessment:
Amnesty International published major report documenting Iranian state violations:
torture, extrajudicial killings, arbitrary detention, execution of minors. Iran-Iraq
War continuing. Executions of political prisoners documented systematically. Legal
framework nominally in place but with zero practical transparency or accessibility
for political matters. Revolutionary courts the dominant institution for security cases.
No genuine rule of law.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Violations of Human Rights (1987)
UN Special Rapporteur on Human Rights in Iran, 1987 report

1988.assessment:
Ceasefire with Iraq July 1988. Massacre of political prisoners (MKO and leftists)
in August-September 1988 — approximately 3,000-5,000 executed in secret by
special "death committees" operating entirely outside any legal framework.
Khomeini issued fatwa authorizing executions. This represents the nadir of legal
transparency and rule of law in Iran's history. Families not notified; bodies
buried in mass graves. Absolute zero on any legal transparency or framework.

sources.citation: Amnesty International, Iran: Prisoners Executed After Unfair Trials (1989)
Human Rights Watch, 'No Safe Haven': Iran's Global Assassination Campaign (1992)
Abrahamian, E. Tortured Confessions (1999), University of California Press

1989.assessment:
Khomeini died June 1989. Khamenei appointed Supreme Leader. Rafsanjani elected
president. Constitutional revision June 1989 streamlined governance. Some
stabilization of legal framework under Rafsanjani's pragmatic approach. Revolutionary
courts continued but with somewhat less intensity. Post-war reconstruction
required some legal predictability for contracts. However, fundamental arbitrary
enforcement of political law continued. No freedom of information. Still
no accessible framework for political rights.

sources.citation: Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1990)
Amnesty International Annual Report 1990, Iran section

1990.assessment:
Rafsanjani reconstruction era. Legal framework for commercial contracts somewhat
improved to facilitate post-war reconstruction. Investment laws published. However,
Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and bonyads (revolutionary foundations)
operated outside normal legal accountability. Intelligence Ministry (VEVAK) replaced
SAVAK but with similar arbitrary powers. Assassinations of dissidents abroad
(Chain Murders) conducted outside any legal framework. Political legal transparency
minimal.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1991, Iran
Bakhash, S. The Reign of the Ayatollahs (1990)

1991.assessment:
Shapour Bakhtiar (former PM) assassinated in Paris by Iranian agents August 1991.
Chain murders of dissidents abroad continued. Domestically, some commercial legal
predictability improved under Rafsanjani reconstruction program. Published legal
framework for business contracts and property. However, intelligence services
operated outside legal framework. No freedom of information. Political legal
transparency remained minimal.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1992, Iran

1992.assessment:
Mykonos restaurant assassinations (Berlin, September 1992) — Iranian agents
killed Kurdish opposition leaders. German courts later convicted Iranian agents
and found Iranian state responsibility (1997). Domestically, commercial legal
infrastructure continued improving. Laws published in Official Gazette. However,
security services operated outside any published legal framework. Bonyads and
IRGC outside legal accountability. Political transparency minimal.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1993, Iran
Mykonos Trial records, Berlin (1997)

1993.assessment:
Rafsanjani reelected for second term. Economic liberalization continued requiring
more predictable commercial legal framework. Investment regulations published.
International business engagement required accessible contract law. However,
fundamental political legal transparency remained absent. Judicial system subordinated
to clerical authority. Intelligence services unaccountable. No freedom of information
legislation. Press Law enforced to suppress criticism.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1994, Iran

1994.assessment:
AMIA bombing Buenos Aires July 1994 — Argentina later charged Iranian officials
with directing the attack. Chain murders of intellectuals and dissidents continuing
inside Iran. Commercial legal framework moderately predictable. Official Gazette
published laws. But pattern of extrajudicial operations by intelligence ministry
(VEVAK) continued. Legal framework existed on paper but arbitrary enforcement
for political matters remained the norm. Minimal practical legal transparency.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1995, Iran

1995.assessment:
US imposed comprehensive sanctions on Iran (Executive Order 12959, May 1995).
Domestic legal framework continued publishing laws in Official Gazette. Commercial
courts moderately accessible. However, sanctions context and security apparatus
created additional legal opacity. VEVAK continued chain murders. Press censorship
enforced. No FOI legislation. Political legal framework remained inaccessible
and arbitrarily enforced.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1996, Iran

1996.assessment:
Final year of Rafsanjani presidency. Some commercial legal infrastructure in place.
Chain murders of intellectuals and writers continued. Saidi Sirjani (writer) died
in custody 1994, Faraj Sarkohi disappeared 1996 (VEVAK). No FOI framework.
Published legal code for commercial matters but political matters arbitrarily decided
by intelligence services and revolutionary courts. Judiciary not independent.
Legal transparency: minimal/decree-based for most matters.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1997, Iran

1997.assessment:
Khatami elected president May 1997 on reform platform including rule of law.
Tehran Spring began: new newspapers, civil society discussions, legal reform
rhetoric. Khatami advocated "rule of law" and civil society. Published legal reforms
proposed. However, conservative judiciary blocked implementation. VEVAK still
operating under Khamenei's direction. Chain murders peaked in November 1998
(Pouyandeh, Mokhtari). Legal transparency began improving marginally under
Khatami's rhetoric but institutional change minimal.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 1998, Iran
Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1998.assessment:
Chain murders of November-December 1998: Dariush Forouhar and Parvaneh Foruhar
(political leaders), Mohammad Mokhtari and Mohammad Pouyandeh (writers) assassinated
by VEVAK agents. Khatami ordered investigation; VEVAK Deputy Minister Said Emami
arrested (later died in custody). This momentarily increased legal accountability but
demonstrated the fundamental opacity: intelligence services operating as death squads.
Published laws existed but enforcement entirely arbitrary. Mykonos verdict in Germany
(April 1997) already confirmed Iranian state responsibility for assassinations.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Iran Chain Murders (1999)
Buchta, W. Who Rules Iran? (2000)

1999.assessment:
July 1999 student uprising at Tehran University; dormitory attack by Ansar-e Hizbollah
(hardline vigilantes) killed one student. Khatami unable to protect protesters from
security forces. Legal reform parliament (Majles) elected February 2000 with reformist
majority. However, Guardian Council and judiciary under Khamenei's appointees blocked
all legal reforms. Chain murder investigations stalled. Legal framework for commercial
matters accessible; political legal framework opaque and arbitrary.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2000, Iran

2000.assessment:
April 2000: judiciary ordered mass closure of reformist newspapers (over 20 closed
within weeks). Reformist Majles elected but Guardian Council began systematic
vetting. Press Law used as weapon against reform. Emadeddin Baghi (journalist)
imprisoned. Legal framework for political participation effectively shut down by
conservative judiciary using existing laws selectively. Khatami's rule of law project
defeated by parallel conservative legal institutions. Legal transparency at minimal
level — rule by decree within formal but inaccessible framework.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2001, Iran
Committee to Protect Journalists, Iran report 2000

2001.assessment:
Khatami reelected June 2001 despite reformist frustration. Abdol-Karim Soroush and
other reformist intellectuals harassed and forced into exile. Special Court for the
Clergy operated with complete opacity (not subject to normal judicial review). Press
courts operated outside normal judicial procedures. WJP not yet published.
Legal framework: laws exist and published in Official Gazette but conservative
parallel judicial structures effectively nullify legal accessibility for civil society.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2002, Iran
Amnesty International Annual Report 2002, Iran

2002.assessment:
Hashem Aghajari (professor) sentenced to death for "apostasy" in a speech (November
2002) — sentence later commuted after student protests. This case illustrated the
extreme arbitrariness of the legal framework: death sentences for speech. Legal
transparency for ordinary commercial matters moderate; for political and religious
matters completely arbitrary. No freedom of information. Guardian Council vetted
parliamentary candidates. Reformist efforts to pass transparency legislation blocked.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2003, Iran
Freedom House Freedom in the World 2003, Iran

2003.assessment:
Canadian-Iranian journalist Zahra Kazemi killed in Evin Prison July 2003 during
interrogation. Case revealed impunity of security forces within legal system. Court
case against VEVAK agent acquitted despite evidence. Iranian Nobel Peace Prize
laureate Shirin Ebadi (2003) documented legal system failures. Reformist Majles
blocked by Guardian Council. Laws published but selectively enforced by conservative
judiciary aligned with Supreme Leader. Minimal legal transparency for political matters.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Iran: Zahra Kazemi Case (2004)
Ebadi, S. Iran Awakening (2006)

2004.assessment:
Guardian Council disqualified 2,000+ reformist candidates for February 2004
parliamentary elections, effectively ending reformist period. Conservative-dominated
7th Majles elected. Legal transparency regressed as conservative forces consolidated
control of all state institutions. Nuclear program became central issue; government
argued national security justified increased secrecy. No FOI legislation. Published
laws exist but civil society unable to use legal channels for accountability.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2005, Iran
Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005, Iran

2005.assessment:
Ahmadinejad elected president June 2005. IRGC's political and economic role expanded
rapidly under Ahmadinejad. IRGC entities exempt from normal legal accountability.
Hardline judiciary continued blocking transparency measures. Conservative press law
enforcement intensified. Nuclear secrecy expanded. No FOI legislation. Legal
framework entirely decree-based for political matters. IRGC expansion outside
commercial law enforcement further reduced legal transparency.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2006, Iran

2006.assessment:
UN Security Council sanctions begun (UNSC Resolution 1737, December 2006) over
nuclear program. Domestic legal opacity deepened around nuclear and IRGC activities.
Women's rights activists (One Million Signatures Campaign) faced prosecution under
broadly interpreted security laws. Constitutional right to defense lawyer frequently
denied in security cases. Published laws exist but accessible only to those aligned
with the system. No FOI legislation. Minimal legal transparency.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2007, Iran
Amnesty International Annual Report 2007, Iran

2007.assessment:
Ahmadinejad second year. Intensified crackdown on civil society. Trade union leaders,
journalists, academics arrested under security law provisions. Lawyers defending
political prisoners themselves prosecuted. Nobel Laureate Shirin Ebadi's Human Rights
Defenders Center closed by authorities. WJP first edition published (2008 covers 2007)
— Iran not included in inaugural edition. No FOI legislation. Laws published but
legal process opaque and arbitrary for political matters.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2008, Iran
Amnesty International Annual Report 2008, Iran

2008.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index first published in 2008 but Iran was not included in the
inaugural edition. Continued Ahmadinejad era repression. Roxana Saberi (dual US-Iranian
journalist) arrested January 2009 (in this period). UNSC Additional sanctions
(Resolution 1803, March 2008). Nuclear-related legal opacity deepened. Civil society
organizations systematically closed. Multiple UN Special Rapporteur reports on
arbitrary detention. Legal framework on paper but entirely opaque in practice for
political matters.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2009, Iran
UN Human Rights Council, Report of Special Rapporteur on Iran (2008)

2009.assessment:
Green Movement uprising following disputed June 2009 presidential election. Massive
crackdown: thousands arrested, protesters killed by security forces (Neda Agha-Soltan
June 20, 2009). Revolutionary courts held mass trials in August-September 2009.
Defense lawyers denied access to clients. Confessions broadcast on state TV.
Legal process completely abandoned for Green Movement detainees. The post-election
period represents a major regression in legal transparency and rule of law.
WJP 2009/10 edition did not include Iran.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, 'After the Islamic Revolution: New Crisis for Iran's Greens' (2010)
Amnesty International, Iran: Election Contested Amid Crackdown (2009)

2010.assessment:
Post-Green Movement repression continuing. Green Movement leaders Mousavi and Karroubi
eventually placed under house arrest (2011). Key reformist lawyers and journalists
imprisoned. Mohammad Ali Dadkhah (human rights lawyer) sentenced to 9 years.
Internet filtering intensified. UNSC Resolution 1929 (June 2010) additional nuclear
sanctions. Legal transparency remains at minimal/decree level. WJP 2010 edition
— Iran not included. Judicial proceedings for political cases completely opaque.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2011, Iran
Amnesty International Annual Report 2011, Iran

2011.assessment:
Mousavi and Karroubi placed under house arrest (February 2011) without charge or
trial — illustrating the complete irrelevance of legal process in political matters.
UN Special Rapporteur Ahmad Shaheed appointed March 2011 to monitor Iran's human
rights (Iran refused access). Arab Spring protests suppressed violently in Iran.
WJP not published in 2011 (gap year). Legal transparency at minimal decree level.
Published laws inaccessible for accountability purposes. No FOI legislation.

sources.citation: UN Human Rights Council, Special Rapporteur Ahmad Shaheed, Iran Reports (2011-2019)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2012, Iran

2012.assessment:
US and EU comprehensive sanctions intensified in 2012. SWIFT banking system access
cut off. Economic pressure but no legal transparency improvement. IRGC-linked
entities expanded operations outside normal legal accountability. UN Special Rapporteur
Shaheed unable to access Iran — reporting from exile sources. Journalist and blogger
arrests continued. WJP not published 2012 (gap year). Legal transparency minimal.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur Shaheed, Report on Iran, March 2013
Human Rights Watch World Report 2013, Iran

2013.assessment:
Rouhani elected June 2013 on reform and moderation platform. Preliminary nuclear
agreement with P5+1. WJP resumed publication in 2013 (resumed after gap years 2011-12)
and began including Iran — Factor 3 (Open Government) estimated at approximately 0.29
(29/100) based on 2014 first available data point. Rouhani administration announced
intentions for legal reform and greater transparency. However, conservative judiciary
and security apparatus unchanged. Hassan Rouhani's government modestly improved legal
rhetoric but institutional transparency change minimal in 2013.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2014, Iran

2014.assessment:
WJP 2014 data available for Iran — Factor 3 (Open Government) estimated at
approximately 0.29 (29/100). Nuclear negotiations under JCPOA framework. Rouhani
reforms: some journalist releases, ministerial accountability rhetoric. However,
judiciary (under Khamenei appointee Larijani) unchanged. Political prisoners
continued. Iran ranked in bottom tier globally on open government. No FOI legislation
passed. Laws published in Official Gazette but legal accountability mechanisms
remain inaccessible. Minimal rule-by-decree framework.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran
Human Rights Watch World Report 2015, Iran

2015.assessment:
JCPOA nuclear deal signed July 2015. Rouhani administration's diplomatic achievement.
Some improvement in government's willingness to discuss public policy. WJP Factor 3
for Iran estimated approximately 0.30 (30/100). Despite JCPOA success, domestic legal
transparency unchanged: political prisoners (Jason Rezaian, journalists, lawyers)
continued to be held. Revolutionary courts inaccessible. Conservative judiciary
enforced strict social laws. Published legal framework exists but inaccessible for
accountability. Marginal improvement from pre-Rouhani era.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2016, Iran

2016.assessment:
Jason Rezaian (Washington Post journalist) released January 2016 as part of prisoner
swap. Post-JCPOA partial sanctions relief. Rouhani reformists won parliamentary
elections (February 2016). WJP Factor 3 for Iran estimated approximately 0.30
(30/100). Despite some positive signals, domestic legal transparency improvements
were limited. New parliament had reformist majority but judiciary remained conservative.
Dual citizenship holders arrested as "hostages" — pattern of arbitrary detention
for leverage rather than legal process. Minimal rule-by-decree with marginal
improvements under Rouhani.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2017, Iran

2017.assessment:
Rouhani reelected May 2017. Iran ranked approximately 80th out of 113 countries
in WJP overall Rule of Law Index 2017-2018, but Factor 3 (Open Government) was
much lower — estimated 0.30 (30/100) given Iran's poor scores on right to
information and civic participation sub-components. Trump administration began
pulling back from JCPOA. Domestic legal transparency: political arrests of civil
society, labor activists, environmentalists. No FOI legislation. Judiciary under
Khamenei appointee Raisi (appointed 2019). Minimal rule-by-decree.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-2018, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2018, Iran

2018.assessment:
US withdrew from JCPOA May 2018 (Trump). Maximum pressure sanctions campaign
reimposed. Iranian economy under severe stress. November 2018 gas price protests
followed by internet shutdown (November 2019 most severe). Environmentalists arrested
on spurious espionage charges. Khashoggi murder (Saudi, October 2018) highlighted
regional pattern of authoritarian legal impunity. Iran's WJP Factor 3 estimated
~0.29 (declining slightly under sanctions pressure). Legal transparency declining
as government cited security threats to justify more opacity.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2019, Iran

2019.assessment:
November 2019: fuel price increase protests resulted in catastrophic crackdown —
internet shut down for ~5 days nationally (first total shutdown), security forces
killed approximately 1,500 protesters according to Reuters investigation. Shooting
of plane PS752 (Ukrainian Airlines) January 2020 was covered up initially. These
events demonstrate systematic use of state power to suppress legal accountability.
WJP Factor 3 estimated approximately 0.28 (28/100) — declining under maximum
pressure period. No transparency on protests; no accountability for killings.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated)
Reuters, Special Report: Iran's leader ordered crackdown on protests (2019)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2020, Iran

2020.assessment:
PS752 cover-up revealed January 2020 — Iran admitted shooting down Ukrainian Airlines
plane after days of denial, killing 176 people. IRGC acted outside legal framework.
COVID-19 pandemic used to justify additional opacity and restrictions. Parliamentary
elections February 2020 — Guardian Council disqualified most reformist candidates.
Iran ranked 128th globally in WJP Open Government (Factor 3) — estimated score 0.27
(27/100). Maximum pressure sanctions continued. Legal transparency at very low level.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated ~0.27)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2021, Iran

2021.assessment:
Raisi elected president June 2021 (former judiciary chief, on US sanctions list for
role in 1988 prison massacres). Major regression expected in legal transparency.
Raisi's election marked by lowest voter turnout in Islamic Republic history (49%).
Guardian Council barred most candidates. WJP Factor 3 for Iran estimated ~0.27 (27/100)
— Iran consistently ranked in bottom tier globally (126th-128th of ~140 countries).
No FOI legislation. Legal framework published but inaccessible for accountability.
Continued pattern of arbitrary detention of dual nationals and civil society.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated ~0.27)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2022, Iran

2022.assessment:
Mahsa (Zhina) Amini killed in morality police custody September 16, 2022. Nationwide
Woman Life Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi) uprising. Security forces killed 500+
protesters. Internet shutdowns (Cloudflare data documented systematic regional
shutdowns). Executions of protesters began. Show trials broadcast on state TV.
Defense lawyers arrested. Legal accountability mechanisms completely suspended.
WJP Factor 3 for Iran estimated ~0.26 (26/100) — declining under Raisi/Mahsa
crackdown. This represents the most severe legal transparency crisis since 1988.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated ~0.26)
Human Rights Watch, Iran: Uprising Crackdown (2022-2023)
Amnesty International, Iran: Unlawful Use of Force Against Protests (2022)

2023.assessment:
Post-uprising consolidation. Executions of protest leaders continued into 2023
(Mohammad Ghobadlou, Majid Kazemi, Saleh Mirhashemi executed). More show trials.
Iran ranked 126th out of 142 countries in WJP Rule of Law Index 2023 on Open
Government (Factor 3) — estimated score approximately 0.26 (26/100). No freedom
of information legislation. Lawyers defending political prisoners prosecuted.
Internet restrictions systematized. Legal framework exists on paper but offers
zero practical access to accountability for citizens opposing the government.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran — Iran ranked 126th/142
Human Rights Watch World Report 2024, Iran
Amnesty International Annual Report 2023, Iran

2024.assessment:
Raisi killed in helicopter crash May 2024. Pezeshkian (reformist) elected July 2024
in runoff. Some cautious optimism but structural legal transparency unchanged.
Judiciary remained under Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei (Khamenei appointee). Dual
nationals continued to be held as hostages. Iran attacked Israel directly (April
2024 drone attack) — national security framing further justified legal opacity.
WJP Factor 3 estimated ~0.26 (26/100). No FOI legislation. Minimal rule-by-decree
framework unchanged despite presidential transition.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated ~0.26)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2025, Iran

2025.assessment:
Pezeshkian's first full year as president. Resumed JCPOA-adjacent nuclear diplomacy
with Europe and indirect US talks. Some minor prisoner releases. However, structural
legal transparency unchanged: velayat-e faqih doctrine, conservative judiciary,
IRGC outside legal accountability, no FOI legislation, internet filtering, arbitrary
detention of political opponents. WJP Factor 3 estimated ~0.26 (26/100). Iran remains
in bottom 10% globally on legal transparency. Pre-regime change era — system
fundamentally unchanged.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025, Factor 3: Open Government, Iran (estimated ~0.26)
Human Rights Watch World Report 2025, Iran

2026.assessment:
Regime change year marker. No data available for 2026. The Islamic Republic legal
framework — velayat-e faqih, conservative judiciary, no FOI, arbitrary enforcement
for political matters — was in place throughout the Islamic Republic era. The
nature of legal transparency in any post-regime period is unknown at time of data
collection.

1960.assessment:
Under the Pahlavi regime in 1960, press freedom was significantly constrained but not
completely absent. SAVAK (the secret police, established 1957) monitored and censored
media. Multiple newspapers existed but operated under implicit self-censorship; direct
criticism of the Shah was forbidden. The 1960 period preceded the White Revolution
and featured some political pluralism under pressure.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.
PBS Wide Angle. 'Politics and the Press in Iran: Under the Pahlavis.'

1961.assessment:
Brief political opening under PM Ali Amini (1961-62) allowed slightly more press
activity. The National Front and other parties had some press presence. However,
SAVAK surveillance continued and critical journalism remained risky. The Shah's
White Revolution program was being developed; media criticism of it was suppressed.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1962.assessment:
Press freedom tightened as the Shah consolidated the White Revolution program.
Political opposition parties were suppressed. SAVAK enforced censorship through
directives to newspaper editors. Self-censorship was pervasive. Critical voices
were jailed or exiled.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1963.assessment:
The June 1963 uprisings (led in part by Khomeini) were violently suppressed and
media coverage was tightly controlled. Khomeini's arrest and subsequent exile in
1964 were underreported or framed by state-aligned media. SAVAK increased
censorship activity following unrest.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1964.assessment:
SAVAK censorship continued. The press was required to support the Shah's White
Revolution reforms. Ministry of Information controlled licensing of publications.
Critical coverage of religious leadership arrests was suppressed.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1965.assessment:
Press remained under SAVAK surveillance with strict prohibition on criticizing the
Shah or royal family. Newspapers survived by focusing on cultural, sports, and
approved political content. Small circulation newspapers below state thresholds
were shut down. Ministry of Culture and Arts managed censorship alongside SAVAK.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1966.assessment:
Continuation of SAVAK-era press control. Iran had multiple newspapers but all were
subject to official directives. SAVAK's Directorate Five maintained censorship of
print, film, and academic publications. Foreign press access was limited and
controlled.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1967.assessment:
SAVAK censorship intensified in the late 1960s. Periodic inspection of public and
school libraries began. Works by prominent writers considered politically suspect
were removed. Journalists covering social unrest or labor activism faced arrest.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1968.assessment:
Press censorship remained tight as student and intellectual unrest grew globally.
SAVAK monitored leftist and Islamist publications. Underground publications
circulated despite bans. The official press remained pro-government.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica. 'Censorship in Persia.' Online edition.

1969.assessment:
SAVAK censorship continued. Iran had approximately 30 daily and weekly newspapers
but all operated under SAVAK-enforced self-censorship. Criticism of the Shah,
the monarchy, or government policies was prohibited. Some cultural journals
